## PAGE 00003: Adding a concept model to Teamwork Cloud

- page_id: `170478840`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478840/Adding+a+concept+model+to+Teamwork+Cloud
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:57.271+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Adding a concept model to Teamwork Cloud and export it as an OWL ontology
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

571948278

571948280

571948279

You can add a concept model to Teamwork Cloud (or TWCloud), a new generation of server that is designed to work with large amounts of data. TWCloud provides a modeling repository standard that can be transparently scaled from a single workstation to hundreds of servers. It enables multiple servers to interconnect and share resources. See [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Teamwork Cloud Documentation OBSOLETE' space='TWC2024xR1'] for more information.

You must be logged into the server in order to add a concept model to the TWCloud server. Our software has built TWCloud to group projects into categories; hence, you will need to select a category for your concept model when adding it to the server. If you do not select any category, your model will be stored under the **Uncategorized** category by default. Nevertheless, you are allowed to move your model to another category using Teamwork Cloud Admin (TWAdmin), the user interface of TWCloud. For more information about moving a project category, see [Moving projects from one category to another](http://docs.nomagic.com/display/TWCloud184/Moving+projects+from+one+category+to+another).

To log into the TWCloud server

1. Click the main menu **Collaborate** > **Login**. The **Login** dialog opens. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Type your username and password, e.g. Administrator.
3. Enter the server address, e.g. 10.1.1.123 .
4. From the Server type list, select Teamwork Cloud .
5. Click OK . You will be connected to the server.

Once you are logged into TWCloud, you can add a concept model to the server. The following instructions use the concept model **StereotypeDisjointSample** as an example.

To add a concept model to the TWCloud server

1. Open a concept model project.
2. On the main menu, click Collaborate > Add Project to Server. The Add Project to the Server dialog opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='image2016-8-30 13:24:51.png']
3. The name of the active concept model that you will add to the server will appear in the dialog by default. In this example, the StereotypeDisjointSample concept model is used. Select a category for your concept model. The default option is Uncategorized .
4. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='image2016-8-30 13:26:31.png'] . The concept model will be added to the server.

****

To check if the concept model has been successfully added to TWCloud

- From the main menu, click Collaborate > Projects . The Manage Projects dialog opens, and the concept model has been added to TWCloud. The concept model used in this example is StereotypeDisjointSample .

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Manage Projects dialog showing the concept model StereotypeDisjointSample****that has been added to Teamwork Cloud.

A concept model that has been added to TWCloud is saved on the server as a server project. Users with the permission to work on server projects can access the concept model. Before editing the concept model, you first need to lock it to prevent others from editing it at the same time. For more information on locking models for editing, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Locking model for edit' space='MD2024xR1'].

To open a concept model in TWCloud

1. Log into the TWCloud server.
2. On the MagicDraw main menu, click Collaborate > Projects to open the Manage Projects dialog.
3. Select a concept model that you want to open and click [ATTACHMENT filename='Open_button.png'] . The concept model opens in read-only mode.
4. To edit the model, right-click Data in the Containment tree, and select Lock > Lock Elements for Edit Recursively . The concept model is locked for editing.

If you make changes to the model, you must commit them so that other users can update the model with your changes. See [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Committing changes to Teamwork Cloud' space='MD2024xR1'] for the instructions to commit changes to TWCloud.

571980076

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Usage' space='MCM2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Adding a concept model to Teamwork Cloud and export it as an OWL ontology' space='MCM2024xR1']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478839 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00004: Adding a concept model to Teamwork Cloud and export it as an OWL ontology

- page_id: `170478839`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478839/Adding+a+concept+model+to+Teamwork+Cloud+and+export+it+as+an+OWL+ontology
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:57.190+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

571589876

571589878

571589877

You can collaborate with your team members in constructing a concept model. The collaboration feature in MagicDraw allows you to add a concept model to the TWCloud server for everyone on the team to access the shared model, make changes to it, and commit those changes to the server. You can easily update the model every time someone commits those changes to the server.

Once your model or project has been added to TWCloud, you can start a collaborative session. You can save the model locally so that you can continue working on the model in offline mode. The offline mode feature allows you to commit the changes the next time you are online and connected to the server. For more information about offline projects, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Offline modeling' space='MD2024xR1'].

Exporting either a concept model on your local machine or the machine in TWCloud to an OWL ontology works the same way. The Concept Modeler allows you to export it to the default location, a previous location, or a selected destination folder. Prior to exporting the model, you can enable the prompt to export OWL to a selected destination so that you can select a desired location every time you export an OWL ontology.

571598657

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Usage' space='MCM2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Adding a concept model to Teamwork Cloud' space='MCM2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exporting a concept model from TWCloud to an OWL ontology' space='MCM2024xR1']

The offline mode feature allows you to commit the changes the next time you are online and connected to the server. For more information about offline projects, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Offline modeling" /></ac:link>.</p><p>Exporting either a concept model on your local machine or the machine in TWCloud to an OWL ontology works the same way. The Concept Modeler allows you to export it to the default location, a previous location, or a selected destination folder. Prior to exporting the model, you can enable the prompt to export OWL to a selected destination so that you can select a desired location every time you export an OWL ontology.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="04c6bae0-35a0-4919-994e-5b28da3e2a2e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">571598657</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Usage" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Adding a concept model to Teamwork Cloud" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Exporting a concept model from TWCloud to an OWL ontology" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 00005: Adding property subsetting

- page_id: `170478988`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478988/Adding+property+subsetting
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:58.682+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Working with properties
- labels: ['property-subset', 'subsetted-property']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1278262971

1278262973

1278262972

To subset a property

1. Double-click on the property ("composed of" from "Futsal Team") that subsets another property ("consists of" from "Soccer Team"). Remember that the property is owned by the class at the opposite end of the association. [ATTACHMENT filename='subsetted_property_.png']
2. The specification dialog opens for this property. [ATTACHMENT filename='property_specification_dialog.png']
3. Locate and select the property that is subsetted in the Containment tree ("consists of" from "Soccer Team"), then press Alt+B . [ATTACHMENT filename='selecting_subsetted_property.png']
4. Drag the property that is subsetted ("consists of" from "Soccer Team") from the Containment tree to the box next to "Subsetted Property" in the right pane of the Specification window for the subsetting property ("composed of" from "Futsal Team"). [ATTACHMENT filename='dragging_subsetted_property.png']
5. The property is subsetted. [ATTACHMENT filename='subsetted_property.png']

1278262970

**Related page**

- Usage

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="44a33b3e-b3dd-4a52-90df-b94675a7f61b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1465286649</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b899c854-d8e3-4d99-a5df-48b5dd90f959"><ac:parameter ac:name="width">100.00002%</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1465286661</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="46349aa0-c02b-4b83-80e7-a89a1b728284"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1465286651</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>This section is designed to inform you about the advance modeling patterns that the modeling tool supports. The subsections are shown below.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8177168a-7dd1-423d-9f72-98c0b441fc45"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1465286639</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="37986370-e544-46ba-bab3-f2c905585110"><ac:parameter ac:name="depth">2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478232 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00007: Annotation and annotation properties

- page_id: `170478232`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478232/Annotation+and+annotation+properties
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:50.294+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1149134371

1149134373

1149134372

The OWL language provides a way to comment on any subject that has a URI, using *annotations*. One can annotate classes, properties, and ontologies. In addition to providing a way to comment on a subject, the OWL language provides an open-ended way to define *annotation properties*. An annotation property defines a type of annotation with a relatively precise meaning.

Every annotation is a value for an annotation property*.*An annotation describes some subject URI using an annotation property URI and a (usually textual) value, forming what is called a *triple*. For example, a well-known vocabulary called *Dublin Core* defines an annotation property that has the URI [http://purl.org/dc/terms/description](http://purl.org/dc/terms/Description). That annotation property is what the Concept Modeler uses by default to document a class called Person. It forms the triple [http://example.com/ontology/Person](http://example.com/ontology/Person) [http://purl.org/dc/terms/description](http://purl.org/dc/terms/Description) “A human being”.

The Concept Modeler allows the user to define any number of annotation types. The user does this by declaring that a property is an *annotation property* using the **«**Annotation Property» stereotype on a UML property. Alternatively, a user can import annotation properties from existing OWL ontologies. When the Concept Modeler imports annotation properties, it automatically applies the «Annotation Property» stereotype.

Any UML comment can be exported as an OWL annotation. By default, the concept modeler converts UML element documentation, notes, and comments into the Dublin Core annotation property [http://purl.org/dc/terms/description](http://purl.org/dc/terms/Description). When the user would like to use some other annotation property, he or she can specify which one as a tagged value in a UML comment stereotyped as an **«**Annotation**»**. Applying this stereotype allows one to use any annotation property to provide more precise meaning for the information the comment contains and to properly export the comment into OWL.

For example, the following diagram illustrates a UML comment stereotyped with **«**Annotation**»**to document the concept *Item*. It uses a specific kind of annotation property, called *explanatory note,* to provide context for that documentation. That annotation property definition is shown as the third attribute stereotyped as an «Annotation Property» in the pink property holder. Its usage as a tagged value is shown as “{annotationProperty = explanatory note}” in the UML Comment stereotyped as an «Annotation». Please see the normal MagicDraw documentation for how to create a tagged value for a stereotype in the Specification window.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**A comment stereotyped with «Annotation» to provide a correct meaning of an item.**

You may have an imported model that contains annotation properties and documentation. A UML Comment can have more than one «Annotation» and each of these «Annotation»s can also have their own annotation property tagged value.

1149134370

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']

One can annotate classes, properties, and ontologies. In addition to providing a way to comment on a subject, the OWL language provides an open-ended way to define <em>annotation properties</em>. An annotation property defines a type of annotation with a relatively precise meaning.</p><p>Every annotation is a value for an annotation property<em>. </em>An annotation describes some subject URI using an annotation property URI and a (usually textual) value, forming what is called a <em>triple</em>. For example, a well-known vocabulary called <em>Dublin Core</em> defines an annotation property that has the URI <a href="http://purl.org/dc/terms/Description">http://purl.org/dc/terms/description</a>. That annotation property is what the Concept Modeler uses by default to document a class called Person. It forms the triple <a href="http://example.com/ontology/Person">http://example.com/ontology/Person</a> <a href="http://purl.org/dc/terms/Description">http://purl.org/dc/terms/description</a> “A human being”.</p><p>The Concept Modeler allows the user to define any number of annotation types. The user does this by declaring that a property is an <em>annotation property</em> using the <strong>«</strong>Annotation Property» stereotype on a UML property. Alternatively, a user can import annotation properties from existing OWL ontologies. When the Concept Modeler imports annotation properties, it automatically applies the «Annotation Property» stereotype.</p><p>Any UML comment can be exported as an OWL annotation. By default, the concept modeler converts UML element documentation, notes, and comments into the Dublin Core annotation property <a href="http://purl.org/dc/terms/Description">http://purl.org/dc/terms/description</a>.  When the user would like to use some other annotation property, he or she can specify which one as a tagged value in a UML comment stereotyped as an <strong>«</strong>Annotation<strong>»</strong>. Applying this stereotype allows one to use any annotation property to provide more precise meaning for the information the comment contains and to properly export the comment into OWL.</p><p>For example, the following diagram illustrates a UML comment stereotyped with <strong>«</strong>Annotation<strong>» </strong>to document the concept <em>Item</em>. It uses a specific kind of annotation property, called <em>explanatory note,</em> to provide context for that documentation. That annotation property definition is shown as the third attribute stereotyped as an «Annotation Property» in the pink property holder. Its usage as a tagged value is shown as “{annotationProperty = explanatory note}” in the UML Comment stereotyped as an «Annotation». Please see the normal MagicDraw documentation for how to create a tagged value for a stereotype in the Specification window.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Annotation Property.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Annotation and annotation properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="text-align: center;"><strong>A comment stereotyped with «Annotation» to provide a correct meaning of an item.</strong></p><p>You may have an imported model that contains annotation properties and documentation. A UML Comment can have more than one «Annotation» and each of these «Annotation»s can also have their own annotation property tagged value.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0ba8d491-87e1-486f-bfbd-a7f826eb4f92"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1149134370</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 00008: Annotation and annotation property

- page_id: `170478393`

- version_number: 1

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Available from 19.0 SP1

5440934

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Available from 19.0 SP1

5440936

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Available from 19.0 SP1

5440935

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A class with annotation and annotation property.

```text
Declaration(Class(:Book))
      Declaration(Class(:Item))
      Declaration(Class(:Multimedia))
      Declaration(    AnnotationProperty())
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Book "Book"@en)
      SubClassOf(:Book :Item)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Item "Item"@en) AnnotationAssertion( :Item "This represents any item physical or electronic that can be lent out by a library."@en)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Multimedia "Multimedia"@en)
      SubClassOf(:Multimedia :Item)
)]]>
```

GreenAvailable from 19.0 SP1

The following OWL and figure show an annotation containing a copyright notice being imported into MCM as the annotation of the corresponding concept model.

```text
Copyright (c) 2013-2018 Object Management Group, Inc.
	Accounting Equity Ontology           
	….
]]>
```

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The annotation of the corresponding concept model is imported into MCM.

5440933

````html
      Declaration(Class(:Item))
      Declaration(Class(:Multimedia))
      Declaration(    AnnotationProperty(<http://spec.edmcouncil.org/fibo/FND/Utilities/AnnotationVocabulary/explanatoryNote>))

      SubClassOf(:Book :Item)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Item "Item"@en) AnnotationAssertion(<http://spec.edmcouncil.org/fibo/FND/Utilities/AnnotationVocabulary/explanatoryNote> :Item "This represents any item physical or electronic that can be lent out by a library."@en)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Multimedia "Multimedia"@en)
      SubClassOf(:Multimedia :Item)
)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="status" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cafd3cb8-c72f-4e0d-b134-0c00c0ad07a0"><ac:parameter ac:name="colour">Green</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Available from 19.0 SP1</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><p>The following OWL and figure show an annotation containing a copyright notice being imported into MCM as the annotation of the corresponding concept model.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="93c2db2c-645f-47e3-9d5f-9ab65c8f7ac5"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<owl:Ontology 
	rdf:about="https://spec.edmcouncil.org/fibo/ontology/FND/Accounting/AccountingEquity/">
	<sm:copyright>Copyright (c) 2013-2018 Object Management Group, Inc.</sm:copyright>
	<rdfs:label>Accounting Equity Ontology</rdfs:label>           
	….
</owl:Ontology>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Annotation of Concept Model Imported into CCM.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Annotation and annotation property" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The annotation of the corresponding concept model is imported into MCM.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4cb767a7-2bbc-434f-bd0e-084575f3739a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">5440933</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478373 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00009: Anonymous union class

- page_id: `170478373`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478373/Anonymous+union+class
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:56.627+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1469046763

1469046765

1469046764

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Anonymous union class.

```text
Declaration(
             Class(:Cat)
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:Dog)
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:Person)
      )
      Declaration(
             ObjectProperty(:caredForBy)
      )
      Declaration(
             ObjectProperty(:caresFor)
      )
	  AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Cat "Cat"@en)
	  DisjointClasses(:Cat :Dog)
	  AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Dog "Dog"@en)
	  AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Person "Person"@en)
	  AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :caredForBy "cared for by"@en)
	  InverseObjectProperties(:caredForBy :caresFor)
	  ObjectPropertyDomain(:caredForBy ObjectUnionOf(:Dog :Cat))
	  ObjectPropertyRange(:caredForBy :Person)
	  AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :caresFor "cares for"@en)
	  ObjectPropertyDomain(:caresFor :Person)
	  ObjectPropertyRange(:caresFor ObjectUnionOf(:Dog :Cat))
)]]>
```

1469046762

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax' space='MCM2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="005460f7-7766-48cf-9007-442b3b621641"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1469046763</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="92c920f0-c3f4-4242-a084-a8a413da1d3c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1469046765</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ad69104b-1962-4312-be1a-b122aeada374"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1469046764</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-23 14:22:7.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Anonymous union class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Anonymous union class.</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2dbcee64-f534-486a-a259-20355848c5ab"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Ontology(<http://nomagic.com/ontology/example-case/case-26>
      Declaration(
             Class(:Cat)
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:Dog)
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:Person)
      )
      Declaration(
             ObjectProperty(:caredForBy)
      )
      Declaration(
             ObjectProperty(:caresFor)
      )
	  AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Cat "Cat"@en)
	  DisjointClasses(:Cat :Dog)
	  AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Dog "Dog"@en)
	  AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Person "Person"@en)
	  AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :caredForBy "cared for by"@en)
	  InverseObjectProperties(:caredForBy :caresFor)
	  ObjectPropertyDomain(:caredForBy ObjectUnionOf(:Dog :Cat))
	  ObjectPropertyRange(:caredForBy :Person)
	  AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :caresFor "cares for"@en)
	  ObjectPropertyDomain(:caresFor :Person)
	  ObjectPropertyRange(:caresFor ObjectUnionOf(:Dog :Cat))
)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="937b4ad9-273b-4144-8116-dd5a7dcc281b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1469046762</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478158 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00010: Anonymous union classes

- page_id: `170478158`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478158/Anonymous+union+classes
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:48.319+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Available From 19.0 SP2

84975880

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Available From 19.0 SP2

84975882

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Available From 19.0 SP2

84975881

An anonymous union is an unnamed class used to represent a set of classes that can be used as a type of a property. An anonymous union class always implies a complete subclass generalization. (See [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Complete subclasses' space='MCM2024xR1'].)

The following diagram states that an instance of a Person may have a value of type Cat or Dog for the *cares for*property. The diagram also states that an instance of a Cat or a Dog may have a value of type Person for the *cared for by* property.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An anonymous union class.

In an ontology, if anonymous union, with same classes within the union, is used in multiple places, the Concept Modeler can distinguish it when importing the ontology. In other words, if the anonymous union has the same union members, the Concept Modeler will identify it as the same anonymous union.

GreenAvailable From 19.0 SP2

An anonymous Class with Subclasses but without an explicit «Union» is now treated the same as a union of its Subclasses. The following image show what that means.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The boxed diagram represents the usual relationship between an anonymous Union class. Notice the stereotype, *«Union»*. On the other hand, the diagram not boxed represents a similar structure but there's no anonymous Union class with the stereotype, *«Union»*. The modeling tool is programmed to consider the non-stereotyped class with the subclasses as a Union. Let's see that in the exported OWL of this model.

Reading the following code piece, notice the ObjectUnionOf between A and B, and the ObjectIUnionOf between C and D.

```text

```

The full ontology is displayed below:

```text
Declaration(Class(:A))
Declaration(Class(:B))
Declaration(Class(:C))
Declaration(Class(:D))
Declaration(Class(:P))
Declaration(Class(:Q))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasP))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasQ))

############################
#   Object Properties
############################

# Object Property: :hasP (has p)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasP "has p"^^xsd:string)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasP ObjectUnionOf(:A :B))
ObjectPropertyRange(:hasP :P)

# Object Property: :hasQ (has q)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasQ "has q"^^xsd:string)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasQ ObjectUnionOf(:C :D))
ObjectPropertyRange(:hasQ :Q)



############################
#   Classes
############################

# Class: :A (a)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :A "a"^^xsd:string)

# Class: :B (b)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :B "b"^^xsd:string)

# Class: :C (c)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :C "c"^^xsd:string)

# Class: :D (d)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :D "d"^^xsd:string)

# Class: :P (p)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :P "p"^^xsd:string)

# Class: :Q (q)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Q "q"^^xsd:string)


)]]>
```

84975878

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

(See <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Complete subclasses" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Complete Subclasses]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.)</p><p>The following diagram states that an instance of a Person may have a value of type Cat or Dog for the <em>cares for </em>property. In other words, if the anonymous union has the same union members, the Concept Modeler will identify it as the same anonymous union.</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="status" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b018c0fe-00e6-4d6c-a82b-570d76b46208"><ac:parameter ac:name="colour">Green</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Available From 19.0 SP2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><p>An anonymous Class with Subclasses but without an explicit «Union» is now treated the same as a union of its Subclasses. The following image show what that means.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="u2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Anonymous union classes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>The boxed diagram represents the usual relationship between an anonymous Union class. Notice the stereotype, <em>«Union»</em>. On the other hand, the diagram not boxed represents a similar structure but there's no anonymous Union class with the stereotype, <em>«Union»</em>. The modeling tool is programmed to consider the non-stereotyped class with the subclasses as a Union. Let's see that in the exported OWL of this model.</p><p>Reading the following code piece, notice the ObjectUnionOf between A and B, and the ObjectIUnionOf between C and D.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="df5d7f2f-a6ab-442e-a5f3-e60e80a32d7f"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[# Object Property: :hasP (has p)
````html
ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasP ObjectUnionOf(:A :B))
ObjectPropertyRange(:hasP :P)
# Object Property: :hasQ (has q)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasQ ObjectUnionOf(:C :D))
ObjectPropertyRange(:hasQ :Q)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The full ontology is displayed below:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a33e7606-9465-4719-a8c3-0ef7e555d054"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Ontology(<http://example.com/ontology/uniondemo>

Declaration(Class(:A))
Declaration(Class(:B))
Declaration(Class(:C))
Declaration(Class(:D))
Declaration(Class(:P))
Declaration(Class(:Q))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasP))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasQ))

############################
#   Object Properties
############################

# Object Property: :hasP (has p)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasP "has p"^^xsd:string)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasP ObjectUnionOf(:A :B))
ObjectPropertyRange(:hasP :P)

# Object Property: :hasQ (has q)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasQ "has q"^^xsd:string)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasQ ObjectUnionOf(:C :D))
ObjectPropertyRange(:hasQ :Q)



############################
#   Classes
############################

# Class: :A (a)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :A "a"^^xsd:string)

# Class: :B (b)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :B "b"^^xsd:string)

# Class: :C (c)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :C "c"^^xsd:string)

# Class: :D (d)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :D "d"^^xsd:string)

# Class: :P (p)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :P "p"^^xsd:string)

# Class: :Q (q)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Q "q"^^xsd:string)


)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cc44eb80-7a65-4145-aa01-18908b2cda1d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">84975878</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170479123 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00011: Applying an annotation stereotype to a comment

- page_id: `170479123`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170479123/Applying+an+annotation+stereotype+to+a+comment
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-31T14:21:34.680+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Working with annotations > Working with annotation properties
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To apply an annotation stereotype to a comment

1. Create a UML Comment containing whatever text you like. AnchoringYou may wish to anchor your comment to any element in your model, but that is completely up to you. The rest of instructions apply regardless of anchoring.
2. Double-click the Comment to open its specification window.
3. In the specification window, click the three dots at the end of the Applied Stereotype field. A shortcut menu appears.
4. Start typing Annotation to search for the annotation stereotypes. You will see three stereotypes appear: Annotation, Literal Annotation, and IRI Annotation.
5. Select whichever stereotype you wish.
6. Click Apply .
7. Click Close.

#### NOTE: AnnotationProperty

AnnotationProperty

Notice that you cannot apply an AnnotationProperty stereotype to the comment. If you would like to read about AnnotationProperty stereotype, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying the annotation property stereotype to a property' space='CCMP'].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To apply an annotation stereotype to a comment</p><hr /><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Create a UML Comment containing whatever text you like.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b76a6918-11e5-4941-ae50-dde59e3c6d71"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Anchoring</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You may wish to anchor your comment to any element in your model, but that is completely up to you. The rest of instructions apply regardless of anchoring.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li>Double-click the <strong>Comment</strong> to open its specification window.</li><li>In the specification window, click the three dots at the end of the <strong>Applied Stereotype</strong> field. A shortcut menu appears. </li><li>Start typing <em>Annotation</em> to search for the annotation stereotypes. You will see <strong>three</strong> stereotypes appear: <em>Annotation, Literal Annotation,</em> and <em>IRI Annotation.</em></li><li>Select whichever stereotype you wish. </li><li>Click <strong>Apply</strong>.</li><li>Click <strong>Close.</strong> </li></ol><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7b444367-0792-4351-aee6-ff7c4d56bfaf"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">AnnotationProperty</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Notice that you cannot apply an AnnotationProperty stereotype to the comment. If you would like to read about AnnotationProperty stereotype, refer to <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CCMP" ri:content-title="Specifying the annotation property stereotype to a property" /></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170479124 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00012: Associating an annotation property with an annotation

- page_id: `170479124`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170479124/Associating+an+annotation+property+with+an+annotation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:59.713+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Working with annotations > Working with annotation properties
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1048807676

1048807678

1048807677

The UML comment specification dialog allows you to select a particular kind of annotation property for each annotation.

To select a type of annotation property for an annotation

1. Double-click an Annotation (Literal/IRI/Comment) on the diagram pane. The Specification window of the selected Annotation will open.
2. Select Tags on the left-hand side list and select annotationProperty . [ATTACHMENT filename='image2016-8-30 15:14:43.png'] **Selecting an annotation property tagged value.**
3. Click Create Value . The Select Property dialog will open (see the following figure). [ATTACHMENT filename='image2016-8-30 15:15:14.png'] **Selecting explanatory note as the Annotation Property tagged value.**
4. Select an Annotation Property and click OK . The selected Annotation Property will be created for the Annotation (see the following figure). [ATTACHMENT filename='image2016-8-30 15:15:38.png'] **Showing the Annotation Property tagged value of the Annotation in the Specification window.**

In this example, the annotation property ***explanatory note*** is a UML property stereotyped with **«**Annotation Property**»**.

1277839353

**Related pages**

- Usage

The <strong>Select Property</strong> dialog will open (see the following figure).<br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-30 15:15:14.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Associating an annotation property with an annotation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><p style="text-align: center;"><strong>Selecting explanatory note as the Annotation Property tagged value.</strong></p></li><li>Select an Annotation Property and click <strong>OK</strong>. The selected Annotation Property will be created for the Annotation (see the following figure).<br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-30 15:15:38.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Associating an annotation property with an annotation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><p style="text-align: center;"><strong>Showing the Annotation Property tagged value of the Annotation in the Specification window.</strong></p></li></ol><p>In this example, the annotation property <strong><em>explanatory note</em></strong> is a UML property stereotyped with <strong>«</strong>Annotation Property<strong>»</strong>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="201f1ed2-5aa5-4e53-84a3-1ff90a55f3fc"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1277839353</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li class="ancestor-link"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Usage">Usage</a></li></ul><p class="ancestor-link parent-link"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478162 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00013: Association class

- page_id: `170478162`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478162/Association+class
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:53.837+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics > Advanced Modeling Patterns
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

324872294

324872305

324872295

With the modeling tool's new version, an Association Class can now be modeled in a Concept Modeling diagram. It is interpreted as a reified Class with additional noun-phrased properties that are named after the types at the ends of the association. An Association Class is a model element with both[Association](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Association)and[Class](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Class)properties. You can look at an Association Class as an Association that also has Class properties, or as a Class that also has Association properties. Additionally, it connects a set of classifiers, and defines a set of features belonging only to the relationship and not to any of the classifiers.

In a concept model any “fact” may have properties. Of particular importance is the “provenance” of the fact – where the fact came from and thus how much it can be trusted. As implied by its name, an association class has both the properties of an association and the properties of a class. While the association line and box may seem somewhat visually distinct – they represent the same concept.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The above example shows the “Enrollment” UML Association Class, which as an equivalent, shorthand meaning to the next diagram. Note that the expansion of the «Role» concepts will be explained [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Role' space='MCM2024xR1']. Similarly, the Facet Of stereotype will be explained here.

If you would like to learn about creating an example similar to one presented above, please read [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with association classes' space='MCM2024xR1']. Furthermore, if you would like to see how the modeling tool supports Associations Classes, please view [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Association classes' space='MCM2024xR1'].

324872293

**Related Pages:**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Phase' space='MCM2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Role' space='MCM2024xR1']

It is interpreted as a reified Class with additional noun-phrased properties that are named after the types at the ends of the association. <span>An Association Class is a model element with both </span><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Association" class="ancestor-link parent-link">Association </a><span>and </span><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Class">Class</a><span> properties. You can look at an Association Class as an Association that also has Class properties, or as a Class that also has Association properties. Additionally, it connects a set of classifiers, and defines a set of features belonging only to the relationship and not to any of the classifiers. </span></span></p><p>In a concept model any “fact” may have properties. Of particular importance is the “provenance” of the fact – where the fact came from and thus how much it can be trusted. As implied by its name, an association class has both the properties of an association and the properties of a class. While the association line and box may seem somewhat visually distinct – they represent the same concept.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="CCM__AssociationClass__Example.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Association class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>The above example shows the “Enrollment” UML Association Class, which as an equivalent, shorthand meaning to the next diagram. Note that the expansion of the «Role» concepts will be explained <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Role" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. Similarly, the Facet Of stereotype will be explained here.</p><p>If you would like to learn about creating an example similar to one presented above, please read <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Working with association classes" /></ac:link>. Furthermore, if you would like to see how the modeling tool supports Associations Classes, please view <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Association classes" /></ac:link>.</p><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="50586848-89ed-46a9-8f06-3a4395d74989"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">324872293</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages:</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Phase" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Role" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478375 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00014: Association classes

- page_id: `170478375`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478375/Association+classes
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:56.778+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

```text
Import()
Import()

Declaration(Class(:Enrollment))
Declaration(Class(:Organization))
Declaration(Class(:Person))
Declaration(Class(:Student))
Declaration(Class(:University))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:enrolledBy))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:enrollment_enrolledBy))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:enrollment_enrolls))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:enrolls))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:isEnrollment))

############################
#   Object Properties
############################

# Object Property: :enrolledBy (enrolled by)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :enrolledBy "enrolled by"^^xsd:string)
InverseObjectProperties(:enrolls :enrolledBy)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:enrolledBy :Student)
ObjectPropertyRange(:enrolledBy :University)

# Object Property: :enrollment_enrolledBy (:enrollment_enrolledBy)

SubObjectPropertyOf(:enrollment_enrolledBy )
ObjectPropertyDomain(:enrollment_enrolledBy :Enrollment)
ObjectPropertyRange(:enrollment_enrolledBy :University)

# Object Property: :enrollment_enrolls (:enrollment_enrolls)

SubObjectPropertyOf(:enrollment_enrolls )
ObjectPropertyDomain(:enrollment_enrolls :Enrollment)
ObjectPropertyRange(:enrollment_enrolls :Student)

# Object Property: :enrolls (:enrolls)

ObjectPropertyDomain(:enrolls :University)
ObjectPropertyRange(:enrolls :Student)

# Object Property: :isEnrollment (is Enrollment)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :isEnrollment "is Enrollment"^^xsd:string)
SubObjectPropertyOf(:isEnrollment )
ObjectPropertyDomain(:isEnrollment :Enrollment)
ObjectPropertyRange(:isEnrollment :Enrollment)



############################
#   Classes
############################

# Class: :Enrollment (:Enrollment)

SubClassOf(:Enrollment )
SubClassOf(:Enrollment ObjectExactCardinality(1 :enrollment_enrolledBy))
SubClassOf(:Enrollment ObjectExactCardinality(1 :enrollment_enrolls))
SubClassOf(:Enrollment ObjectExactCardinality(1 :isEnrollment))
SubClassOf(:Enrollment ObjectHasSelf(:isEnrollment))

# Class: :Student (:Student)

SubClassOf(:Student :Person)
SubClassOf(:Student ObjectMinCardinality(1 ObjectInverseOf(:enrollment_enrolls)))

# Class: :University (:University)

SubClassOf(:University :Organization)
SubClassOf(:University ObjectExactCardinality(3 ObjectInverseOf(:enrollment_enrolledBy)))


SubObjectPropertyOf(ObjectPropertyChain(ObjectInverseOf(:enrollment_enrolledBy) :isEnrollment :enrollment_enrolls) :enrolls)
SubObjectPropertyOf(ObjectPropertyChain(ObjectInverseOf(:enrollment_enrolls) :isEnrollment :enrollment_enrolledBy) :enrolledBy)
)

## Generated using the No Magic Magic Concept Modeler (19.0 SP2 Local, build 201902272256) on 2019-02-28 at 03:56:37 GMT


]]>
```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="CCM__AssociationClass__Example.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Association classes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="07df80e1-9b9f-4aac-a68d-d6a878f1c979"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Ontology(<http://example.com/ontology/AssociationClass>
Import(<http://nomagic.com/ConceptModeler/ConceptModelingSupportLibrary>)
Import(<http://nomagic.com/ConceptModeler/ConceptModelingSupportLibrary/>)

Declaration(Class(:Enrollment))
Declaration(Class(:Organization))
Declaration(Class(:Person))
Declaration(Class(:Student))
Declaration(Class(:University))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:enrolledBy))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:enrollment_enrolledBy))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:enrollment_enrolls))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:enrolls))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:isEnrollment))

############################
#   Object Properties
############################

# Object Property: :enrolledBy (enrolled by)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :enrolledBy "enrolled by"^^xsd:string)
InverseObjectProperties(:enrolls :enrolledBy)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:enrolledBy :Student)
ObjectPropertyRange(:enrolledBy :University)

# Object Property: :enrollment_enrolledBy (:enrollment_enrolledBy)

SubObjectPropertyOf(:enrollment_enrolledBy <http://nomagic.com/ConceptModeler/ConceptModelingSupportLibrary/mediates>)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:enrollment_enrolledBy :Enrollment)
ObjectPropertyRange(:enrollment_enrolledBy :University)

# Object Property: :enrollment_enrolls (:enrollment_enrolls)

SubObjectPropertyOf(:enrollment_enrolls <http://nomagic.com/ConceptModeler/ConceptModelingSupportLibrary/mediates>)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:enrollment_enrolls :Enrollment)
ObjectPropertyRange(:enrollment_enrolls :Student)

# Object Property: :enrolls (:enrolls)

ObjectPropertyDomain(:enrolls :University)
ObjectPropertyRange(:enrolls :Student)

# Object Property: :isEnrollment (is Enrollment)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :isEnrollment "is Enrollment"^^xsd:string)
SubObjectPropertyOf(:isEnrollment <http://nomagic.com/ConceptModeler/ConceptModelingSupportLibrary/isMediatingThing>)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:isEnrollment :Enrollment)
ObjectPropertyRange(:isEnrollment :Enrollment)



############################
#   Classes
############################

# Class: :Enrollment (:Enrollment)

SubClassOf(:Enrollment <http://nomagic.com/ConceptModeler/ConceptModelingSupportLibrary/MediatingThing>)
SubClassOf(:Enrollment ObjectExactCardinality(1 :enrollment_enrolledBy))
SubClassOf(:Enrollment ObjectExactCardinality(1 :enrollment_enrolls))
SubClassOf(:Enrollment ObjectExactCardinality(1 :isEnrollment))
SubClassOf(:Enrollment ObjectHasSelf(:isEnrollment))

# Class: :Student (:Student)

SubClassOf(:Student :Person)
SubClassOf(:Student ObjectMinCardinality(1 ObjectInverseOf(:enrollment_enrolls)))

# Class: :University (:University)

SubClassOf(:University :Organization)
SubClassOf(:University ObjectExactCardinality(3 ObjectInverseOf(:enrollment_enrolledBy)))


SubObjectPropertyOf(ObjectPropertyChain(ObjectInverseOf(:enrollment_enrolledBy) :isEnrollment :enrollment_enrolls) :enrolls)
SubObjectPropertyOf(ObjectPropertyChain(ObjectInverseOf(:enrollment_enrolls) :isEnrollment :enrollment_enrolledBy) :enrolledBy)
)
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478396 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00015: Asymmetrical inverse property

- page_id: `170478396`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478396/Asymmetrical+inverse+property
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:58.294+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

5465564

5465567

5465565

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Asymmetrical inverse property.

```text
Declaration(Class(:Mother))
            Declaration(Class(:Son))
            Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasMother))
            Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasSon))
            AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Mother "Mother"@en)
            SubClassOf(:Mother ObjectIntersectionOf(ObjectMinCardinality(1 :hasSon :Son)))
            AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Son "Son"@en)
            SubClassOf(owl:Thing ObjectIntersectionOf(ObjectMaxCardinality(1 :hasMother :Mother) ObjectMinCardinality(1 :hasMother :Mother)))
            AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasMother "has mother"@en)
            ObjectPropertyRange(:hasMother :Mother)
            AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasSon "has son"@en)
            ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasSon :Mother)
            ObjectPropertyRange(:hasSon :Son)
) ]]>
```

5465563

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax' space='MCM2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2e433b6a-48c1-407e-a578-e4e51ee3c4d3"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">5465564</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1300ae85-8074-49e8-bcf5-d4a057d60dad"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">5465567</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0d6e9055-355d-42be-b7e9-5a02b5c428f6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">5465565</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-24 14:45:37.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Asymmetrical inverse property" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Asymmetrical inverse property.</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b3b87bd1-ebce-423c-836c-8588205b9e8e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Ontology(<http://example.com/ontology/AsymmetricalInverseProperty>
            Declaration(Class(:Mother))
            Declaration(Class(:Son))
            Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasMother))
            Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasSon))
            AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Mother "Mother"@en)
            SubClassOf(:Mother ObjectIntersectionOf(ObjectMinCardinality(1 :hasSon :Son)))
            AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Son "Son"@en)
            SubClassOf(owl:Thing ObjectIntersectionOf(ObjectMaxCardinality(1 :hasMother :Mother) ObjectMinCardinality(1 :hasMother :Mother)))
            AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasMother "has mother"@en)
            ObjectPropertyRange(:hasMother :Mother)
            AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasSon "has son"@en)
            ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasSon :Mother)
            ObjectPropertyRange(:hasSon :Son)
) ]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="376e19b5-bc20-48c5-838b-cf2131f2095c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">5465563</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170479070 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00016: Automatically Generating Glossaries

- page_id: `170479070`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170479070/Automatically+Generating+Glossaries
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:40:23.294+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Working with glossaries
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1300883037

1300883039

1300883038

The Concept Modeler can automatically generate glossaries for classes, association ends, attributes, enumerations, and enumeration literals in a concept model upon importing an OWL ontology. Additionally, it can generate glossary entries when those concept model elements are created or edited in the diagram or the containment tree.

To set the options for automatic generation of glossaries

1. Select Project from the Options menu. [ATTACHMENT filename='OptionsProject_menu.png'] The Concept Modeler's project options.
2. Select General project options.
3. Click on the corresponding checkbox for the option to enable or disable the following options (by default, these options are disabled):
  - Add classes to the glossary.
  - Add association ends to the glossary.
  - Add attributes to the glossary.
  - Add enumerations to the glossary.
  - Add enumeration literals to the glossary.
4. Click OK . [ATTACHMENT filename='image2016-8-30 13:38:26.png'] The Concept Modeler's glossary options.

1300883035

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Usage' space='MCM2024xR1']

Additionally, it can generate glossary entries when those concept model elements are created or edited in the diagram or the containment tree.</p><p>To set the options for automatic generation of glossaries</p><hr /><ol><li>Select <strong>Project</strong> from the <strong>Options</strong> menu.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="OptionsProject_menu.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Automatically Generating Glossaries" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Concept Modeler's project options.</h6></li><li>Select <strong>General project options.</strong></li><li>Click on the corresponding checkbox for the option to enable or disable the following options<strong style="line-height: 1.42857;"> </strong>(by default, these options are disabled):<br /><ul><li>Add classes to the glossary.</li><li>Add association ends to the glossary.</li><li>Add attributes to the glossary.</li><li>Add enumerations to the glossary.</li><li>Add enumeration literals to the glossary.</li></ul></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-30 13:38:26.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Automatically Generating Glossaries" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Concept Modeler's glossary options.</h6></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0d5076f0-5475-4b68-a5b9-5036c6eb71f5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1300883035</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Usage" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478584 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00017: AutoStyler Documentation

- page_id: `170478584`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478584/AutoStyler+Documentation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:40:06.766+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

AutoStyler is a plugin used in MagicDraw for non-technical users seeking to understand big concepts from large diagrams rather than having to focus on minute details. This is where AutoStyler comes in, AutoStyler helps simplify large diagrams in a couple of different ways.

- It allows a class to become the defining diagram for all classes in separate diagrams.
- It highlights classes that have been hyperlinked.
- AutoStyler allows automatic defining diagram assignment to classes.

AutoStyler has four functions: Set Defining Diagram, Repair Styles, Enable Auto Diagram Assignment, and Disable Auto Diagram Assignment. Classes are hyperlinked together using two of the functions of AutoStyler: Set Defining Diagram, and Enable Auto Diagram Assignment. They both do the same thing, but how it is done is different. Repair Styles is used for more visibility purposes, and it is intended to highlight which classes are hyperlinked. Disable Auto Diagram Assignment negates the function of Enable Auto Diagram Assignment. Furthermore, AutoStyler is a customizable plugin meaning you are allowed to change any of the default styles of AutoStyler.

The following links demonstrate how to use the functions of AutoStyler and how to customize AutoStyler:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Set Defining Diagram' space='MCM2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Repair Styles' space='MCM2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Enable Auto Diagram Assignment' space='MCM2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Disable Auto Diagram Assignment' space='MCM2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customizing AutoStyler' space='MCM2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>   AutoStyler is a plugin used in MagicDraw for non-technical users seeking to understand big concepts from large diagrams rather than having to focus on minute details. This is where AutoStyler comes in, AutoStyler helps simplify large diagrams in a couple of different ways.</p><ul><li>It allows a class to become the defining diagram for all classes in separate diagrams.</li><li>It highlights classes that have been hyperlinked.</li><li>AutoStyler allows automatic defining diagram assignment to classes.</li></ul><p>   AutoStyler has four functions: Set Defining Diagram, Repair Styles, Enable Auto Diagram Assignment, and Disable Auto Diagram Assignment. Classes are hyperlinked together using two of the functions of AutoStyler: Set Defining Diagram, and Enable Auto Diagram Assignment. They both do the same thing, but how it is done is different. Repair Styles is used for more visibility purposes, and it is intended to highlight which classes are hyperlinked. Disable Auto Diagram Assignment negates the function of Enable Auto Diagram Assignment. Furthermore, AutoStyler is a customizable plugin meaning you are allowed to change any of the default styles of AutoStyler.</p><p>The following links demonstrate how to use the functions of AutoStyler and how to customize AutoStyler:</p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Set Defining Diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Repair Styles" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Enable Auto Diagram Assignment" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Disable Auto Diagram Assignment" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Customizing AutoStyler" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478215 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00018: Cardinality restrictions

- page_id: `170478215`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478215/Cardinality+restrictions
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:49.870+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: OWL Functional

1610760554

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: OWL Functional

1610760565

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: OWL Functional

1610760555

There are two kinds of cardinality restrictions: **qualified** and **unqualified.** This page explains both kinds, but we must consider the following setup model.

##### Setup Model

To set up the case for qualified and/or unqualified cardinality restrictions

1. Create four classes and two unidirectional object properties such that it looks as shown below. [ATTACHMENT filename='petpetownercatcatowner.PNG']
2. Drag and drop the top has pet property onto the bottom has pet property until you see the Create subproperty text. [ATTACHMENT filename='dragdrop.png']
3. Once you drop the property, the following menu will appear. Select the highlighted option. [ATTACHMENT filename='fourthoption.png']
4. The result should look as shown below. Notice the restriction {subset has pet} . [ATTACHMENT filename='subsets.PNG']
5. Now, we export to OWL.

#### WARNING: OWL Functional

OWL Functional

In order to follow the rest of the explanation, set your OWL export syntax to **OWL Functional** as shown below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Excerpt of the OWL ontology

The following code block shows an excerpt of the OWL ontology that marks the distinction between qualified and unqualified cardinality restrictions.

```text

```

##### Unqualified Cardinality Restriction

From the OWL ontology excerpt above, line 9 shows an unqualified cardinality restriction. It is an unqualified cardinality restriction because the property's type emanating from *Person*, *Pet* with cardinality of *2..** does not specify the *Pet,* such as *Cat,* that the *Person* could have.

##### Qualified Cardinality Restriction

From the OWL ontology excerpt above, line 4 shows a qualified cardinality restriction. It is a qualified cardinality restriction because the property's type emanating from *Cat Owner, Cat* with cardinality *2..3* specifies the pet, *Cat,* and the restriction actually says

**:Cat**. In other words, the restriction in line 4 is more specific than the restriction in line 9; that's why the cardinality restriction in line 4 is qualified.

1610760553

**Related Pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b821eb38-b3bc-450c-9f5d-0450603aa85b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1610760554</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="14985b2f-fd4a-4302-825b-6e686b98a49b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1610760565</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="72a77a51-bc83-49d4-8e5a-0c3ef03b8fd3"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1610760555</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>There are two kinds of cardinality restrictions: <strong>qualified</strong> and <strong>unqualified.</strong> This page explains both kinds, but we must consider the following setup model.</p><h3>Setup Model</h3><p>To set up the case for qualified and/or unqualified cardinality restrictions</p><hr /><ol><li>Create four classes and two unidirectional object properties such that it looks as shown below.<br /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="116"><ri:attachment ri:filename="petpetownercatcatowner.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Cardinality restrictions" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Drag and drop the top <em>has pet</em> property onto the bottom <em>has pet</em> property until you see the <strong>Create subproperty</strong> text.<br /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="450"><ri:attachment ri:filename="dragdrop.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Cardinality restrictions" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Once you drop the property, the following menu will appear. Select the highlighted option.<br /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="400"><ri:attachment ri:filename="fourthoption.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Cardinality restrictions" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>The result should look as shown below. Notice the restriction <em>{subset has pet}</em>.<br /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="400"><ri:attachment ri:filename="subsets.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Cardinality restrictions" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Now, we export to OWL.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ac6282cc-96bb-4cfe-9fd9-5f21dc040c66"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">OWL Functional</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>In order to follow the rest of the explanation, set your OWL export syntax to <strong>OWL Functional</strong> as shown below.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="20"><ri:attachment ri:filename="OWLfunctional.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Cardinality restrictions" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Excerpt of the OWL ontology</h3><p>The following code block shows an excerpt of the OWL ontology that marks the distinction between qualified and unqualified cardinality restrictions.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6e3e48ff-09e2-4c19-b2fc-3cfad7f73af8"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[# Class: :CatOwner (Cat Owner)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :CatOwner "Cat Owner"^^xsd:string)
SubClassOf(:CatOwner ObjectMinCardinality(2 :hasPet :Cat))
SubClassOf(:CatOwner ObjectMaxCardinality(3 :hasPet :Cat))

# Class: :Person (:Person)

SubClassOf(:Person ObjectMinCardinality(2 :hasPet))]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Unqualified Cardinality Restriction</h3><p>From the OWL ontology excerpt above, line 9 shows an unqualified cardinality restriction. It is an unqualified cardinality restriction because the property's type emanating from <em>Person</em>, <em>Pet</em> with cardinality of <em>2..*</em> does not specify the <em>Pet,</em> such as <em>Cat,</em> that the <em>Person</em> could have. </p><h3>Qualified Cardinality Restriction</h3><p>From the OWL ontology excerpt above, line 4 shows a qualified cardinality restriction. It is a qualified cardinality restriction because the property's type emanating from <em>Cat Owner, Cat</em> with cardinality <em>2..3</em> specifies the pet, <em>Cat,</em> and the restriction actually says </p><p><strong>:Cat</strong>. In other words, the restriction in line 4 is more specific than the restriction in line 9; that's why the cardinality restriction in line 4 is qualified. </p><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ab093bfc-b42a-41e4-aba0-1bd0ff2818ea"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1610760553</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478212 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00019: Cascading restrictions

- page_id: `170478212`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478212/Cascading+restrictions
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:49.774+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics > Property restrictions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Sufficient Restrictions

1057539196

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Sufficient Restrictions

1057539199

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Sufficient Restrictions

1057539197

**Anonymous Restrictions**

Think of anonymous restrictions as an anonymous class with a restriction coming out of it, as shown in the image below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Showing the restriction coming out of the Anonymous class and going into the class *College.***

**Cascading Restrictions**

A cascading restriction, as shown in the image below, is a chain of property restrictions emanating from one anonymous class and terminating at one or more classes. In other words, cascading restrictions depend on anonymous restrictions. Each property restriction in the cascade is an unnamed sub-setting or redefining property. All classes between a source and a named target class is an anonymous class expression.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**A cascading restriction showing anonymous property restrictions and unnamed class expression between the source and named target class.**

**Importing and Exporting Cascading Restrictions**

The modeling tool supports importing and exporting cascading restrictions. The list below shows some cases that are supported for import and export.

- Simple cascading restrictions with one restriction class
- Simple cascading restrictions with one restriction class and cardinalities
- Restriction class with both the universal and existential restrictions for above listed cases
- Restriction class with sufficient restriction for above listed cases
- Cascading restrictions with more than one restriction class
- Cascading restrictions with more than one restriction class that includes restriction(s) that has more than one restriction of its own
- Cascading restrictions with more than one restriction class that includes restriction(s) that has more than one restriction of its own of both the universal and existential types
- Cascading restrictions on anonymous union classes used in all of the test cases above
- Cascading restrictions with anonymous union class as type of a property

#### NOTE: Sufficient Restrictions

Sufficient Restrictions

Sufficient restrictions is not supported in cascading restrictions. This is because the 'Sufficient' restriction is not meaningful in the context of anonymous restriction.

**Merging Cascading Restrictions**

If an ontology being imported has updates to the cascading restrictions, the modeling tool will merge identical restrictions.

1057539194

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Property restrictions' space='MCM2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']

In other words, cascading restrictions depend on anonymous restrictions. Each property restriction in the cascade is an unnamed sub-setting or redefining property.  All classes between a source and a named target class is an anonymous class expression.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:height="143" ac:width="586"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Cascading Class.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Cascading restrictions" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="text-align: center;"><strong>A cascading restriction showing anonymous property restrictions and unnamed class expression between the source and named target class.</strong></p><p><strong>Importing and Exporting Cascading Restrictions</strong></p><p>The modeling tool supports importing and exporting cascading restrictions. The list below shows some cases that are supported for import and export.</p><ul><li>Simple cascading restrictions with one restriction class</li><li>Simple cascading restrictions with one restriction class and cardinalities</li><li>Restriction class with both the universal and existential restrictions for above listed cases</li><li>Restriction class with sufficient restriction for above listed cases</li><li>Cascading restrictions with more than one restriction class</li><li>Cascading restrictions with more than one restriction class that includes restriction(s) that has more than one restriction of its own</li><li>Cascading restrictions with more than one restriction class that includes restriction(s) that has more than one restriction of its own of both the universal and existential types</li><li>Cascading restrictions on anonymous union classes used in all of the test cases above</li><li>Cascading restrictions with anonymous union class as type of a property</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="987b148f-5987-4997-a431-755ecfe678a2"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Sufficient Restrictions</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Sufficient restrictions is not supported in cascading restrictions. ### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1286069695

1286069698

1286069697

For a simple UML class, the diagram below shows that the ontology is transformed as the package containing the UML class. For the sake of brevity, subsequent diagrams do not show the package in the diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A class diagram in Concept Modeler.

```text
Declaration(
 				Class(:Person)
 		)
 		AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Person "Person"@en)
 )]]>
```

1286069694

## PAGE 00027: Class equivalent to Restriction

- page_id: `170478505`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478505/Class+equivalent+to+Restriction
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-31T14:21:11.833+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax > Necessary and sufficient conditions of anonymous subclasses
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1567797543

1567797564

1567797554

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

```text
Import()

Declaration(Class(:A))
Declaration(Class(:B))
Declaration(Class(:C))
Declaration(Class(:D))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:p1))
############################
#   Object Properties
############################

# Object Property: :p1 (:p1)

ObjectPropertyRange(:p1 owl:Thing)


############################
#   Classes
############################

# Class: :A (:A)

EquivalentClasses(:A ObjectAllValuesFrom(:p1 :B))

# Class: :C (:C)

EquivalentClasses(:C ObjectIntersectionOf(ObjectMinCardinality(2 :p1 :D) ObjectMaxCardinality(3 :p1 :D)))


)


]]>
```

1468949123

1468949122

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Generalization in Concept Modeler.

```text
Declaration(
 				Class(:FutsalTeam)
		)
		Declaration(
				Class(:SoccerTeam)
		)
		AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :FutsalTeam "Futsal Team"@en)
		SubClassOf(:FutsalTeam :SoccerTeam)
		AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :SoccerTeam "Soccer Team"@en)
) ]]>
```

```text

```

````html
)
````

```text

ObjectPropertyRange(:hasChild :Child)




)
]]>
```

```text
Import()
Declaration(
Class(:Person)
)
Declaration(
DataProperty(:hasName)
)
Declaration(
AnnotationProperty()
)
Declaration(
Datatype(xsd:string)
)
AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label
:Person "Person"@en)
SubClassOf(
:Person 
ObjectIntersectionOf(
DataMaxCardinality(1
:hasName xsd:string) 
DataMinCardinality(1
:hasName xsd:string)
)
)

:hasName "has name"@en)
DataPropertyDomain(:hasName
:Person)
DataPropertyRange(:hasName
xsd:string)
AnnotationAssertion(http://purl.org/dc/terms/description
 "An
instance of String defines a piece of text. The semantics of the string itself
depends on its purpose, it can be a comment, computational language expression,
OCL expression, etc. It is used for String attributes and String expressions in
the metamodel."@en)
)]]>
```

```text
Declaration(Class(:Husband))
Declaration(Class(:Wife))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasHusband))

############################

FunctionalObjectProperty(:hasHusband)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasHusband :Wife)
ObjectPropertyRange(:hasHusband :Husband)

)
```
