```text
Declaration(Class(:Husband))

)
```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target" style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:height="80"><ri:attachment ri:filename="InverseFunctional0.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Class with Inverse Functional Object Property" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Inverse Functional Object Property</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="08c6aba8-0a26-4663-bde1-85f6684ab4ef"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Ontology(<http://example.com/ontology/InverseFunctional>

Declaration(Class(:Husband))
Declaration(Class(:Wife))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:isHusbandTo))

############################
#   Object Properties
############################

# Object Property: :isHusbandTo (is husband to)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :isHusbandTo "is husband to"^^xsd:string)
InverseFunctionalObjectProperty(:isHusbandTo)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:isHusbandTo :Husband)
ObjectPropertyRange(:isHusbandTo :Wife)



############################
#   Classes
############################

# Class: :Husband (:Husband)

SubClassOf(:Husband ObjectMaxCardinality(1 :isHusbandTo))


)


]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

```text
Annotation(rdfs:label "Example"^^xsd:string)

Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasChild))

############################
#   Object Properties

ObjectPropertyRange(:hasChild :Child)

)
```

## PAGE 00041: Class with necessary and sufficient property

- page_id: `170478341`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478341/Class+with+necessary+and+sufficient+property
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:54.673+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

5273548

5273550

5273549

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A class with necessary and sufficient property.

```text
Declaration(
             Class(:CarManufacturer)
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:Manufacturer)
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:SteeringWheelManufacturer)
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:WindshieldManufacturer)
      )
      Declaration(
             ObjectProperty(:hasContractWith)
      )
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :CarManufacturer "Car Manufacturer"@en)
      EquivalentClasses(
             :CarManufacturer
             ObjectIntersectionOf(
                     ObjectMinCardinality(1 :hasContractWith :SteeringWheelManufacturer)
                     ObjectSomeValuesFrom(:hasContractWith :SteeringWheelManufacturer)
             )
      )
      EquivalentClasses(
             :CarManufacturer 
             ObjectIntersectionOf(
                       ObjectMinCardinality(1 :hasContractWith :WindshieldManufacturer) 
                       ObjectSomeValuesFrom(:hasContractWith :WindshieldManufacturer)
             )
      )
      SubClassOf(:CarManufacturer :Manufacturer)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Manufacturer "Manufacturer"@en)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :SteeringWheelManufacturer "Steering Wheel Manufacturer"@en)
      SubClassOf(:SteeringWheelManufacturer :Manufacturer)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :WindshieldManufacturer "Windshield Manufacturer"@en)
      SubClassOf(:WindshieldManufacturer :Manufacturer)


AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label
:hasContractWith "has contract with"@en)


ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasContractWith
:Manufacturer)


ObjectPropertyRange(:hasContractWith
:Manufacturer)


)]]>
```

````html
:hasContractWith "has contract with"@en)


ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasContractWith
:Manufacturer)


ObjectPropertyRange(:hasContractWith
:Manufacturer)
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478343 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00044: Class with property having unspecified multiplicity

- page_id: `170478343`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478343/Class+with+property+having+unspecified+multiplicity
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:54.816+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

5307430

5307433

5307431

UML allows the cardinality of a property to be left unspecified. The concept modeling profile interprets unspecified cardinalities as being zero to many (“0..*”).

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A class with property whose multiplicity is unspecified.

```text
Declaration(
             Class(:SoccerPlayer)
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:SoccerTeam)
      )
      Declaration(ObjectProperty(:consistsOf))
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label:SoccerPlayer "Soccer Player"@en)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :SoccerTeam "Soccer Team"@en)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :consistsOf "consists of"@en)
      ObjectPropertyDomain(:consistsOf :SoccerTeam)
      ObjectPropertyRange(:consistsOf :SoccerPlayer)
)]]>
```

5307429

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax' space='MCM2024xR1']

The concept modeling profile interprets unspecified cardinalities as being zero to many (“0..*”).</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-24 14:40:30.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Class with property having unspecified multiplicity" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><h6 style="text-align: center;">A class with property whose multiplicity is unspecified.</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c94f7e07-ca71-4325-8d0c-d73263e2ce8e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Ontology(<http://nomagic.com/ontology/example-case/case-21>
````html
      Declaration(
             Class(:SoccerPlayer)
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:SoccerTeam)
      )
      Declaration(ObjectProperty(:consistsOf))
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label:SoccerPlayer "Soccer Player"@en)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :SoccerTeam "Soccer Team"@en)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :consistsOf "consists of"@en)
      ObjectPropertyDomain(:consistsOf :SoccerTeam)
      ObjectPropertyRange(:consistsOf :SoccerPlayer)
)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="29ff9bc5-47dc-4bb8-b45c-3034dd7d94b5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">5307429</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

4996783

4996785

4996784

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A class with subproperty that has no range.

```text
Declaration(
             Class(:Game)
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:SoccerMatch)
      )
      Declaration(
             ObjectProperty(:isACompetitionBetween)
      )
      Declaration(
             ObjectProperty(:isPlayedBetween)
      )
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Game "Game"@en)
      SubClassOf(
             :Game 
             ObjectIntersectionOf(
                    ObjectMinCardinality(2 :isPlayedBetween)
             )
      )
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :SoccerMatch "Soccer Match"@en)
      SubClassOf(:SoccerMatch :Game)
      SubClassOf(
             :SoccerMatch 
             ObjectIntersectionOf(
                    ObjectMaxCardinality(2 :isACompetitionBetween) 
                    ObjectMinCardinality(2 :isACompetitionBetween)
             )
      )
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :isACompetitionBetween "is a competition between"@en)
      SubObjectPropertyOf(:isACompetitionBetween :isPlayedBetween)
      ObjectPropertyDomain(:isACompetitionBetween :SoccerMatch)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :isPlayedBetween "is played between"@en)
      ObjectPropertyDomain(:isPlayedBetween :Game)
)  ]]>
```

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478365 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00050: Class with Transitive Object Property

- page_id: `170478365`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478365/Class+with+Transitive+Object+Property
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:56.122+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A property with a Transitive stereotype

```text
Declaration(Class(:BiologicalMother))
Declaration(Class(:Mary))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasBiologicalGrandmother))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasBiologicalMother))

############################
#   Object Properties
############################

# Object Property: :hasBiologicalGrandmother (has biological grandmother)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasBiologicalGrandmother "has biological grandmother"^^xsd:string)
TransitiveObjectProperty(:hasBiologicalGrandmother)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasBiologicalGrandmother :Mary)
ObjectPropertyRange(:hasBiologicalGrandmother :BiologicalMother)

# Object Property: :hasBiologicalMother (has biological mother)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasBiologicalMother "has biological mother"^^xsd:string)
FunctionalObjectProperty(:hasBiologicalMother)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasBiologicalMother ObjectUnionOf(:BiologicalMother :Mary))
ObjectPropertyRange(:hasBiologicalMother :BiologicalMother)



############################
#   Classes
############################

# Class: :BiologicalMother (Biological Mother)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :BiologicalMother "Biological Mother"^^xsd:string)
SubClassOf(:BiologicalMother ObjectExactCardinality(1 :hasBiologicalMother))

# Class: :Mary (:Mary)

SubClassOf(:Mary ObjectExactCardinality(1 :hasBiologicalGrandmother))
SubClassOf(:Mary ObjectExactCardinality(1 :hasBiologicalMother))


)

]]>
```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="189"><ri:attachment ri:filename="transitiveobjectproperty.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Class with Transitive Object Property" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A property with a Transitive stereotype</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9430b101-828d-4b15-9702-14737f302030"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Ontology(<http://example.com/ontology/Transitive>

Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasBiologicalMother))

TransitiveObjectProperty(:hasBiologicalGrandmother)

ObjectPropertyRange(:hasBiologicalGrandmother :BiologicalMother)

# Object Property: :hasBiologicalMother (has biological mother)

FunctionalObjectProperty(:hasBiologicalMother)

ObjectPropertyRange(:hasBiologicalMother :BiologicalMother)



############################

############################

SubClassOf(:BiologicalMother ObjectExactCardinality(1 :hasBiologicalMother))

# Class: :Mary (:Mary)

SubClassOf(:Mary ObjectExactCardinality(1 :hasBiologicalGrandmother))
SubClassOf(:Mary ObjectExactCardinality(1 :hasBiologicalMother))


)

]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

```text
             Class(:Dog)

      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Dog "Dog"@en)
      SubClassOf(:Dog :Pet)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :DogOwner "Dog Owner"@en)
      SubClassOf(:DogOwner :Person)
      SubClassOf(
             :DogOwner 
             ObjectIntersectionOf(
                   ObjectMinCardinality(1 :has :Dog)
                   ObjectAllValuesFrom(:has :Dog)
             )
      )
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Person "Person"@en)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Pet "Pet"@en)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :has "has"@en)
      ObjectPropertyDomain(:has :Person)
      ObjectPropertyRange(:has :Pet)
)]]>
```

1404676

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478332 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00052: Class with universal quantification constraint on property II

- page_id: `170478332`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478332/Class+with+universal+quantification+constraint+on+property+II
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:54.196+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax
- labels: ['redefined-object-property']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

2197653

2197655

2197654

The following example differs from the previous example primarily because the superclasses “Person” and “Pet” are from a different package than their subclasses “Dog Lover” and “Dog,” respectively. This difference is reflected in the OWL ontology by the import of this namespace.

As shown below in the next diagram, the superclasses “Person” and “Pet”, defined in the package “Case 06”, are a different color and a lighter shade than the classes defined in the package “Case 07”. This color differentiation is to distinguish them from the classes defined on this diagram. MagicDraw’s AutoStyler plugin can automatically set the display properties for classes and other UML elements using the “defined elsewhere” style; that is, when they are shown on a non-defining diagram for the UML element (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Magic Concept Modeler Capabilities' space='MCM2024xR1']).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A class with universal quantification constraint on property II.

```text
Import()
      Declaration(
             Class()
      )
      Declaration(
             Class()
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:Dog)
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:DogLover)
      )
      Declaration(
             ObjectProperty()
      )
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Dog "Dog"@en)
      SubClassOf(:Dog
      )
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :DogLover "Dog Lover"@en)
      SubClassOf(:DogLover )
      SubClassOf(
             :DogLover
                   ObjectAllValuesFrom( :Dog)
      )
)]]>
```

2197652

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax' space='MCM2024xR1']

This difference is reflected in the OWL ontology by the import of this namespace.</p><p>As shown below in the next diagram, the superclasses “Person” and “Pet”, defined in the package “Case 06”, are a different color and a lighter shade than the classes defined in the package “Case 07”. This color differentiation is to distinguish them from the classes defined on this diagram. MagicDraw’s AutoStyler plugin can automatically set the display properties for classes and other UML elements using the “defined elsewhere” style; that is, when they are shown on a non-defining diagram for the UML element (see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Magic Concept Modeler Capabilities" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Automatic Styling of Concept Models]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>).</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-24 14:31:17.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Class with universal quantification constraint on property II" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A class with universal quantification constraint on property II.</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bc473ef3-8f19-48c1-92a4-9fc5b0c09a76"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Ontology(<http://nomagic.com/ontology/example-case/case-07>
````html
      Import(<http://nomagic.com/ontology/example-case/case-06>)
      Declaration(
             Class(<http://nomagic.com/ontology/example-case/case-06#Person>)
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(<http://nomagic.com/ontology/example-case/case-06#Pet>)
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:Dog)
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:DogLover)
      )
      Declaration(
             ObjectProperty(<http://nomagic.com/ontology/example-case/case-06#has>)
      )
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Dog "Dog"@en)
      SubClassOf(:Dog
      <http://nomagic.com/ontology/example-case/case-06#Pet>)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :DogLover "Dog Lover"@en)
      SubClassOf(:DogLover <http://nomagic.com/ontology/example-case/case-06#Person>)
      SubClassOf(
             :DogLover
                   ObjectAllValuesFrom(<http://nomagic.com/ontology/example-case/case-06#has> :Dog)
      )
)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f37234fd-2227-4947-b5d8-394e6a44840c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">2197652</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478153 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00053: Classes

- page_id: `170478153`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478153/Classes
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:48.229+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Complex Chain

73480483

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Complex Chain

73480486

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Complex Chain

73480484

In the concept modeling interpretation of the UML standard, a class is a set or collection of individual things called *members*. The members of a class in a concept model are either things that exist in the real world around us, or things we can imagine to exist, such as unicorns. For example, depending on the stated scope of a concept model, the members of a Chair class would include the one you sit upon to do your work, or the one in a warehouse ready to be shipped to a customer.

#### INFO: UML Classes

UML Classes

The owner is no longer shown by default on a UML Class in Concept Modeling diagrams.

**Anonymous Classes**

An unnamed UML Class represents an anonymous class expression, used for defining conditions, including domains and ranges.

#### INFO: Complex Chain

Complex Chain

Importing a complex chain of restrictions no longer shares anonymous UML classes among multiple concept models.

When you hover over the newly created anonymous class, description of each anonymous class appears in the containment tree, specification window, and diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Containment Tree

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Specification Window

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Diagram Pane

When you hover over the newly created anonymous class, which has self stereotyped property enabled, an appropriate description appears as shown below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Appropriate explanation shown for anonymous class having self stereotypes property

73480482

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']

## PAGE 00054: Complement disjoint with Class

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

```text
Declaration(Class(:A))
Declaration(Class(:B))
Declaration(Class(:C))
Declaration(Class(:D))
############################
#   Classes
############################

# Class: :B (:B)

DisjointClasses(:B ObjectComplementOf(:A))

# Class: :C (:C)

DisjointClasses(:C ObjectComplementOf(:D))


)


]]>
```

```text
#   Object Properties
############################

# Object Property: :p1 (:p1)

ObjectPropertyRange(:p1 owl:Thing)



DisjointClasses(ObjectComplementOf(:A) ObjectAllValuesFrom(:p1 :B))
)
```


````html
]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478268 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00067: Complement Of

- page_id: `170478268`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478268/Complement+Of
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:51.592+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1610555878

1610555889

1610555879

You can define a concept which is specifically the *complement of* a set of other concepts. Magic Concept Modeler (MCM) supports defining complement sets, exporting complements to an ontology as appropriate corresponding OWL constructs, and importing such OWL constructs into MCM. To learn how to work with the feature, please read [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with Complement Of' space='CCMP'].

The set B, denoted by the red area, represents the complement of the set A, which is denoted in the gray area, as shown below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Complement sets**

Stating that B is the complement of A means that any individual that is a member of B is not a member of A. Furthermore, any individual in the set A is not in the set B.

MCM provides the use of a property with a *{complement of}* stereotype to specify that one concept is the complement of another concept. (Using a generalization would be incorrect, as there is no specification/generalization relationship between a concept and its complement.) Such complement concepts will be allowed to be used as the type of a restricted property, a member of a union, or a member of an intersection. The concepts in a complement relationship can be named or unnamed. If you are using a property with a specific stereotype to define the complement relationship, that property can be owned by an association. This allows for the complement relationship to be owned in a concept model that owns neither of the concepts that are part of the relationship.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**OWL Description**

**Importing and Exporting 'Complement Of'**

MCM supports importing and exporting OWL complementOf stereotypes. When importing such a stereotype, MCM will create a complement relationship as described above. For instance, given the OWL description in the second representation above, MCM will create a complementOf relationship between two concepts A and B:

MCM will also export concepts A and B to an OWL complementOf construct, as shown in the second representation above. As a complementOf assertion can be defined in a namespace different from the namespace(s) of its domain and range, MCM will maintain that separation of the corresponding concepts and relationship in order to be able to correctly export the relationship back to OWL. As noted above, this will be possible as per the use of the {complement of} constraint on a property in MCM.

1610555877

**Related Pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b93058c1-bcbf-4376-acbc-98d10c6bb747"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1610555878</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1e1928f0-cd15-4af3-a064-dc58b3f55c22"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1610555889</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="be1c9930-8357-4791-b201-da9a95297f70"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1610555879</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can define a concept which is specifically the <em>complement of</em> a set of other concepts. Magic Concept Modeler (MCM) supports defining complement sets, exporting complements to an ontology as appropriate corresponding OWL constructs, and importing such OWL constructs into MCM. To learn how to work with the feature, please read <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CCMP" ri:content-title="Working with Complement Of" /></ac:link>.</p><p>The set B, denoted by the red area, represents the complement of the set A, which is denoted in the gray area, as shown below.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2019-7-24_16-42-13.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Complement Of" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="text-align: center;"> <strong>Complement sets</strong></p><p>Stating that B is the complement of A means that any individual that is a member of B is not a member of A. Furthermore, any individual in the set A is not in the set B.</p><p>MCM provides the use of a property with a <em>{complement of}</em> stereotype to specify that one concept is the complement of another concept. (Using a generalization would be incorrect, as there is no specification/generalization relationship between a concept and its complement.) Such complement concepts will be allowed to be used as the type of a restricted property, a member of a union, or a member of an intersection. The concepts in a complement relationship can be named or unnamed. If you are using a property with a specific stereotype to define the complement relationship, that property can be owned by an association. This allows for the complement relationship to be owned in a concept model that owns neither of the concepts that are part of the relationship.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2019-7-24_16-52-2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Complement Of" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="text-align: center;"> <strong>OWL Description </strong></p><p><strong>Importing and Exporting 'Complement Of'</strong></p><p>MCM supports importing and exporting OWL complementOf stereotypes. When importing such a stereotype, MCM will create a complement relationship as described above. For instance, given the OWL description in the second representation above, MCM will create a complementOf relationship between two concepts A and B:</p><p>MCM will also export concepts A and B to an OWL complementOf construct, as shown in the second representation above.  As a complementOf assertion can be defined in a namespace different from the namespace(s) of its domain and range, MCM will maintain that separation of the corresponding concepts and relationship in order to be able to correctly export the relationship back to OWL.  As noted above, this will be possible as per the use of the {complement of} constraint on a property in MCM.</p><p><br /></p><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3e2b88b0-e4ce-411c-85a3-ef85ce861d2b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1610555877</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

## PAGE 00068: Complement subset of Class

- page_id: `170478476`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478476/Complement+subset+of+Class
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-31T14:20:59.757+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax > Necessary and sufficient conditions of anonymous subclasses
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1563800799

1563800810

1563800809

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

```text
Import()

Declaration(Class(:A))
Declaration(Class(:B))

SubClassOf(ObjectComplementOf(:A) :B)
)


]]>
```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2c37c590-3647-447e-8b49-860e6e96b640"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1563800799</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b452f845-ddb5-48d1-91b9-6b88df7b72ba"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1563800810</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="37432914-a2d2-46e5-9490-87514cc544b2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1563800809</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="CCM__ClassNotationAnalysisRow227__ClassNotationAnalysisRow227.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Complement subset of Class" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ed387638-7ad3-4370-a9e4-5e9ff0b6fb6d"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Ontology(<http://www.semanticweb.org/wmilnor/ontologies/2018/3/ClassNotationAnalysisRow227>

Declaration(Class(:B))

)


]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478254 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00073: Complete and disjoint subclasses

- page_id: `170478254`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478254/Complete+and+disjoint+subclasses
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:54.284+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics > Generalization
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

74929400

74929402

74929401

This variation means that an instance can only be classified by one of the subclasses. The instance cannot be classified as only the superclass, and it cannot be classified by two subclasses at the same time.

For example, in the subsequent diagram, two instances are shown. One is an instance of “Windshield Manufacturer”, and one is an instance of “Car Manufacturer”. There can be no instance of “Manufacturer” that is not also an instance of one of the subclasses, and there can be no instance that is classified as both a “Windshield Manufacturer” and a “Car Manufacturer” at the same time.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Complete and disjoint instances.

The diagram below shows an example of complete and disjoint subclasses in standard UML notation. The diagram shows that “Steering Wheel Manufacturer”, “Car Manufacturer”, and “Windshield Manufacturer” are all subclasses of “Manufacturer”. In addition, the standard UML {complete, disjoint} notation declares that the subclasses are complete and disjoint.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Complete and disjoint subclasses in standard UML notation.

74929399

- Disjoint Subclasses
- Overlapping and incomplete subclasses

One is an instance of “Windshield Manufacturer”, and one is an instance of “Car Manufacturer”. There can be no instance of “Manufacturer” that is not also an instance of one of the subclasses, and there can be no instance that is classified as both a “Windshield Manufacturer” and a “Car Manufacturer” at the same time.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:height="250"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-23 13:18:31.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Complete and disjoint subclasses" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Complete and disjoint instances.</h6><p>The diagram below shows an example of complete and disjoint subclasses in standard UML notation. The diagram shows that “Steering Wheel Manufacturer”, “Car Manufacturer”, and “Windshield Manufacturer” are all subclasses of “Manufacturer”. In addition, the standard UML {complete, disjoint} notation declares that the subclasses are complete and disjoint.<span style="text-align: center;"> </span></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-23 13:19:44.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Complete and disjoint subclasses" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Complete and disjoint subclasses in standard UML notation.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cb215a44-37a2-4d1c-abc4-26273423106e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">74929399</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Generalization" /></ac:link></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Complete+subclasses">Complete subclasses</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Disjoint+subclasses">Disjoint Subclasses</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Overlapping+and+incomplete+subclasses">Overlapping and incomplete subclasses</a></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478251 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00074: Complete subclasses

- page_id: `170478251`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478251/Complete+subclasses
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:54.223+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics > Generalization
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

74747367

74747369

74747368

This variation means that an instance can only be classified by one of the subclasses; it cannot be classified by only the superclass. However, an instance of a subclass is indirectly an instance of a superclass at the same time.

For example, the following diagram shows three instances. One is an instance of “Windshield Manufacturer”, one is an instance of “Car Manufacturer”, and one is an instance of both “Car Manufacturer” and “Windshield Manufacturer”. Note that there can be no instance of “Manufacturer” that is not also an instance of one of the subclasses.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of complete subclasses.

The following diagram shows an example of complete subclasses in standard UML notation. The diagram shows that “Steering Wheel Manufacturer”, “Car Manufacturer”, and “Windshield Manufacturer” are all subclasses of “Manufacturer”. In addition, the standard UML {complete, overlapping} notation declares that the subclasses are complete and overlapping.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Complete subclasses in standard UML notation.

74747366

**Related pages**

- Concept Modeling Semantics
- [Generalization](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Generalization)
- Disjoint and complete subclasses
- Disjoint Subclasses
- [Overlapping and incomplete subclasses](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Overlapping+and+incomplete+subclasses)

However, an instance of a subclass is indirectly an instance of a superclass at the same time.</p><p>For example, the following diagram shows three instances. One is an instance of “Windshield Manufacturer”, one is an instance of “Car Manufacturer”, and one is an instance of both “Car Manufacturer” and “Windshield Manufacturer”. Note that there can be no instance of “Manufacturer” that is not also an instance of one of the subclasses.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:height="250"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-23 12:2:2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Complete subclasses" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">An example of complete subclasses.</h6><p>The following diagram shows an example of complete subclasses in standard UML notation. The diagram shows that “Steering Wheel Manufacturer”, “Car Manufacturer”, and “Windshield Manufacturer” are all subclasses of “Manufacturer”. In addition, the standard UML {complete, overlapping} notation declares that the subclasses are complete and overlapping.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-23 12:2:26.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Complete subclasses" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Complete subclasses in standard UML notation.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ba6fbab6-436d-49a3-91b0-0c641da7596b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">74747366</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li class="ancestor-link"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Concept+Modeling+Semantics">Concept Modeling Semantics</a></li><li class="ancestor-link parent-link"><p><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Generalization">Generalization</a></p></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Disjoint+and+complete+subclasses">Disjoint and complete subclasses</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Disjoint+subclasses">Disjoint Subclasses</a></li><li><p><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Overlapping+and+incomplete+subclasses">Overlapping and incomplete subclasses</a></p></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478272 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00075: Concept model export URI style

- page_id: `170478272`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478272/Concept+model+export+URI+style
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:51.802+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Importing and Exporting URI/IRI

1610536686

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Importing and Exporting URI/IRI

1610536697

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Importing and Exporting URI/IRI

1610536687

The Web uses Uniform Resource Identifiers (URIs) as a global identification system. A URI is used to identify a resource, such as a document or an abstract thing, either by a location, such as a DNS host name and a path on that machine, or a name.

When identifying real-world objects using a URI, you can choose **Hash URI** or **303 URI**. The differences are as follows:

- Hash URI: for smaller and stable sets of resources that evolve together, e.g. RDF Schema vocabularies and OWL ontologies. The advantage is that all resources are in the same file, because the redirection target cannot be configured separately for each resource.
- 303 URI: for large-scale data sets that are likely to grow over time. One document can be used for describing either each or all resources. When using 303 URI for an ontology, it can reduce a client’s performance and cause higher latency.

#### NOTE: Importing and Exporting URI/IRI

Importing and Exporting URI/IRI

- The Concept Modeler imports and preserves the URI or IRI for every OWL class and property from an OWL ontology file as the tagged value on the corresponding UML class and property in the Concept Modeler.
- When exporting this particular model back to OWL, the Concept Modeler will not apply the normal automatic URI/IRI generation.
- It will preserve the URI or IRI from the original OWL ontology, so that the classes and properties can be used with their correct URIs/IRIs.
- The export URI style option also has no effect on the preserved URI or IRI.

1610536685

**Related Pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']

A URI is used to identify a resource, such as a document or an abstract thing, either by a location, such as a DNS host name and a path on that machine, or a name.</p><p>When identifying real-world objects using a URI, you can choose <strong>Hash URI</strong> or <strong>303 URI</strong>. The differences are as follows:</p><ul><li><strong>Hash URI:</strong> for smaller and stable sets of resources that evolve together, e.g. RDF Schema vocabularies and OWL ontologies. The advantage is that all resources are in the same file, because the redirection target cannot be configured separately for each resource.</li><li><strong>303 URI:</strong> for large-scale data sets that are likely to grow over time. One document can be used for describing either each or all resources. When using 303 URI for an ontology, it can reduce a client’s performance and cause higher latency.</li></ul><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2a6540a2-6cd5-4e15-86e1-2a6d2675cc93"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Importing and Exporting URI/IRI</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li class="_mce_tagged_br">The Concept Modeler imports and preserves the URI or IRI for every OWL class and property from an OWL ontology file as the tagged value on the corresponding UML class and property in the Concept Modeler.</li><li><p class="_mce_tagged_br">When exporting this particular model back to OWL, the Concept Modeler will not apply the normal automatic URI/IRI generation.</p></li><li><p class="_mce_tagged_br">It will preserve the URI or IRI from the original OWL ontology, so that the classes and properties can be used with their correct URIs/IRIs.</p></li><li><p class="_mce_tagged_br">The export URI style option also has no effect on the preserved URI or IRI.</p></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8241504e-bc7d-40be-9354-bc558c666ae3"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1610536685</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478145 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00076: Concept modeling purpose

- page_id: `170478145`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478145/Concept+modeling+purpose
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:53.564+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Introduction
- labels: ['concept-modeling', 'ontologies', 'reasoners', 'open-world-assumption', 'closed-world-assumption']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

696272340

696272342

696272341

When building a system for a business, there exists a wide variety of methodologies to choose from, as well as numerous existing models, profiles and plug-ins across any given enterprise. What should be the starting point of the effort, business concepts, is often lost in overwhelming technical detail. Many profiles are at such an intricate technology level (e.g., DDL, XSD, AndroMDA) that a developing team is faced with too many technical choices, leading to inconsistent models. Technology concerns drag down the level of abstraction to the point in which business concerns can get "lost in the weeds." Aligning models becomes too difficult and too much work, resulting almost invariably in disconnected model silos.

A business concept model, unifying business concepts across an enterprise, is the basis for a solution to this dilemma. A concept model represents the concepts and defining relationships of the *business*. It is a model of the real world of the business, not the data used by business systems. Additionally, the concept model provides the vocabulary for process models that describe the way the business is run. The concept model is created by capturing the business knowledge of business experts. It is understood by business experts and validated by business experts.

Data models, defining the definition and structure of data used by a system, can be built or generated by "subsetting" a concept model. The concept model becomes the "Rosetta Stone" for enterprise level semantic integration, e.g., automatically generating data transformations between systems within the enterprise described by the concept model.

696272339

**Related pages**

- MDA
- The role of ontologies and reasoners
- Open-world assumption vs. closed-world assumption
- Information modeling purpose

What should be the starting point of the effort, business concepts, is often lost in overwhelming technical detail. Many profiles are at such an intricate technology level (e.g., DDL, XSD, AndroMDA) that a developing team is faced with too many technical choices, leading to inconsistent models. Technology concerns drag down the level of abstraction to the point in which business concerns can get &quot;lost in the weeds.&quot; Aligning models becomes too difficult and too much work, resulting almost invariably in disconnected model silos.</p><p>A business concept model, unifying business concepts across an enterprise, is the basis for a solution to this dilemma. A concept model represents the concepts and defining relationships of the <em>business</em>. It is a model of the real world of the business, not the data used by business systems. Additionally, the concept model provides the vocabulary for process models that describe the way the business is run. The concept model is created by capturing the business knowledge of business experts. It is understood by business experts and validated by business experts.</p><p>Data models, defining the definition and structure of data used by a system, can be built or generated by &quot;subsetting&quot; a concept model. The concept model becomes the &quot;Rosetta Stone&quot; for enterprise level semantic integration, e.g., automatically generating data transformations between systems within the enterprise described by the concept model.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f1652313-ff44-4af9-8cd3-95d9253b7368"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">696272339</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul class="childpages-macro"><li class="current"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/MDA">MDA</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/The+role+of+ontologies+and+reasoners">The role of ontologies and reasoners</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Open-world+assumption+vs.+closed-world+assumption">Open-world assumption vs. closed-world assumption</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Information+modeling+purpose">Information modeling purpose</a></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478150 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00077: Concept Modeling Semantics

- page_id: `170478150`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478150/Concept+Modeling+Semantics
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-31T14:21:54.615+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Available From 19.0 SP2

1282297602

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Available From 19.0 SP2

1282297604

1282297603

In order to improve UML’s suitability for modeling real-world concepts, the Concept Modeler interprets the UML standard to allow subproperties, existential quantification constraints, and universal quantification constraints. In addition to those interpretations, the Concept Modeler uses a small UML profile to add the capabilities of global properties, necessary and sufficient properties, and other future capabilities. Simply having or applying a «Concept Model» stereotype on a UML package causes anything within that package to have this interpretation, and allows these added capabilities.

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Complex Chain

379122602

###### classClass

In the concept modeling interpretation of the UML standard, a class is a set or collection of individual things called *members*. The members of a class in a concept model are either things that exist in the real world around us, or things we can imagine to exist, such as unicorns. For example, depending on the stated scope of a concept model, the members of a Chair class would include the one you sit upon to do your work, or the one in a warehouse ready to be shipped to a customer.

#### INFO: UML Classes

UML Classes

The owner is no longer shown by default on a UML Class in Concept Modeling diagrams.

**Anonymous Classes**

An unnamed UML Class represents an anonymous class expression, used for defining conditions, including domains and ranges.

#### INFO: Complex Chain

Complex Chain

Importing a complex chain of restrictions no longer shares anonymous UML classes among multiple concept models.

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Available From 19.0 SP2

379157074

###### Anonymous Union Classes

An anonymous union is an unnamed class used to represent a set of classes that can be used as a type of a property. An anonymous union class always implies a complete subclass generalization. (See [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Complete subclasses' space='MCM2024xR1'].)

The following diagram states that an instance of a Person may have a value of type Cat or Dog for the *cares for*property. The diagram also states that an instance of a Cat or a Dog may have a value of type Person for the *cared for by* property.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An anonymous union class.

In an ontology, if anonymous union, with same classes within the union, is used in multiple places, the Concept Modeler can distinguish it when importing the ontology. In other words, if the anonymous union has the same union members, the Concept Modeler will identify it as the same anonymous union.

GreenAvailable From 19.0 SP2

An anonymous Class with Subclasses but without an explicit «Union» is now treated the same as a union of its Subclasses. The following image show what that means.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The boxed diagram represents the usual relationship between an anonymous Union class. Notice the stereotype, *«Union»*. On the other hand, the diagram not boxed represents a similar structure but there's no anonymous Union class with the stereotype, *«Union»*. The modeling tool is programmed to consider the non-stereotyped class with the subclasses as a Union. Let's see that in the exported OWL of this model.

Reading the following code piece, notice the ObjectUnionOf between A and B, and the ObjectIUnionOf between C and D.

```text

```

The full ontology is displayed below:

```text
Declaration(Class(:A))
Declaration(Class(:B))
Declaration(Class(:C))
Declaration(Class(:D))
Declaration(Class(:P))
Declaration(Class(:Q))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasP))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasQ))

############################
#   Object Properties
############################

# Object Property: :hasP (has p)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasP "has p"^^xsd:string)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasP ObjectUnionOf(:A :B))
ObjectPropertyRange(:hasP :P)

# Object Property: :hasQ (has q)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasQ "has q"^^xsd:string)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasQ ObjectUnionOf(:C :D))
ObjectPropertyRange(:hasQ :Q)



############################
#   Classes
############################

# Class: :A (a)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :A "a"^^xsd:string)

# Class: :B (b)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :B "b"^^xsd:string)

# Class: :C (c)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :C "c"^^xsd:string)

# Class: :D (d)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :D "d"^^xsd:string)

# Class: :P (p)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :P "p"^^xsd:string)

# Class: :Q (q)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Q "q"^^xsd:string)


)]]>
```

For example, depending on the stated scope of a concept model, the members of a Chair class would include the one you sit upon to do your work, or the one in a warehouse ready to be shipped to a customer.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2158da44-e620-4ee5-9416-7b3e6804a8fd"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">UML Classes</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The owner is no longer shown by default on a UML Class in Concept Modeling diagrams.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong>Anonymous Classes</strong></p><p>An unnamed UML Class represents an anonymous class expression, used for defining conditions, including domains and ranges.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9c77841e-74ba-409e-a83b-a053a6375f4e"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Complex Chain</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Importing a complex chain of restrictions no longer shares anonymous UML classes among multiple concept models.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="83b4ab02-268e-4759-a8f6-03404bf6fae8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">379157074</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><h5>Anonymous Union Classes</h5><p>An anonymous union is an unnamed class used to represent a set of classes that can be used as a type of a property. An anonymous union class always implies a complete subclass generalization. (See <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Complete subclasses" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Complete Subclasses]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.)</p><p>The following diagram states that an instance of a Person may have a value of type Cat or Dog for the <em>cares for </em>property. The diagram also states that an instance of a Cat or a Dog may have a value of type Person for the <em>cared for by</em> property.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-23 13:22:5.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">An anonymous union class.</h6><p>In an ontology, if anonymous union, with same classes within the union, is used in multiple places, the Concept Modeler can distinguish it when importing the ontology. In other words, if the anonymous union has the same union members, the Concept Modeler will identify it as the same anonymous union.</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="status" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b018c0fe-00e6-4d6c-a82b-570d76b46208"><ac:parameter ac:name="colour">Green</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Available From 19.0 SP2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><p>An anonymous Class with Subclasses but without an explicit «Union» is now treated the same as a union of its Subclasses. The following image show what that means.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="u2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>The boxed diagram represents the usual relationship between an anonymous Union class. Notice the stereotype, <em>«Union»</em>. On the other hand, the diagram not boxed represents a similar structure but there's no anonymous Union class with the stereotype, <em>«Union»</em>. The modeling tool is programmed to consider the non-stereotyped class with the subclasses as a Union. Let's see that in the exported OWL of this model.</p><p>Reading the following code piece, notice the ObjectUnionOf between A and B, and the ObjectIUnionOf between C and D.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="df5d7f2f-a6ab-442e-a5f3-e60e80a32d7f"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[# Object Property: :hasP (has p)
````html
ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasP ObjectUnionOf(:A :B))
ObjectPropertyRange(:hasP :P)
# Object Property: :hasQ (has q)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasQ ObjectUnionOf(:C :D))
ObjectPropertyRange(:hasQ :Q)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The full ontology is displayed below:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a33e7606-9465-4719-a8c3-0ef7e555d054"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Ontology(<http://example.com/ontology/uniondemo>

Declaration(Class(:A))
Declaration(Class(:B))
Declaration(Class(:C))
Declaration(Class(:D))
Declaration(Class(:P))
Declaration(Class(:Q))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasP))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasQ))

############################
#   Object Properties
############################

# Object Property: :hasP (has p)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasP "has p"^^xsd:string)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasP ObjectUnionOf(:A :B))
ObjectPropertyRange(:hasP :P)

# Object Property: :hasQ (has q)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasQ "has q"^^xsd:string)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasQ ObjectUnionOf(:C :D))
ObjectPropertyRange(:hasQ :Q)



############################
#   Classes
############################

# Class: :A (a)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :A "a"^^xsd:string)

# Class: :B (b)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :B "b"^^xsd:string)

# Class: :C (c)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :C "c"^^xsd:string)

# Class: :D (d)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :D "d"^^xsd:string)

# Class: :P (p)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :P "p"^^xsd:string)

# Class: :Q (q)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Q "q"^^xsd:string)


)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478189 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00078: Conditions

- page_id: `170478189`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478189/Conditions
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:49.004+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A condition is any kind of class expression: union, intersection, named, property restriction, etc. There are three kinds of conditions: Necessary, Sufficient, and Necessary and Sufficient.

**Necessary Conditions**

A property's multiplicity or type is declared in the context of an owning class or a property holder. When the minimum cardinality is at least one, these declarations are always *necessary* conditions for an instance to be a member of the owning class, or, in the case of a property holder, for an instance to be valid at all.

**Necessary and Sufficient Conditions**

Another kind of condition is known as both *necessary and sufficient*. A class with at least one necessary and sufficient condition is known as a *defined* class, which means the differentiating characteristics of the class that make it distinguishable from its parent and sibling classes are defined. Note that using a necessary and sufficient condition on a property with a minimum cardinality of zero is not meaningful.

In the modeling tool, a necessary and sufficient condition for a UML class are intersected together, and the UML class is equivalent to that intersection. The condition is available as a stereotype in the concept model and it can applied to a UML Generalization or a property restriction.

###### GreenAvailable From 18.0 SP12 Aristotelian Definitions

An Aristotelian definition is achieved when you want to model concept B as a kind of concept A. More specifically, this definition is achieved when the concept B is equivalent to all of the necessary and sufficient conditions of the concept A intersected with all the necessary and sufficient conditions of concept B.

Another explanation is:

- Concept A is equivalent to the intersection of Necessary & Sufficient conditions of A.
- Concept B is equivalent to the intersection of (intersection of Necessary & Sufficient conditions of A) and (the intersection of Necessary & Sufficient conditions of B).

#### INFO: Importing and Exporting

Importing and Exporting

The modeling tool supports importing and exporting Aristotelian definitions.

An Aristotelian definition can have only a single sufficient property constraint or generalization attached to it.

In a MCM model, given a class that has one or more sufficient property constraints and/or generalizations, when that class is exported the OWL will specify that the class is owl:equivalent to a owl:Class that is owl:intersectionOf all the restrictions and/or superclasses corresponding to those sufficient property constraints and/or generalizations, respectively. In the case that a MCM class has only a single sufficient property constraint of generalizations, during export MCM creates the equivalent intersection of that single sufficient property constraint or generalization. As it is an intersection containing only a single element that means that the class is equivalent to that single restriction or the superclass. Due to this, OWLAPI removes the intersection axiom to simplify the OWL.

****

**Example**

The diagram below shows that when an instance with the property “has contract with” satisfies specific multiplicity (“1..*”) and type constraints (of type ‘Steering Wheel Manufacturer” or “Windshield Manufacturer”) for the property’s values, the instance meets a necessary and sufficient condition to be a member of the class “Car Manufacturer”. Therefore, an inferencing engine would classify this as an instance of the class “Car Manufacturer”. As discussed above, an instance meeting any one of these necessary and sufficient conditions is enough to classify the instance regardless of conditions on the values of any other properties with the {sufficient} constraint owned by the class “Car Manufacturer”. The conditions on the values of these properties become necessary conditions on an instance for it to be a valid member of class “Car Manufacturer.” Also, an instance meeting any one of these necessary and sufficient conditions is enough to distinguish instances of the class “Car Manufacturer’ from its parent class “Manufacturer.”

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**An example of necessary and sufficient condition.**

**Related Pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A condition is any kind of class expression: union, intersection, named, property restriction, etc. There are three kinds of conditions: Necessary, Sufficient, and Necessary and Sufficient.</p><p><strong>Necessary Conditions</strong></p><p>A property's multiplicity or type is declared in the context of an owning class or a property holder. When the minimum cardinality is at least one, these declarations are always <em>necessary</em> conditions for an instance to be a member of the owning class, or, in the case of a property holder, for an instance to be valid at all.</p><p><strong>Necessary and Sufficient Conditions</strong></p><p>Another kind of condition is known as both <em>necessary and sufficient</em>. A class with at least one necessary and sufficient condition is known as a <em>defined</em> class, which means the differentiating characteristics of the class that make it distinguishable from its parent and sibling classes are defined. Note that using a necessary and sufficient condition on a property with a minimum cardinality of zero is not meaningful.</p><p>In the modeling tool, a necessary and sufficient condition for a UML class are intersected together, and the UML class is equivalent to that intersection. The condition is available as a stereotype in the concept model and it can applied to a UML Generalization or a property restriction.</p><h4><ac:structured-macro ac:name="status" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f3a4f083-df4c-4ac2-86b1-026656d640cb"><ac:parameter ac:name="colour">Green</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Available From 18.0 SP12</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro> Aristotelian Definitions</h4><p>An Aristotelian definition is achieved when you want to model concept B as a kind of concept A. More specifically, this definition is achieved when the concept B is equivalent to all of the necessary and sufficient conditions of the concept A intersected with all the necessary and sufficient conditions of concept B. </p><p>Another explanation is:</p><ul><li>Concept A is equivalent to the intersection of Necessary &amp; Sufficient conditions of A.</li><li>Concept B is equivalent to the intersection of (intersection of Necessary &amp; Sufficient conditions of A) <strong>and</strong> (the intersection of Necessary &amp; Sufficient conditions of B).</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="203a79d0-6541-4eb8-8efc-fd901d83ecef"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Importing and Exporting</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The modeling tool supports importing and exporting Aristotelian definitions.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>An Aristotelian definition can have only a single sufficient property constraint or generalization attached to it.</p><p>In a MCM model, given a class that has one or more sufficient property constraints and/or generalizations, when that class is exported the OWL will specify that the class is owl:equivalent to a owl:Class that is owl:intersectionOf all the restrictions and/or superclasses corresponding to those sufficient property constraints and/or generalizations, respectively.  In the case that a MCM class has only a single sufficient property constraint of generalizations, during export MCM creates the equivalent intersection of that single sufficient property constraint or generalization.  As it is an intersection containing only a single element that means that the class is equivalent to that single restriction or the superclass.  Due to this, OWLAPI removes the intersection axiom to simplify the OWL.</p><p><strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong>Example</strong></p><p>The diagram below shows that when an instance with the property “has contract with” satisfies specific multiplicity (“1..*”) and type constraints (of type ‘Steering Wheel Manufacturer” or “Windshield Manufacturer”) for the property’s values, the instance meets a necessary and sufficient condition to be a member of the class “Car Manufacturer”. Therefore, an inferencing engine would classify this as an instance of the class “Car Manufacturer”. As discussed above, an instance meeting any one of these necessary and sufficient conditions is enough to classify the instance regardless of conditions on the values of any other properties with the {sufficient} constraint owned by the class “Car Manufacturer”. The conditions on the values of these properties become necessary conditions on an instance for it to be a valid member of class “Car Manufacturer.” Also, an instance meeting any one of these necessary and sufficient conditions is enough to distinguish instances of the class “Car Manufacturer’ from its parent class “Manufacturer.”</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="233"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ns6.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Conditions" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="text-align: center;"><strong>An example of necessary and sufficient condition.</strong></p><p><br /></p><p><strong>Related Pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478665 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00079: Converting a UML model into a concept model

- page_id: `170478665`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478665/Converting+a+UML+model+into+a+concept+model
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:55.420+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

65451481

65451483

65451482

To change a UML model to a concept model

1. Open an existing UML project.
2. On the main menu, click **File** > **Use Project** > **Use Local Project**. The **Use Project** dialog opens. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Select **Profile** and **Concept Modeling Profile**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Click **Next**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Specify usage options and click **Finish**. The **Concept Modeling Profile** is added to your project in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Containment Tree' space='MD2024xR1'].
6. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a concept modeling project' space='MCM2024xR1'] in your project.
7. Right-click the created package and select **Stereotype**.
8. Select the stereotype **« » Concept Model [Package]** and click **Apply**. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
9. On the main menu, select **File** > **Open Project** to open a new concept modeling project.
10. On the main menu, click **Options** > **Project** to open the **Project Options** dialog.
11. Click **Symbol styles** from the tree view. Select the **Default** or **Defined Elsewhere** style to the UML project and click **Export**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
12. Switch to the previous UML project and click **Options** > **Project** from the main menu to open the **Project Options** dialog. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
13. Select **Symbol styles** and click **Import**.
14. Select the exported **Default** and **Define Elsewhere** styles and click **OK**. The imported styles are added to the **Symbol styles** under the **Default (Default)** style.
15. Select the imported **Default** style and click **Make Default**. The imported **Default** is now the default style. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
16. Select the old **Default** style and click **Delete**. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
17. Click **OK**.
18. From the main menu, click **Options** > **Project**. The **Project Options** dialog opens.
19. Expand **Default model properties** and select **Association**.
20. Change the **Association** visibility to **public**. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
21. Select **Property** and change the **Property** visibility to **public**. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
22. Click **OK**.

65460621

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Usage' space='MCM2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a concept model' space='MCM2024xR1']

To create a concept model

1. Right-click a package in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Containment Tree' space='MD2024xR1'].
2. Select **Concept Modeling**.
3. Select **Create Concept Model**. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Using the Create Concept Model shortcut menu to create a concept model. NoteIf an Unnamed package already exists in the Containment tree, a number in the package name will be added or incremented.
4. The result should look as shown below.
