<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478836 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00117: Exporting models and concept models at any level in package hierarchy

- page_id: `170478836`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478836/Exporting+models+and+concept+models+at+any+level+in+package+hierarchy
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:57.006+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Exporting your concept model to an OWL ontology
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Canceling Bulk Exporting

154875173

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Canceling Bulk Exporting

154875175

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Canceling Bulk Exporting

154875174

Magic Concept Modeler can export «Model» and «Concept Model» stereotyped packages at any level under a package hierarchy by right-clicking any package in the hierarchy. The selected package(s) and their sub-packages that are «Model» or «Concept Model» stereotyped will be exported.

To export the selected package(s) and their sub-packages that are «Model» or «Concept Model» stereotyped

1. Select one or more packages (by holding CTRL when selecting other packages) to export. Right-click one of the selected packages and select Export Model to OWL . [ATTACHMENT filename='Hierarchy Package Level.png']
2. In the Browse dialog, specify the export location.
3. Click Open . The fileExportPath tag value will be updated, and the selected package(s) and their sub-packages that are «Model» or «Concept Model» stereotyped will be exported.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

For server projects, you must lock the package(s) first to update tag value.

#### NOTE: Failure in Bulk Exporting

Failure in Bulk Exporting

A failure in multi-package bulk export to OWL now does not cancel exporting all the remaining packages.

#### NOTE: Canceling Bulk Exporting

Canceling Bulk Exporting

Clicking Cancel for bulk export to OWL operation also cancels the export of all the Packages.

154875172

**Related pages**

- Usage
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exporting your concept model to an OWL ontology' space='MCM2024xR1']

The selected package(s) and their sub-packages that are «Model» or «Concept Model» stereotyped will be exported.</p><p><br /></p><p>To export the selected package(s) and their sub-packages that are «Model» or «Concept Model» stereotyped</p><hr /><ol><li>Select one or more packages (by holding <strong>CTRL</strong> when selecting other packages) to export. Right-click one of the selected packages and select <strong>Export Model to OWL</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Hierarchy Package Level.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Exporting models and concept models at any level in package hierarchy" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>In the Browse dialog, specify the export location.</li><li class="auto-cursor-target">Click <strong>Open</strong>. The <strong>fileExportPath</strong> tag value will be updated, and the selected package(s) and their sub-packages that are «Model» or «Concept Model» stereotyped will be exported.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d89a74b5-2056-428f-a770-d437e37c222b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="_mce_tagged_br">For server projects, you must lock the package(s) first to update tag value.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b07d0d4b-15e8-4d4a-a747-c1ae88304ea1"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Failure in Bulk Exporting</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>A failure in multi-package bulk export to OWL now does not cancel exporting all the remaining packages.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="64f635ff-a0b4-4ace-bb79-e57a4a0a06d8"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Canceling Bulk Exporting</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Clicking Cancel for bulk export to OWL operation also cancels the export of all the Packages.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="37aa5287-f47e-44ec-bdd8-e20e63b7ccc2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">154875172</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li class="ancestor-link"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Usage">Usage</a></li><li class="ancestor-link"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Exporting your concept model to an OWL ontology" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478821 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00118: Exporting your concept model

- page_id: `170478821`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478821/Exporting+your+concept+model
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:56.891+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Exporting your concept model to an OWL ontology
- labels: ['concept-model-export']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: XML Namespaces

241471256

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: XML Namespaces

241471259

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: XML Namespaces

241471257

#### INFO: Foreign Namespaces

Foreign Namespaces

When a UML element does not share the same namespace as its owning «Model», it is now defined in the exported OWL.

Classes that are not directly owned by a package now results in an informative warning during export. The warning is similar to the following:

```text
ERROR [com.nomagic.conceptmodeler.ui.i:160] - Unexpected error
```

```text
'com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.activities.mdfundamentalactivities.impl.ActivityImpl
```

```text
cannot be cast to org.semanticweb.owlapi.model.OWLClassExpression' occurred while trying to export
```

#### INFO: Multiple Equivalence Classes

Multiple Equivalence Classes

Exporting a UML Class with multiple «Equivalent Class» relations now results in only one equivalent Class axiom per equivalent Class.

#### INFO: XML Namespaces

XML Namespaces

When exporting a concept model, MCM will now automatically retain and populate the XML Namespace in any case where an XML Namespace exists, even if you are not exporting the main file.

To export a concept model to an OWL ontology

1. Right-click on your desired concept model in the Containment tree. Select Concept Modeling > Export to OWL. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. A dialog box will appear asking whether or not you want to freeze the elements' IRIs. This dialog will show only if there are elements with no IRI tagged value. [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2019-09-23 at 5.22.48 PM.png']
3. 
  1. If you click No , the notification window will show this warning message: [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2019-09-23 at 5.40.02 PM.png']
  2. If you click Yes , the notification window will show you which elements IRIs were frozen. [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2019-09-23 at 5.42.15 PM.png']
4. A progress bar will appear.
5. A dialog will appear asking you to choose the folder in which you want to save the exported model.
6. A Path Variables dialog will appear. The default path variable is <project.dir>\OWL. Choose Use Selected.
7. Your concept model is now exported into the folder of your choice with the file extension of your choice.

If you check the **Remember my selection box**, the above dialog will be suppressed the next time you open your project.

The exported OWL ontology file is saved in a folder named **OWL**. The **OWL** folder is created at the same location as the MagicDraw project file containing the concept model.

You can also suppress or unsuppress the offer to freeze IRIs when exporting your concept model to OWL through the **Options** tab.

To suppress or unsuppress the option to freeze IRIs through the **Options** tab

1. Click Options>Project . [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2019-09-23 at 4.39.26 PM.png']
2. Select General , then Concept Modeling . [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2019-09-23 at 4.42.29 PM.png']
3. The Concept Modeling dialog will open. Select Freeze IRI when exporting OWL. [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2019-09-23 at 4.41.18 PM.png']
4. You will be given three preferred options for freezing IRIs when exporting to OWL from which to choose. a. Selecting Always freeze before export will silently set missing IRI values. b. Selecting Never freeze before export will not set missing IRI values. c. Selecting Ask will prompt you to select for every exporting package that has elements with missing IRI values.

241471255

**Related pages**

- Usage
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exporting your concept model to an OWL ontology' space='MCM2024xR1']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478174 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00120: Facets

- page_id: `170478174`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478174/Facets
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-31T14:20:09.224+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics > Advanced Modeling Patterns
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1468810409

1468810420

1468810410

A concept is often used as a mutually exclusive, fundamental, identity-bearing kind for its members. But there are also other concepts where members can come and go over time without changing their essences. This profile calls this sort of concept a “Facet”. A particular thing can be a member of any number of facets at any time.

This version of the concept modeling profile specification defines only two kinds of facets, but may define more in subsequent versions. The defined facets are currently «Role» and «Phase». They are described in the following subsections, linked in the **Related pages** section below.

1468810408

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']

But there are also other concepts where members can come and go over time without changing their essences. This profile calls this sort of concept a “Facet”. A particular thing can be a member of any number of facets at any time.</p><p>This version of the concept modeling profile specification defines only two kinds of facets, but may define more in subsequent versions. The defined facets are currently «Role» and «Phase». They are described in the following subsections, linked in the <strong>Related pages</strong> section below. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7140e755-fa3a-4026-9f83-96a2e10b116d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1468810408</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="88ffd1d1-0a77-44db-a851-5e517daafe00" /></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478863 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00121: Freezing and unfreezing IRIs

- page_id: `170478863`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478863/Freezing+and+unfreezing+IRIs
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:40:16.258+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The effective, or derived, IRI of a package or an element is dynamically generated from its name. When working on a concept model, an element's IRI will change anytime the name of the class or the package changes. To prevent this from happening, and from the IRI changing on import from and export to OWL, you are given the option to freeze the element's IRI tagged value.

To freeze an IRI

1. Right-click on the element in the Containment tree whose IRI you want to freeze, or you can also right-click on an element in your diagram whose IRI you want to freeze.
2. Select **Concept Modeling>Freeze IRI.** 
**[IMAGE alt='' src='']** 
**The Freeze IRI option from right-clicking on the package in the Containment tree.** 
******** 
**** 
**[IMAGE alt='' src='']** 
**The Freeze IRI option from right-clicking on an element.** 
******[IMAGE alt='' src='']** 
**The Freeze IRI option from right-clicking on an element in the diagram.******

The IRI of the element of which you chose to freeze will show that it has been successfully frozen in the Notification Window, as shown below. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**The Notification Window showing that the elements' IRIs were successfully frozen.**

Freezing the IRI will create a tagged value as well. To view its newly-created tagged value:

1. Right-click on the frozen element in the Containment tree .
2. Select Specification. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Select Tags. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 The newly-created IRI tagged value "TartCherry,Cranberry" is created.

You will want to unfreeze an element's IRI if you want to make any changes to it.

To unfreeze an IRI

1. Right-click on the element in the Containment tree whose IRI you want to unfreeze, or you can also right-click on the element in your diagram whose IRI you want to unfreeze.
2. Select **Concept Modeling>Unfreeze IRI**. 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
The IRI of the element of which you chose to unfreeze will show that it has been successfully unfrozen in the **Notification Window**, as shown below. 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
**The Notification Window showing that the elements' IRIs were successfully unfrozen.** 
**** 
The IRI's tagged value will also be removed, as shown below in the **Specification Window.** 
**[IMAGE alt='' src='']** 
**The Specification Window showing that the IRI's tagged value was removed.****** The option to unfreeze an IRI will only be given if there is already an IRI tagged value.The valid elements of which its IRI can be frozen and unfrozen include Classes, Properties, Datatypes, Association classes, Enumerations, and Instance specifications. In the **Notification Window**, if you click on the element whose IRI is frozen or unfrozen, it will be highlighted in blue in the **Containment tree,**as shown below.[IMAGE alt='' src=''] ****

/></p></li></ol></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target">The IRI of the element of which you chose to freeze will show that it has been successfully frozen in the Notification Window, as shown below.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:width="500"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Screen Shot 2019-10-07 at 2.05.47 PM.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Freezing and unfreezing IRIs" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><strong>The Notification Window showing that the elements' IRIs were successfully frozen.</strong></p><p><br /></p><p>Freezing the IRI will create a tagged value as well. To view its newly-created tagged value:</p><ol><li>Right-click on the frozen element in the <strong>Containment tree</strong>.</li><li>Select <strong>Specification.</strong><br /><strong><ac:image ac:width="400"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Screen Shot 2019-10-07 at 2.40.05 PM_cut.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Freezing and unfreezing IRIs" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></strong></li><li>Select <strong>Tags. </strong><br /><strong><ac:image ac:width="550"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Screen Shot 2019-10-07 at 2.14.07 PM.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Freezing and unfreezing IRIs" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />          The newly-created IRI tagged value &quot;TartCherry,Cranberry&quot; is created.</strong><br /><strong><br /></strong></li></ol></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>You will want to unfreeze an element's IRI if you want to make any changes to it. </p><p>To unfreeze an IRI</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click on the element in the <strong>Containment tree</strong> whose IRI you want to unfreeze, or you can also right-click on the element in your diagram whose IRI you want to unfreeze.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Select <strong>Concept Modeling&gt;Unfreeze IRI</strong>.<br /><ac:image ac:width="550"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Screen Shot 2019-10-07 at 2.27.56 PM_cut.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Freezing and unfreezing IRIs" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br />The IRI of the element of which you chose to unfreeze will show that it has been successfully unfrozen in the <strong>Notification Window</strong>, as shown below.<br /><ac:image ac:width="500"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Screen Shot 2019-10-07 at 2.28.20 PM.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Freezing and unfreezing IRIs" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /><strong>The Notification Window showing that the elements' IRIs were successfully unfrozen.</strong><br /><strong><br /></strong><br />The IRI's tagged value will also be removed, as shown below in the <strong>Specification Window.</strong><br /><strong><ac:image ac:height="400"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Screen Shot 2019-10-07 at 2.18.42 PM.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Freezing and unfreezing IRIs" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></strong><br /><strong>The Specification Window showing that the IRI's tagged value was removed.<br /></strong><strong><br /></strong></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7e14432e-9f9e-44e8-9746-0b7341051b66"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The option to unfreeze an IRI will only be given if there is already an IRI tagged value.</p><p>The valid elements of which its IRI can be frozen and unfrozen include <span style="font-size: 14.0px;letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Classes, Properties, Datatypes, Association classes, Enumerations, and Instance specifications.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e26e85f5-e697-487f-8c8f-5c5a0e32bfde"><ac:rich-text-body><p>In the <strong>Notification Window</strong>, if you click on the element whose IRI is frozen or unfrozen, it will be highlighted in blue in the <strong>Containment tree, </strong>as shown below.</p><p><ac:image ac:width="400"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Screen Shot 2019-10-07 at 2.30.06 PM_cut.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Freezing and unfreezing IRIs" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /><strong><br /></strong></p></li></ol></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478241 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00122: Generalization

- page_id: `170478241`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478241/Generalization
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-31T14:25:49.634+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

74258711

74258714

74258712

A generalization is a subsumption relationship between a more general class and a more specific class. Every instance of the specific class is also an instance of the subsuming general class. Because of this subsumption relationship, the specific class inherits all of the necessary conditions of the more general classifier.

For a simple example, if we define “Futsal Team” as a subclass of “Soccer Team”, then the set of individuals in “Futsal Team” must be a subset of the set of individuals in “Soccer Team”.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The relation between subclass 'Futsal Team' and class 'Soccer Team' represents generalization.

There are four variations on generalization described in the following subsections. The first variation corresponds to the example above: overlapping and incomplete subclasses. That variation is the default in both UML and concept modeling.

74258709

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']

Every instance of the specific class is also an instance of the subsuming general class. Because of this subsumption relationship, the specific class inherits all of the necessary conditions of the more general classifier.</p><p>For a simple example, if we define “Futsal Team” as a subclass of “Soccer Team”, then the set of individuals in “Futsal Team” must be a subset of the set of individuals in “Soccer Team”. </p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-31 10:38:36.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Generalization" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The relation between subclass 'Futsal Team' and class 'Soccer Team' represents generalization.</h6><p>There are four variations on generalization described in the following subsections. The first variation corresponds to the example above: overlapping and incomplete subclasses. That variation is the default in both UML and concept modeling.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="734ab7ae-c3f1-44d7-8d3e-f1c02ac99689"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">74258709</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="a2afe237-ea79-411c-8d8c-0b9f667c5f31" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478701 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00123: Generalization Set Names

- page_id: `170478701`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478701/Generalization+Set+Names
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-31T14:24:39.112+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Existing Project Migration
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1283615883

1283615885

1283615884

When you open a project which was created in an earlier version of MCM, you now have the option to retain **Generalization Set Names,**or remove them for a cleaner, less-cluttered look.

To remove generalization set names

1. Open your desired project. The generalization set name, in this case "(set 1)", will be visible, as shown on the diagram below. [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2019-09-21 at 3.58.22 PM.png'] Project with visible generalization set name "set 1".
2. The Remove generalization set names dialog will also appear. [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2019-09-21 at 3.12.33 PM.png'] The Remove generalization set names dialog.
3. Select Yes to remove all generalization set names, and your diagram will appear with the set name removed, as shown in the diagram below. [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2019-09-21 at 4.02.20 PM.png'] Project with generalization set name "set 1" removed.
4. The Notification Window will show that the generalization set name has been removed, and will ask you to save the changes. [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2019-09-21 at 4.13.11 PM.png']
5. Click File>Save Project to save the changes.

To retain generalization set names

1. Open your desired project. The generalization set name, in this case "(set 1)", will be visible, as shown on the diagram below. [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2019-09-21 at 3.58.22 PM.png'] Diagram showing generalization name set "set 1".
2. The **Remove generalization set names** dialog will also pop up. [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2019-09-21 at 3.12.33 PM.png'] The Remove generalization set names dialog.
3. Select **No**to retain generalization set names, and update them at a later time. The diagram will remain the same, and the **Notification Window** will show the following warning: 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] Selecting the **Do Not Show Again** check box in the **Remove generalization set names** dialog, will suppress the dialog the next time you open your project.

You also have the option to suppress the **Remove the Generalization set names** dialog, so that it does not ask you every time you open a project if you would like to remove or retain the generalization set name.

To suppress the **Remove generalization set name** dialog

1. Click Options>Project . [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2019-09-17 at 5.41.44 PM.png']
2. Select Genera l, then Concept Modeling . [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2019-09-17 at 5.43.31 PM.png']
3. In the Project Options dialog, set the Always ask to remove generalization set names option to False .

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Project Options dialog showing the "Always ask to remove generalization set names" option.**

1283615882

**Related Pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Usage' space='MCM2024xR1']

## PAGE 00124: Generalization with disjoint subclasses

- page_id: `170478369`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478369/Generalization+with+disjoint+subclasses
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:56.319+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1286268782

1286268784

1286268783

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Generalization with disjoint subclasses.

```text
Declaration(
               Class(:Animal)
        )
        Declaration(
               Class(:Cat)
        )
        Declaration(
               Class(:Dog)
        )
        Declaration(
               Class(:Mouse)
        )
		SubClassOf(:Cat :Animal)
		SubClassOf(:Dog :Animal)
		SubClassOf(:Mouse :Animal)
		DisjointClasses(:Cat :Dog)
		DisjointClasses(:Cat :Mouse)
		DisjointClasses(:Dog :Mouse)
		AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Animal "Animal"@en)
		AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Cat "Cat"@en)
		AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Dog "Dog"@en)
		AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Mouse "Mouse"@en)
)]]>
```

1286268781

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax' space='MCM2024xR1']

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5d4132e5-c88e-43e7-96f1-1fa6ab03e341"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1286268782</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ed23a1a9-6e15-4c46-b890-38d9221021b7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1286268784</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5ab0df18-d229-471b-8242-0091c312d104"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1286268783</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-23 14:4:0.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Generalization with disjoint subclasses" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Generalization with disjoint subclasses.<span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);font-size: 14.0px;"> </span></h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="567685a7-f7d1-4656-b481-fdbd28347ac5"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Ontology(<http://nomagic.com/ontology/example-case/case-24>
		Declaration(
               Class(:Animal)
        )
        Declaration(
               Class(:Cat)
        )
        Declaration(
               Class(:Dog)
        )
        Declaration(
               Class(:Mouse)
        )
		SubClassOf(:Cat :Animal)
		SubClassOf(:Dog :Animal)
		SubClassOf(:Mouse :Animal)
		DisjointClasses(:Cat :Dog)
		DisjointClasses(:Cat :Mouse)
		DisjointClasses(:Dog :Mouse)
		AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Animal "Animal"@en)
		AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Cat "Cat"@en)
		AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Dog "Dog"@en)
		AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Mouse "Mouse"@en)
)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c365c00c-ee15-4cb6-a8c7-c671ed204b0b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1286268781</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478371 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00125: Generalization with subclass completeness

- page_id: `170478371`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478371/Generalization+with+subclass+completeness
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:56.474+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1286371466

1286371469

1286371467

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Generalization with complete subclasses.

```text
Declaration(
             Class(:CarManufacturer)
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:Manufacturer)
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:SteeringWheelManufacturer
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:WindshieldManufacturer)
      )
	  SubClassOf(:CarManufacturer
	  :Manufacturer)
	  SubClassOf(:SteeringWheelManufacturer
	  :Manufacturer)
	  SubClassOf(:WindshieldManufacturer
	  :Manufacturer)
	  EquivalentClasses(:Manufacturer
	  ObjectUnionOf(:CarManufacturer :SteeringWheelManufacturer
	  :WindshieldManufacturer))
	  AnnotationAsserton(rdfs:label
	  :CarManufacturer "Car Manufacturer"@en)
	  AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label
	  :Manufacturer "Manufacturer"@en)
	  AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label
	  :SteeringWheelManufacturer "Steering Wheel Manufacturer"@en)
	  AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label
	  :WindshieldManufacturer "Windshield Manufacturer"@en)
)]]>
```

1286371465

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax' space='MCM2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a0c54b1b-09f2-44f4-9e24-8c92a70b518c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1286371466</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6cf7d9e6-9d76-4f06-87d3-26c3aa978db4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1286371469</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9ac9622f-ade7-4b94-a339-ef03ca4f5012"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1286371467</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-23 14:14:44.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Generalization with subclass completeness" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Generalization with complete subclasses.</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="88611e7a-82bb-4333-841f-1c4a97e3dd2a"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Ontology(<http://nomagic.com/ontology/example-case/case-23>
      Declaration(
             Class(:CarManufacturer)
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:Manufacturer)
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:SteeringWheelManufacturer
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:WindshieldManufacturer)
      )
	  SubClassOf(:CarManufacturer
	  :Manufacturer)
	  SubClassOf(:SteeringWheelManufacturer
	  :Manufacturer)
	  SubClassOf(:WindshieldManufacturer
	  :Manufacturer)
	  EquivalentClasses(:Manufacturer
	  ObjectUnionOf(:CarManufacturer :SteeringWheelManufacturer
	  :WindshieldManufacturer))
	  AnnotationAsserton(rdfs:label
	  :CarManufacturer "Car Manufacturer"@en)
	  AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label
	  :Manufacturer "Manufacturer"@en)
	  AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label
	  :SteeringWheelManufacturer "Steering Wheel Manufacturer"@en)
	  AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label
	  :WindshieldManufacturer "Windshield Manufacturer"@en)
)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0b1271a2-87ba-418a-8a6c-fa8dfc876cbb"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1286371465</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page<br /></strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478893 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00126: Generalizations with read-only subclasses

- page_id: `170478893`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478893/Generalizations+with+read-only+subclasses
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:40:16.952+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To create a generalization with a read-only subclass

1. Select an element from your model, and drag it into the diagram pane.
2. Select a read-only element from a model.
3. The notification window displays a WARN message as you create a subclass generalization from the read-only element to your element. The WARN message is shown below. [ATTACHMENT filename='g2.PNG']
4. The Select Owner dialog opens for you to choose the owner of the newly created generalization. [ATTACHMENT filename='g1.PNG']
5. Click OK .

The final result should look similar to the following:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To create a generalization with a read-only subclass</p><hr /><ol><li>Select an element from your model, and drag it into the diagram pane.</li><li>Select a read-only element from a model.</li><li>The notification window displays a <strong>WARN</strong> message as you create a subclass generalization from the read-only element to your element. The <strong>WARN</strong> message is shown below.<br /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="25"><ri:attachment ri:filename="g2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Generalizations with read-only subclasses" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>The <strong>Select Owner</strong> dialog opens for you to choose the owner of the newly created generalization. <br /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="600"><ri:attachment ri:filename="g1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Generalizations with read-only subclasses" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>. </li></ol><p>The final result should look similar to the following:</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="51"><ri:attachment ri:filename="g6.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Generalizations with read-only subclasses" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170479149 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00127: Generating a natural language glossary

- page_id: `170479149`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170479149/Generating+a+natural+language+glossary
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:59.966+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Working with the Natural Language Glossary
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1277566472

1277566475

1277566474

You can use the [Report Wizard](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Report+Wizard) to generate a natural language glossary for your model. You can refer to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Report Wizard dialog' space='MD2024xR1'] to learn about each button's functionality.

To generate a Natural Language Glossary

1. Select Tools > Report Wizard from the main menu.
2. Expand the Concept Modeler folder.
3. Select Natural Language Glossary . [ATTACHMENT filename='reportwizard.PNG']
4. Click the Next button.
5. Select Built-in . [ATTACHMENT filename='default.PNG']
6. Click the Next button.
7. Select the package(s) you want to generate a natural language glossary for.
8. Click the Add button.
9. Click the Next button. [ATTACHMENT filename='addexampleccm.png']
10. Name your file and file location for your file.
11. Click the **Generate** button. The Natural Language Glossary will be generated. [ATTACHMENT filename='reportwizard1.png']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**A sample of the Natural Language Glossary generated with class and property definitions.**

1277769734

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Usage' space='MCM2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with the Natural Language Glossary' space='MCM2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customizing your Natural Language Glossary' space='MCM2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The Natural Language Glossary will be generated.</p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="600"><ri:attachment ri:filename="reportwizard1.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Generating a natural language glossary" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /><br /></li></ol><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="700"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Property Definitions in Natural Language Glossary.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Generating a natural language glossary" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="text-align: center;"><strong>A sample of the Natural Language Glossary generated with class and property definitions.</strong></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="96b77af6-87f0-43a4-a07d-e6f1b48aea41"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1277769734</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Usage" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Working with the Natural Language Glossary" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Working with the natural language glossary]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Customizing your Natural Language Glossary" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Customizing your natural language glossary]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478194 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00128: Global properties

- page_id: `170478194`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478194/Global+properties
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:49.163+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

73676136

73676138

73676137

Global properties are property declarations that can be used by any instance. Normally, a UML property cannot be defined outside of a classifier, so a global property declaration is represented as a UML property owned by a class that is stereotyped as a **«**Anything**»**.****The concept of a property holder was introduced in the NIEM-UML standard for a similar purpose. In the concept modeling profile, every property holder is equivalent to one topmost class (⊤) of which all other classes are subclasses. Thus, a property of a property holder is “inherited by” all subclasses and usable in any instances. In addition, while the name of a property holder is irrelevant, consistently naming property holders “Thing”, “Concept”, or “Entity” in all concept models avoids any confusion with normal classes.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A property holder in Concept Modeler.

73676135

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="95044c40-a60c-42d5-a6b4-45e031e1999e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">73676136</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fdd3f443-fb2e-48a8-b01d-54d230a41da7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">73676138</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4f27d063-56c4-48ec-b28e-5370a5de2559"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">73676137</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Global properties are property declarations that can be used by any instance. Normally, a UML property cannot be defined outside of a classifier, so a global property declaration is represented as a UML property owned by a class that is stereotyped as a <strong>«</strong>Anything<strong>»</strong>.<strong> </strong>The concept of a property holder was introduced in the NIEM-UML standard for a similar purpose. In the concept modeling profile, every property holder is equivalent to one topmost class (⊤) of which all other classes are subclasses. Thus, a property of a property holder is “inherited by” all subclasses and usable in any instances. In addition, while the name of a property holder is irrelevant, consistently naming property holders “Thing”, “Concept”, or “Entity” in all concept models avoids any confusion with normal classes.</p><p> </p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-23 11:34:58.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Global properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A property holder in Concept Modeler.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e95627c3-639f-43aa-85a4-0efac1c6ddb3"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">73676135</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478604 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00129: How to change the color of AutoStyler

- page_id: `170478604`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478604/How+to+change+the+color+of+AutoStyler
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:40:07.627+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > AutoStyler Documentation > Customizing AutoStyler
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This page shows you how to change the default fill-in color of AutoStyler. Please note that you can only change the color of classes that have already had the functions of**[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='AutoStyler Documentation' space='MCM2024xR1']** applied to it. When you are done customizing the AutoStyler color make sure to follow the steps described in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customizing AutoStyler' space='MCM2024xR1']**To automatically apply customizations** section. Color differentiation can be really important for highlighting different points as some colors can point to different ideas and thoughts than others.

How to change the fill-in color of AutoStyler

1. Select **Options** > **Project.**

2. Select **Defined Elsewhere** and click on the little plus sign to the left.

3. Find **Shapes** and click on the little plus sign to the left.

4. Look for a folder named **Class** and click on it.

5. At the very top of **Class** left click on the Property Value of **Fill Color** to where it reveals the **three dots** to the right.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Steps 1-5

6. Click on those **three dots** and select a color from the color table.

7. After you have selected a color click **OK.**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Color Table

You can apply a more specific color if you know the HSV, HSL, RGB, or CMYK values to enter into the color table above.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>This page shows you how to change the default fill-in color of AutoStyler. Please note that you can only change the color of classes that have already had the functions of<strong> <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="AutoStyler Documentation" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[AutoStyler]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></strong> applied to it. When you are done customizing the AutoStyler color make sure to follow the steps described in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Customizing AutoStyler" /><ac:link-body><strong>To automatically apply customizations</strong></ac:link-body></ac:link> section. Color differentiation can be really important for highlighting different points as some colors can point to different ideas and thoughts than others.</p><p><br /></p><p>How to change the fill-in color of AutoStyler</p><hr /><p>        <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">1. Select <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Project.</strong></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">        2. Select <strong>Defined Elsewhere</strong> and click on the little plus sign to the left.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">        3. Find <strong>Shapes</strong> and click on the little plus sign to the left.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">        4. Look for a folder named <strong>Class</strong> and click on it.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">        5. At the very top of <strong>Class</strong> left click on the Property Value of <strong>Fill Color</strong> to where it reveals the <strong>three dots</strong> to the right.</span></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="800"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DefinedElsewhereToShapesToClassesToFillColorToThreeDots.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="How to change the color of AutoStyler" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h4 style="text-align: center;">Steps 1-5</h4><p>        6. Click on those <strong>three dots</strong> and select a color from the color table.</p><p>        7. After you have selected a color click <strong>OK.</strong></p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="600"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ColorTable.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="How to change the color of AutoStyler" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h4 style="text-align: center;">Color Table</h4><p>You can apply a more specific color if you know the HSV, HSL, RGB, or CMYK values to enter into the color table above.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478607 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00130: How to remove package names of classes

- page_id: `170478607`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478607/How+to+remove+package+names+of+classes
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:40:07.766+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > AutoStyler Documentation > Customizing AutoStyler
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Sometimes it is easier to avoid the clutter of package names especially if you are dealing with long package names that could potentially block or obscure the class name or other necessary information. This page focuses on removing those package names to avoid that potential problem.

How to remove the package names for faded-out classes in **AutoStyler**

1. Click on **Options** > **Project.**

2. Click on**Defined Elsewhere,**as shown below. Here you can customize**AutoStyler**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

3. Select **Shapes** > **Class** > **Property Value** > **Show Owner** > **Do Not Display** > **OK** to apply the changes. This will get rid of the package names underneath the class names.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

4. Here are the classes with the package names before customizing **AutoStyler**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Package names before being removed

5. Here are what the classes look like after removing the package names by customizing **AutoStyler**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Package names after being removed

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Sometimes it is easier to avoid the clutter of package names especially if you are dealing with long package names that could potentially block or obscure the class name or other necessary information. This page focuses on removing those package names to avoid that potential problem.</p><p><br /></p><p>How to remove the package names for faded-out classes in <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">AutoStyler</strong></p><hr /><p>        1. Click on <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Project.</strong></p><p>        2. Click on<strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> Defined Elsewhere, </strong>as shown below<span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">. Here you can customize </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">AutoStyler</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">.</span></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:border="true" ac:height="250" ac:width="393"><ri:attachment ri:filename="DefinedElsewhere.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="How to remove package names of classes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>        3. Select <strong>Shapes</strong> &gt; <strong>Class</strong> &gt; <strong>Property Value</strong> &gt; <strong>Show Owner</strong> &gt; <strong>Do Not Display</strong> &gt; <strong>OK</strong> to apply the changes. This will get rid of the package names underneath the class names.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:border="true" ac:height="250" ac:width="321"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ChangingFeaturesInAutoStylerJurisdictionDeletion.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="How to remove package names of classes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>         4. Here are the classes with the package names before customizing <strong>AutoStyler</strong>.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:border="true" ac:height="145" ac:width="750"><ri:attachment ri:filename="PackageNamesNotRemoved.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="How to remove package names of classes" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h4 style="text-align: center;">Package names before being removed</h4><p>        5. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170479134 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
````
## PAGE 00131: Importing an ontology that defines annotation properties

- page_id: `170479134`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170479134/Importing+an+ontology+that+defines+annotation+properties
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-31T14:23:49.593+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Working with annotations > Working with annotation properties
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1048722637

1048722639

1048722638

To import an ontology into an existing concept model

1. Select **File** > **Import From** > **OWL Ontology File**. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] **Importing an OWL ontology file to the Concept Modeler.**
2. Browse and select your OWL Ontology file.
3. Annotation properties imported from your OWL ontology will be displayed in the Containment tree under Imported Ontologies as shown in the following figure.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**The imported Annotation Property is highlighted in the Containment tree.**

1209428797

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Usage' space='MCM2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="68b70b84-5592-4e61-9f41-3f203b28a4e6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1048722637</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="22a34155-b0b5-4906-8f1b-c66150f03968"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1048722639</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5c5e1bcd-e452-47aa-bfcf-3cc3a7e112a1"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1048722638</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>To import an ontology into an existing concept model</p><hr /><ol><li style="text-align: left;"><p>Select <strong>File</strong> &gt; <strong>Import From</strong> &gt; <strong>OWL Ontology File</strong>.</p><h6><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-30 15:7:9.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Importing an ontology that defines annotation properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><p style="text-align: center;"><strong><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">Importing an OWL ontology file to the Concept Modeler.</span></strong></p></li><li>Browse and select your OWL Ontology file.</li><li>Annotation properties imported from your OWL ontology will be displayed in the <strong>Containment tree</strong> under <strong>Imported Ontologies</strong> as shown in the following figure.</li></ol><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-30 15:7:55.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Importing an ontology that defines annotation properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;"><strong>The imported Annotation Property is highlighted in the Containment tree.</strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f9761cf4-46d9-4200-a7c6-22191deb5228"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1209428797</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Usage" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478781 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00132: Importing an OWL ontology file

- page_id: `170478781`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478781/Importing+an+OWL+ontology+file
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:56.171+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Importing an OWL ontology to a concept model
- labels: ['owl-ontology']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: XML Namespaces

240792819

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: XML Namespaces

240792821

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: XML Namespaces

240792820

You can import an OWL ontology file (after setting the OWL import catalog, if necessary), and reuse or augment it in the Concept Modeler.

To import an OWL ontology into a concept model (after [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Setting the OWL import catalog in MagicDraw' space='MCM2024xR1'] if necessary)

1. On the main menu, click **File** > **Import From**.Select**OWL Ontology File**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Browse for an ontology file. [ATTACHMENT filename='choosing_ontology_file.png']
3. Click Open . The ontology file will be imported into the MagicDraw project.

#### INFO: Path Variables

Path Variables

If the OWL import catalog is set using a path variable (as described in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using a path variable to share OWL import catalog files' space='MCM2024xR1']), and this path variable is not defined (as described in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a path variable to share OWL import catalog files' space='MCM2024xR1']), the Concept Modeler will not be able to locate it. Consequently, the following dialog box will be displayed.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**A dialog prompting you to specify a path variable.**

Clicking **Yes**allows you to set the path variable to a root directory containing OWL import catalog files and the import of the OWL ontology to proceed.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Setting the path variable to the OWL import catalog files.**

When an OWL file has been successfully imported, an **Imported Ontologies**package will appear in the Containment tree containing the imported OWL data.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**The imported ontology package appears in the Containment tree.**

#### INFO: Anonymous Ontology

Anonymous Ontology

The modeling tool detects anonymous ontologies and shows you an informative message when you import an anonymous ontology. The message is shown below.

Also, the notification window will show you the follow message. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### INFO: XML Namespaces

XML Namespaces

When importing an ontology, MCM will now automatically populate the XML Namespace in any case where an XML Namespace exists, even if you are not importing the main file.

240800348

- Usage
- Importing an OWL ontology to a concept model
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using a path variable to share OWL import catalog files' space='MCM2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Importing annotations on an OWL ontology to a concept model' space='MCM2024xR1']

## PAGE 00133: Importing an OWL ontology into a non-MCM project

- page_id: `170478722`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478722/Importing+an+OWL+ontology+into+a+non-MCM+project
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-31T14:09:06.105+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Working with non-MCM projects
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: MCM 'Default' Symbol Style

343304420

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: MCM 'Default' Symbol Style

343304431

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: MCM 'Default' Symbol Style

343304419

GreenAvailable From 18.0 SP13

If you are working in a non-MCM project, and you want to import an OWL ontology file (File > Import From > OWL Ontology File), a message dialog appears. The dialog will inform you that the Concept Modeling Profile is required for import, and it will ask you whether or not you want to load it.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Message dialog asking if you want to load the Concept Modeling Profile.

If you click **Yes,** the modeling tool will load the profile.

#### NOTE: MCM 'Default' Symbol Style

MCM 'Default' Symbol Style

Magic Concept Modeler comes with its own Default Styles. This message dialog may appear after the Question dialog shown previously. The dialog simply asks whether you would like to load MCM default styles. Note the Warning message in the dialog. The message is there for projects that are really specific and may contain nomenclature that MCM does not recognize.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Click **Yes** or **No**, according to your needs.

If you click **No,** the following ERROR message appears in the Notification Window.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

1283793586

**Related Pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Usage' space='MCM2024xR1']

## PAGE 00134: Importing an OWL ontology to a concept model

- page_id: `170478750`

238385985

238385984

This section provides a simple, step-by-step guide to get you started on creating a concept model from an OWL ontology. This section is divided into several subsections with clear steps for you to follow.

Those subsections are:

- Updating the XML catalog file
- Setting the OWL import catalog in MagicDraw
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a path variable to share OWL import catalog files' space='MCM2024xR1']
- Using a path variable to share OWL import catalog files
- Importing an OWL ontology file
- Displaying and hiding IRI

238385981

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Usage' space='MCM2024xR1']

This section is divided into several subsections with clear steps for you to follow.</p><p>Those subsections are:</p><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Updating+the+XML+catalog+file">Updating the XML catalog file</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Setting+the+OWL+import+catalog+in+MagicDraw">Setting the OWL import catalog in MagicDraw</a></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Creating a path variable to share OWL import catalog files" /></ac:link></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Using+a+path+variable+to+share+OWL+import+catalog+files">Using a path variable to share OWL import catalog files</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Importing+an+OWL+ontology+file">Importing an OWL ontology file</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Displaying+and+hiding+IRI">Displaying and hiding IRI</a></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0c0f7a76-5f4c-4245-a944-c249f0d2a600"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">238385981</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Usage" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478271 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00135: Importing OWL

- page_id: `170478271`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478271/Importing+OWL
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:51.714+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1610352359

1610352370

1610352360

This section is dedicated to explaining exactly what the modeling tool imports.

**Complex, nested class expressions**

Importing or exporting complex, nested class expressions involving unions, intersections, complements, and restrictions no longer results in a loss of fidelity.

**Constructs**

The Concept Modeler supports importing the following constructs: Class, Datatype, Datatype Property, Object Property, Object Property Chains, Class Generalization, Class Equivalence, Class Disjoint Relation, Annotation Property, Object Property Generalization, Object Property Equivalence, Datatype Property Generalization, Class Restriction, Named Individual, Named Individual Property, and Annotations.

**Superclass Intersections**

OWL Classes that have no superclasses and are equivalent to intersections of Classes will be stereotyped as «Superclass Intersection». More about the stereotype can be found here: [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with superclass intersection' space='MCM2024xR1'].

If you would like to learn about the applications of Superclass Intersections, please peruse [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with intersection' space='MCM2024xR1'] and Intersection.

**Property Restrictions**

The Concept Modeler will import each property restriction as a unidirectional association between the two concepts.

**Aristotelian Definitions**

The Concept Modeler supports importing Aristotelian definitions. More information about this is found in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Conditions' space='MCM2024xR1'] page.

**IRI/URI**

When you import an OWL ontology file, the Concept Modeler **preserves** the URI/IRI for every OWL class and property and imports it as a tagged value of the corresponding UML class or property. The tagged value, called IRI, is part of a «Resource» stereotype applied to each UML element.

Concept Modeler supports importing classes, properties, and packages that have the same label by assigning each of them a different URI.

**Round-tripping**

Concept Modeler imports OWL ontology as UML package stereotyped as «Concept Modeler». The modeling tool allows you to edit classes and properties and their relations, and export it back to an OWL ontology. This round-trip (Import then Export) preserves the class and property IRIs. The imported OWL ontology updates any corresponding UML elements instead of deleting and adding entire corresponding UML models.

**Annotations**

An OWL ontology may have one or more annotations added to itself. Concept Modeler can import the annotations as annotations on a concept model. If there is only one annotation on the OWL ontology, it will show u p in the concept model’s Documentation pane in MagicDraw upon import. When there are more than one annotations imported, they will not show up automatically in the Documentation pane. You need to specify which one gets to be displayed as the package’s default documentation by selecting it from the Preferred annotation property option in the Project Options dialog. The rest of the annotations will become the package owned comments (UML comments tagged as Annotation).

1610352358

**Related Pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8dccd708-a6f0-46bd-bd79-d0b4ad015dd6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1610352359</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="848d2e63-9d3d-43a8-9b3f-64642c8df5cb"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1610352370</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="191caf45-06a5-4e67-a8d3-bc121ddc36fa"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1610352360</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>This section is dedicated to explaining exactly what the modeling tool imports.</p><p><strong>Complex, nested class expressions</strong></p><p>Importing or exporting complex, nested class expressions involving unions, intersections, complements, and restrictions no longer results in a loss of fidelity.</p><p><strong>Constructs</strong></p><p>The Concept Modeler supports importing the following constructs: Class, Datatype, Datatype Property, Object Property, Object Property Chains, Class Generalization, Class Equivalence, Class Disjoint Relation, Annotation Property, Object Property Generalization, Object Property Equivalence, Datatype Property Generalization, Class Restriction, Named Individual, Named Individual Property, and Annotations.</p><p><strong>Superclass Intersections</strong></p><p>OWL Classes that have no superclasses and are equivalent to intersections of Classes will be stereotyped as «Superclass Intersection». More about the stereotype can be found here: <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Working with superclass intersection" /></ac:link>.</p><p>If you would like to learn about the applications of Superclass Intersections, please peruse <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Working with intersection" /></ac:link> and Intersection.</p><p><strong>Property Restrictions</strong></p><p>The Concept Modeler will import each property restriction as a unidirectional association between the two concepts.</p><p><strong>Aristotelian Definitions</strong></p><p>The Concept Modeler supports importing Aristotelian definitions. More information about this is found in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Conditions" /></ac:link> page.</p><p><strong>IRI/URI</strong></p><p>When you import an OWL ontology file, the Concept Modeler <strong>preserves</strong> the URI/IRI for every OWL class and property and imports it as a tagged value of the corresponding UML class or property. The tagged value, called IRI, is part of a «Resource» stereotype applied to each UML element.</p><p>Concept Modeler supports importing classes, properties, and packages that have the same label by assigning each of them a different URI.</p><p><strong>Round-tripping</strong></p><p>Concept Modeler imports OWL ontology as UML package stereotyped as «Concept Modeler». The modeling tool allows you to edit classes and properties and their relations, and export it back to an OWL ontology. This round-trip (Import then Export) preserves the class and property IRIs. The imported OWL ontology updates any corresponding UML elements instead of deleting and adding entire corresponding UML models.</p><p class="with-breadcrumbs"><strong>Annotations</strong></p><p>An OWL ontology may have one or more annotations added to itself. Concept Modeler can import the annotations as annotations on a concept model. If there is only one annotation on the OWL ontology, it will show u p in the concept model’s Documentation pane in MagicDraw upon import. When there are more than one annotations imported, they will not show up automatically in the Documentation pane. You need to specify which one gets to be displayed as the package’s default documentation by selecting it from the Preferred annotation property option in the Project Options dialog. The rest of the annotations will become the package owned comments (UML comments tagged as Annotation).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="09e4ec64-29a8-4938-9181-b5d36e796241"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1610352358</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170479167 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00136: Including property definitions in the Natural Language Glossary

- page_id: `170479167`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170479167/Including+property+definitions+in+the+Natural+Language+Glossary
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:40:28.884+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Working with the Natural Language Glossary > Customizing your Natural Language Glossary
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1277624442

1277624445

1277624443

Concept Modeler can also display property definitions, in addition to class definitions, in the Natural Language Glossary through the **Include property definitions in the Natural Language Glossary** option in the **Project Options** dialog.

To enable the **Include property definitions in the Natural Language Glossary** option

1. From the main menu, select Options > Project . The Project Options dialog opens.
2. In the tree structure on the left, expand General and select Concept Modeling .
3. At the Include property definitions in the Natural Language Glossary option, select the check box to set it true . [ATTACHMENT filename='Property Definitions in Project Options Dialog.png']
4. Click OK .

After you generate a report from the project that you have set the **Include property definitions in the Natural Language Glossary** option, the property definitions are listed in the generated Natural Language Glossary report, apart from Class definitions.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The property definitions are displayed when the 'Include property definitions' in the Natural Language Glossary option is set.

Otherwise, the Natural Language Glossary will look like the following figure. Notice the lack of the "*Properties:*"

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The property definitions are not displayed when the 'Include property definitions' in the Natural Language Glossary option is not set.

1277759819

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Usage' space='MCM2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with the Natural Language Glossary' space='MCM2024xR1']

The<strong> Project Options</strong> dialog opens.<strong><br /></strong></li><li>In the tree structure on the left, expand <strong>General</strong> and select <strong>Concept Modeling</strong>.</li><li>At the <strong>Include property definitions in the Natural Language Glossary</strong> option, select the check box to set it <strong>true</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="600"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Property Definitions in Project Options Dialog.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Including property definitions in the Natural Language Glossary" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>After you generate a report from the project that you have set the <strong>Include property definitions in the Natural Language Glossary</strong> option, the property definitions are listed in the generated Natural Language Glossary report, apart from Class definitions.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="700"><ri:attachment ri:filename="WithProps.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Including property definitions in the Natural Language Glossary" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The property definitions are displayed when the 'Include property definitions' in the Natural Language Glossary option is set.</h6><p>Otherwise, the Natural Language Glossary will look like the following figure. Notice the lack of the &quot;<em>Properties:</em>&quot;</p><p> <ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="700"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NoProperties Defined.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Including property definitions in the Natural Language Glossary" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">The property definitions are not displayed when the 'Include property definitions' in the Natural Language Glossary option is not set.</span></h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="50abeedd-9c74-4895-ab5b-f91ea984f000"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1277759819</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Usage" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Working with the Natural Language Glossary" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Working with the natural language glossary]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Customizing your Natural Language Glossary" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Customizing your natural language glossary]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478243 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00137: Incomplete and overlapping subclasses

- page_id: `170478243`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478243/Incomplete+and+overlapping+subclasses
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:54.091+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics > Generalization
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

74371715

74371717

74371716

This variation is the default in both UML and in concept modeling. In this variation, an instance can be a member of the superclass and / or any number of subclasses. In this sense, the classification of instances is “incomplete”—sometimes there is a specific subclass, and sometimes there is not.

For example, the diagram below shows four instances. One is an instance of “Manufacturer”, one is an instance of “Windshield Manufacturer”, one is an instance of “Car Manufacturer”, and one is an instance of both “Windshield Manufacturer” and “Car Manufacturer”.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of incomplete instances.

In both standard UML and in concept modeling, incomplete and overlapping subclasses are shown with either no notation, or with the notation {incomplete, overlapping} near the generalization arrow.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Incomplete and overlapping subclasses in standard UML notation.

74371714

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']

- Disjoint and complete subclasses
- [Disjoint subclasses](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Disjoint+subclasses)

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478148 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00138: Information modeling purpose

- page_id: `170478148`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478148/Information+modeling+purpose
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:53.773+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Introduction
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

699845542

699845544

699845543

An information model describes what information is stored in a system, and is independent of any particular implementation of data management structure or technology. One way to remember the difference between a concept model and an information model is that while a personnel record can represent a person, a personnel record does not go to jail when a person is found guilty of wrongdoing. An information model has a different purpose than a concept model. An information model is designed to meet a set of requirements for the information in a system. However, a concept model can provide an excellent starting point for an information model. The elements of a concept model that would fulfill a system’s requirements can be cherry-picked or subsetted to create an information model in UML. Doing so can retain the traceability from elements in an information model to their definitions in a more precise language than plain English.

699845541

**Related pages**

- MDA
- Concept modeling purpose
- The role of ontologies and reasoners
- Open-world assumption vs. closed-world assumption

One way to remember the difference between a concept model and an information model is that while a personnel record can represent a person, a personnel record does not go to jail when a person is found guilty of wrongdoing. An information model has a different purpose than a concept model. An information model is designed to meet a set of requirements for the information in a system. However, a concept model can provide an excellent starting point for an information model. The elements of a concept model that would fulfill a system’s requirements can be cherry-picked or subsetted to create an information model in UML. Doing so can retain the traceability from elements in an information model to their definitions in a more precise language than plain English. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0eb5d3a4-65dc-4f37-ac96-4b67ea5a2c0a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">699845541</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul class="childpages-macro"><li class="current"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/MDA">MDA</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Concept+modeling+purpose">Concept modeling purpose</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/The+role+of+ontologies+and+reasoners">The role of ontologies and reasoners</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Open-world+assumption+vs.+closed-world+assumption">Open-world assumption vs. closed-world assumption</a></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
