<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478144 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00171: MDA

- page_id: `170478144`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478144/MDA
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:53.496+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Introduction
- labels: ['model-driven-architecture', 'mda']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

695821941

695821943

695821942

The Model Driven Architecture (MDA) approach as defined by the Object Management Group (OMG) "provides an approach for deriving value from models and architecture in support of the full life cycle of physical, organizational and I.T. systems."

695821940

**Related pages**

- Concept modeling purpose
- The role of ontologies and reasoners
- Open-world assumption vs. closed-world assumption
- Information modeling purpose

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="72d39cde-895e-409c-b4a1-8376bd377e3f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">695821941</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c21c7a5d-a99a-4580-a765-cf60aceafc81"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">695821943</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8b723acf-dd59-449a-87c4-993bafea96d9"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">695821942</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The Model Driven Architecture (MDA) approach as defined by the Object Management Group (OMG) &quot;provides an approach for deriving value from models and architecture in support of the full life cycle of physical, organizational and I.T. systems.&quot;</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3f81999a-b7d3-4523-90d5-31113588c0f3"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">695821940</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul class="childpages-macro"><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Concept+modeling+purpose">Concept modeling purpose</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/The+role+of+ontologies+and+reasoners">The role of ontologies and reasoners</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Open-world+assumption+vs.+closed-world+assumption">Open-world assumption vs. closed-world assumption</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Information+modeling+purpose">Information modeling purpose</a></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478677 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00172: Migrate Older Models to Use Relative IRIs

- page_id: `170478677`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478677/Migrate+Older+Models+to+Use+Relative+IRIs
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:55.552+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Existing Project Migration
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

GreenAvailable From 19.0

Migrating older models to use relative IRIs improves model refactoring. This can be done using the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Macro Engine Documentation' space='MD2024xR1']. The modeling tool comes with the macro file installed in the tool's directory. Instructions on how to find the file are described in **step #4** of how to add the macro.

To add the macro

1. In your Concept Modeling Project, click **Tools** > **Macros** > **Organize Macros...,**or use the shortcut key **Ctrl+Alt+M.** [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. The **Organize Macros** dialog opens. Click **Add**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. The **Macro Information** dialog opens. Fill out the **Name, Macro Language,** and **File** containing the macro's description. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. To find the appropriate file, click on the three dots at the end of **File** field. Your local directory opens. Find the application folder, and select **data** > **Concept Modeler** > **scripts** > **UpdateIRI.groovy.** [IMAGE alt='' src='']Figure 4: Showing the steps to find the *.groovy* file
5. Now that you have selected the file, click Ok. You should now see the macro added in the Organize Macros dialog.
6. Click Close.

#### INFO: Macro Language

Macro Language

The **Macro Language** is the macro's file extension. The modeling tool supports **BeanShell, Groovy, JavaScript, JavaScript Rhino,** and **Jython.**

To execute the macro

1. Click on **Tools** > **Macros** > Macro Name e.g. *UpdateIRIMacro*. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. The Notification Window will notify you when the macro was executed. [ATTACHMENT filename='NW.png']

To use the macro for your existing project

1. Right-click on the model on which you would like to update IRIs. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. A dialog will open asking you where to save the results in *.csv* format.
3. After selecting the file, the progress bar shows the progress of the migration.
4. The notification window notifies you when the migration is done, and where the results are stored. [ATTACHMENT filename='updateiri1.PNG']
5. Find the Update IRI results in your designated location, and view the CSV file showing which elements were looked at and updated. The CSV file describes the " Element ID ", " Old IRI "," New IRI ", and " Updated ".

#### NOTE: If you're working with a TWC project...

If you're working with a TWC project...

- For Teamwork Cloud (TWC) projects, the script will try and lock an element before updating its IRI value. For any element that should be updated and cannot be locked, a message will be shown in the Notification Window: " Unable to lock and update IRI for '%s' due to an element being locked by another user ".
- For TWC projects, Property typed elements cannot be locked, so their owner will be locked instead .

#### NOTE: If your element has no IRI...

If your element has no IRI...

- If current element has no IRI value, then no IRI value will be created for it.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478257 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00173: Multiplicities

- page_id: `170478257`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478257/Multiplicities
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:51.140+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Imported Multiplicities

85799310

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Imported Multiplicities

85799313

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Imported Multiplicities

85799311

UML allows the cardinality of a property to be left unspecified, which is interpreted in UML as a minimum of one and a maximum of one. Properties in an OWL ontology are defined with unspecified cardinalities, but unlike UML, the concept modelling profile interprets the cardinalities of such properties as being zero to many (“0..*”). Thus, if you import an ontology, the cardinalities will be “0..*” by default.

#### INFO: Imported Multiplicities

Imported Multiplicities

Importing intersected cardinality restrictions on the same property now results in the cardinalities being applied to the same UML property. The diagram illustrates this concept.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**The modeling tool now imports and transforms multiple multiplicities to the same class into a single multiplicity.**

85799309

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']

The diagram illustrates this concept.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="250"><ri:attachment ri:filename="m4.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Multiplicities" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="text-align: center;"><strong>The modeling tool now imports and transforms multiple multiplicities to the same class into a single multiplicity. </strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6731a452-af27-4756-827c-ec23c22c96a5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">85799309</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478408 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00174: Necessary and sufficient conditions of anonymous subclasses

- page_id: `170478408`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478408/Necessary+and+sufficient+conditions+of+anonymous+subclasses
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-31T14:23:03.816+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1527087235

1527087261

1527087236

This new version of the modeling tool now supports necessary and sufficient conditions of anonymous subclasses.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Necessary and Sufficient condition applied to anonymous subclass

The subsections track a lot of combinations between Intersections, Unions, Restrictions, Complements, and Classes. These subsections are meant to show the different relationships between those four elements and how MCM recognizes them in the exported OWL files. Note that you need to know the meaning of each element: Intersection, Union, Restriction, Complement, and Class, in order to build a cohesive model. If the relationship does not make sense, MCM will notice that and the modeling tool will not export correctly.

1527087234

**Related pages**

****

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

These subsections are meant to show the different relationships between those four elements and how MCM recognizes them in the exported OWL files. Note that you need to know the meaning of each element: Intersection, Union, Restriction, Complement, and Class, in order to build a cohesive model. If the relationship does not make sense, MCM will notice that and the modeling tool will not export correctly. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b39b0c4a-c33e-432e-95c0-92795a377b83"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1527087234</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="09e3734a-dad9-4260-b2f0-fc7d8457feab" /></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478223 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00175: Object properties

- page_id: `170478223`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478223/Object+properties
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:50.094+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1610731570

1610731581

1610731571

Object properties have the following property characteristics: Inverse, Symmetric, Asymmetric, Reflexive, Irreflexive, Functional, Inverse Functional, and Transitive. An example accompanies each characteristic. The modeling tool supports exporting each characteristic; the respective OWL ontology will accompany each example.

**Inverse Object Property**

Sometimes you can invert directions of properties between classes. As shown in the example below, the property *hasBiologicalMother* can be seen as the inverse property of *hasChild.*Furthermore, you can see the Biological Mother and Child are connected by *hasChild,* and Child and Biological Mother are connected by *hasBiologicalMother.*

The OWL ontology is described here: [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class with inverse property' space='MCM2024xR1']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Symmetric Object Property**

At other times, you may want to connect classes which have the same properties. This can be done with a symmetric object property. The example below shows a Person having a Spouse. This is symmetric because that same Spouse would also have a spouse which is a Person.

The OWL ontology is described here: [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class with symmetric object property' space='MCM2024xR1']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Asymmetric Object Property**

Conversely, you may want to characterize properties that are asymmetric. For instance, a biological mother has a child but a child would not have a child and characterize them as a biological mother. Hence, this is property of *hasChild* is asymmetric between a biological mother and her child.

The OWL ontology is described here: [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class with Asymmetric Object Property' space='MCM2024xR1']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Reflexive Object Property**

This object property is used to depict properties that are reflexive to the class. For example, a Person has the right to not reveal their gender on an arbitrary application. This property relates only to that Person.

The OWL ontology is described here: [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class with Reflexive Object Property' space='MCM2024xR1']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Irreflexive Object Property**

On the other hand, some properties are irreflexive, meaning you cannot relate this property to the class itself. For example. John S. is not a relative of himself. Hence, the property *relativeOf* is irreflexive to John S.

The OWL ontology is described here: [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class with Irreflexive Object Property' space='MCM2024xR1']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Functional Object Property**

A functional object property has a multiplicity 0..1 attached to it. The example below is about a husband and a wife, and the functional property *hasHusband* shows that the wife can have at most one husband.

The OWL ontology is described here: [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class with Functional Object Property' space='MCM2024xR1']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Inverse Functional Object Property**

Like the functional object property, the inverse functional object property also has a multiplicity 0..1 attached to it. However, the inverse functional object property, according to the example below, shows that the husband can be a husband to at most one individual, a wife.

The OWL ontology is described here: [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class with Inverse Functional Object Property' space='MCM2024xR1']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Transitive Object Property**

The transitive object property connects two classes when one of the linked class has a connection with another class. The example below shows the transitive object property between Mary and her grandmother.

The OWL ontology is described here: [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class with Transitive Object Property' space='MCM2024xR1']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

1610731569

**Related Pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6159801d-9d82-4b2b-85f3-a427f2917fb1"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1610731570</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c8223dc6-8e77-4645-8782-18f1324afc64"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1610731581</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="47f4b379-ec27-4406-86de-491e3d5def9c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1610731571</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Object properties have the following property characteristics: Inverse, Symmetric, Asymmetric, Reflexive, Irreflexive, Functional, Inverse Functional, and Transitive. An example accompanies each characteristic. The modeling tool supports exporting each characteristic; the respective OWL ontology will accompany each example.</p><p><br /></p><p><strong>Inverse Object Property</strong></p><p>Sometimes you can invert directions of properties between classes. As shown in the example below, the property <em>hasBiologicalMother</em> can be seen as the inverse property of <em>hasChild. </em>Furthermore, you can see the Biological Mother and Child are connected by <em>hasChild,</em> and Child and Biological Mother are connected by <em>hasBiologicalMother.</em></p><p>The OWL ontology is described here: <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Class with inverse property" /></ac:link></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="35"><ri:attachment ri:filename="InverseProperty.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Object properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p> </p><p><strong>Symmetric Object Property</strong></p><p>At other times, you may want to connect classes which have the same properties. This can be done with a symmetric object property. The example below shows a Person having a Spouse. This is symmetric because that same Spouse would also have a spouse which is a Person. </p><p>The OWL ontology is described here: <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Class with symmetric object property" /></ac:link></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="78"><ri:attachment ri:filename="symmetrico.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Object properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><strong>Asymmetric Object Property</strong></p><p>Conversely, you may want to characterize properties that are asymmetric. For instance, a biological mother has a child but a child would not have a child and characterize them as a biological mother. Hence, this is property of <em>hasChild</em> is asymmetric between a biological mother and her child.</p><p>The OWL ontology is described here: <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Class with Asymmetric Object Property" /></ac:link></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="69"><ri:attachment ri:filename="asymmetric0.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Object properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><strong>Reflexive Object Property</strong></p><p>This object property is used to depict properties that are reflexive to the class. For example, a Person has the right to not reveal their gender on an arbitrary application. This property  relates only to that Person. </p><p>The OWL ontology is described here: <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Class with Reflexive Object Property" /></ac:link></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="138"><ri:attachment ri:filename="reflexive0.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Object properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><strong>Irreflexive Object Property</strong></p><p>On the other hand, some properties are irreflexive, meaning you cannot relate this property to the class itself. For example. John S. is not a relative of himself. Hence, the property <em>relativeOf</em> is irreflexive to John S. </p><p>The OWL ontology is described here: <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Class with Irreflexive Object Property" /></ac:link></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="79"><ri:attachment ri:filename="irreflexive0.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Object properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><strong>Functional Object Property</strong></p><p>A functional object property has a multiplicity 0..1 attached to it. The example below is about a husband and a wife, and the functional property <em>hasHusband</em> shows that the wife can have at most one husband.</p><p>The OWL ontology is described here: <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Class with Functional Object Property" /></ac:link></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="85"><ri:attachment ri:filename="functional0.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Object properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><strong>Inverse Functional Object Property</strong></p><p>Like the functional object property, the inverse functional object property also has a multiplicity 0..1 attached to it. However, the inverse functional object property, according to the example below, shows that the husband can be a husband to at most one individual, a wife. </p><p>The OWL ontology is described here: <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Class with Inverse Functional Object Property" /></ac:link></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="80"><ri:attachment ri:filename="InverseFunctional0.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Object properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><strong>Transitive Object Property</strong></p><p>The transitive object property connects two classes when one of the linked class has a connection with another class. The example below shows the transitive object property between Mary and her grandmother. </p><p>The OWL ontology is described here: <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Class with Transitive Object Property" /></ac:link></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="189"><ri:attachment ri:filename="transitiveobjectproperty.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Object properties" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fa490b47-ebbb-4627-a284-92eb7ac64281"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1610731569</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478147 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00176: Open-world assumption vs. closed-world assumption

- page_id: `170478147`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478147/Open-world+assumption+vs.+closed-world+assumption
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:53.704+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Introduction
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

696539738

696539740

696539739

Concept models built by the Concept Modeler satisfy the open-world assumption, that is, no one has complete knowledge of a system and a statement may be true irrespective of whether or not it is known to be true. This is the opposite of the closed-world assumption, which asserts that a statement is true only if it is known to be true and that knowledge of a system is known to be complete. A data model "subsetted" from a concept model would satisfy the closed-world assumption.

696539737

**Related pages**

- MDA
- Concept modeling purpose
- The role of ontologies and reasoners
- Information modeling purpose

This is the opposite of the closed-world assumption, which asserts that a statement is true only if it is known to be true and that knowledge of a system is known to be complete. A data model &quot;subsetted&quot; from a concept model would satisfy the closed-world assumption.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="afd66d87-ab5e-4030-a268-d680755d871c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">696539737</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul class="childpages-macro"><li class="current"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/MDA">MDA</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Concept+modeling+purpose">Concept modeling purpose</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/The+role+of+ontologies+and+reasoners">The role of ontologies and reasoners</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Information+modeling+purpose">Information modeling purpose</a></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478273 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00177: OWL export folder

- page_id: `170478273`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478273/OWL+export+folder
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:51.888+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

353880446

353880457

353880447

Every time you export a concept model to an OWL ontology, it will go into a default export location. The default location is an OWL directory, which is created automatically inside the project file. However, you can disable this default directory and enable the option that will allow you to choose your desired destination folder whenever you export a concept model.

Setting **Always prompt for a file destination when exporting OWL** to**true** in the **Project Options** dialog allows the Concept Modeler to prompt for an export directory to store your «Concept Model» every time you export one. (Please read [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Setting destination for the OWL export folder' space='MCM2024xR1'])

There are some cases to take into account:

- If you have previously selected an export location, a window will open showing the file path of the former saved location.
- If the previously saved export location is invalid, a message will show in the notification window, the previously saved location will clear, and a prompt for a file destination will open to a default location.
- If the previously saved export location no longer exists for any reason, the Concept Modeler will revert to the default location and prompt for file destination.

Leaving this option to **false** (default) allows the Concept Modeler to automatically remember and select the last export location for your «Concept Model» without asking you first.

Similarly, there are some cases to consider:

- If the export location no longer exists, it will export your file to the default OWL directory.
- If there is a valid saved export location, the Concept Modeler will export to the saved export location.
- If the saved export location is not valid and the default location exists, then the Concept Modeler will revert to the default location and display an error message; otherwise, it will create a new OWL folder and revert the saved export location back to the default location.
- If there is no saved export location and the default location exists, then the Concept Modeler will set the export location to the default location; otherwise, it will create a new OWL folder at the project location and set the export location to the default location.

When you would like to select multiple packages, you must right-click on one of the packages to export. You will always receive a prompt for file destination which is applied to all of your packages and the same file location will be loaded to all the packages. You are able to select different file destinations for each package, but the software always loads the same starting location for each package.

The error that appears in the Notification Window when trying to export the concept model to an OWL ontology can be caused by entering an incorrect path name or the path name to the previous export location does not exist anymore. If you encounter this type of error, you need to open the **fileExportPath** tagged value to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying file export paths' space='MCM2024xR1'] in the model’s **Specification** dialog and correct the path name, or select another location.

353880445

**Related Pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The default location is an OWL directory, which is created automatically inside the project file. However, you can disable this default directory and enable the option that will allow you to choose your desired destination folder whenever you export a concept model. </p><p>Setting <strong>Always prompt for a file destination when exporting OWL</strong> to<strong> true</strong> in the <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog allows the Concept Modeler to prompt for an export directory to store your «Concept Model» every time you export one. (Please read <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Setting destination for the OWL export folder" /></ac:link>)</p><p>There are some cases to take into account:</p><ul><li>If you have previously selected an export location, a window will open showing the file path of the former saved location.</li><li>If the previously saved export location is invalid, a message will show in the notification window, the previously saved location will clear, and a prompt for a file destination will open to a default location.</li><li>If the previously saved export location no longer exists for any reason, the Concept Modeler will revert to the default location and prompt for file destination.</li></ul><p>Leaving this option to <strong>false</strong> (default) allows the Concept Modeler to automatically remember and select the last export location for your «Concept Model» without asking you first.</p><p>Similarly, there are some cases to consider:</p><ul><li>If the export location no longer exists, it will export your file to the default OWL directory.</li><li>If there is a valid saved export location, the Concept Modeler will export to the saved export location.</li><li>If the saved export location is not valid and the default location exists, then the Concept Modeler will revert to the default location and display an error message; otherwise, it will create a new OWL folder and revert the saved export location back to the default location.</li><li>If there is no saved export location and the default location exists, then the Concept Modeler will set the export location to the default location; otherwise, it will create a new OWL folder at the project location and set the export location to the default location.</li></ul><p>When you would like to select multiple packages, you must right-click on one of the packages to export. You will always receive a prompt for file destination which is applied to all of your packages and the same file location will be loaded to all the packages. You are able to select different file destinations for each package, but the software always loads the same starting location for each package.</p><p>The error that appears in the Notification Window when trying to export the concept model to an OWL ontology can be caused by entering an incorrect path name or the path name to the previous export location does not exist anymore. If you encounter this type of error, you need to open the <strong>fileExportPath</strong> tagged value to <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Specifying file export paths" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[specify file export paths]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in the model’s <strong>Specification</strong> dialog and correct the path name, or select another location.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="304cea1c-2071-4b55-b026-80f604fefd0f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">353880445</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478176 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00178: Phase

- page_id: `170478176`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478176/Phase
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:48.751+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics > Advanced Modeling Patterns > Facets
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

37516506

37516517

37516507

A «Phase» is a kind of «Facet» that is a state of an identifiable thing that changes over time. For example, Living and Deceased might be phases of an Organism; or Egg, Larva, Pupa, and Adult might be phases of a butterfly.

When the necessary conditions for a «Phase» concept are met, which may include the occurrence of an event or meeting a condition, such as having wings, the identifiable thing will be a member of that «Phase» concept.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

37516505

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

For example, Living and Deceased might be phases of an Organism; or Egg, Larva, Pupa, and Adult might be phases of a butterfly.</p><p>When the necessary conditions for a «Phase» concept are met, which may include the occurrence of an event or meeting a condition, such as having wings, the identifiable thing will be a member of that «Phase» concept.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="242"><ri:attachment ri:filename="p1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Phase" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c6494849-3311-4036-baad-f5b7bbe6550a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">37516505</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478235 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00179: Preferred Annotation Property

- page_id: `170478235`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478235/Preferred+Annotation+Property
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:50.405+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

85604219

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

85604221

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

85604220

The **Project Options** in Concept Modeler provides a **Preferred annotation property** option for the UML Comments or «Annotation»s in a model. This capability allows you to control which annotation property becomes the “special” MagicDraw comment providing documentation, when you enter text into the Documentation pane or create a new Annotation. It also allows the documentation to be included in the Natural Language Glossary. If the UML Comments and the «Annotation» Comments do not have annotation property value assigned (**annotationProperty** Tag from Specifications), they should be treated as if they have "Preferred annotation property" assigned from Project options.

Changing a preferred annotation property tagged value will cause the ownership of existing UML comments and «Annotation»s to change, for example:

- Any UML comment and «Annotation» that was using the old preferred annotation will be moved from being owned by the annotated element to being owned by the next-higher package.
- Any UML comment and «Annotation» that is using the new preferred annotation property will be moved to being owned by the annotated element.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

- Changing the preferred annotation property tagged value of UML comments and «Annotation»s will change their ownership.
- Changing in ownership causes a change in the Documentation pane (on the lower-left) and what shows up in the Natural Language Glossary because the first UML Comment or «Annotation» that is owned by an annotated element is considered “documentation” in MagicDraw.

If you do not select an annotation property tagged value for any UML comment or «Annotation» in your model, any time we export it to an OWL ontology, the Concept Modeler will use the preferred annotation property that does not have a tagged value, which is *[http://purl.org/dc/terms/description](http://purl.org/dc/terms/description)*[3]. This preferred annotation property without a tagged value (unspecified) in the Concept Modeler is active until you change it through the **Project****Options** dialog. Selecting **<UNSPECIFIED>** from the **Project Options** dialog will remove the current preferred annotation property tagged value.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**A comment stereotyped with «Annotation» to provide a correct meaning of an item.**

The following diagram is an example of how the Preferred annotation property option works. In the figure, a class, **autonomous** **agent,** is plotted on the diagram along with five annotations.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**A class with annotations in the diagram pane.**

The first Containment tree example shows you how they are structured before you select a preferred annotation property from the **Project Options** dialog. Notice that the **Documentation** pane shows no annotation when the class is clicked. Using the instructions above, we select the **definition** as the preferred annotation property for the «Annotation» with the (annotationProperty = definition).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Annotations in the Containment tree before the Preferred annotation property is selected.**

The second Containment tree example shows how the annotation ownership changed after you selected **definition** as the preferred annotation property. If you open the Specification dialog of the «Annotation» with the (annotationProperty = definition), you will see that the new owner is **autonomous agent**(previously, it was the package **Agents**’). The **Documentation** pane will also show the annotation if you click the class **autonomous agent**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**The annotation appears in the Documentation pane after the preferred annotation property is selected.**

85604218

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bc400019-56f4-403b-b4b9-0d6b716e24c2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">85604219</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0cc12b8c-2390-4426-90b4-57cc40c006a7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">85604221</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b3abd031-2be7-4d35-9cc9-65815e21143d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">85604220</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The <strong>Project Options</strong> in Concept Modeler provides a <strong>Preferred annotation property</strong> option for the UML Comments or «Annotation»s in a model. This capability allows you to control which annotation property becomes the “special” MagicDraw comment providing documentation, when you enter text into the Documentation pane or create a new Annotation. It also allows the documentation to be included in the Natural Language Glossary. If the UML Comments and the «Annotation» Comments do not have annotation property value assigned (<strong>annotationProperty</strong> Tag from Specifications), they should be treated as if they have &quot;Preferred annotation property&quot; assigned from Project options.</p><p>Changing a preferred annotation property tagged value will cause the ownership of existing UML comments and «Annotation»s to change, for example:</p><ul><li>Any UML comment and «Annotation» that was using the old preferred annotation will be moved from being owned by the annotated element to being owned by the next-higher package.</li><li>Any UML comment and «Annotation» that is using the new preferred annotation property will be moved to being owned by the annotated element.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6e35decb-c748-4352-acbe-de3810a7b98c"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Changing the preferred annotation property tagged value of UML comments and «Annotation»s will change their ownership.</li><li>Changing in ownership causes a change in the Documentation pane (on the lower-left) and what shows up in the Natural Language Glossary because the first UML Comment or «Annotation» that is owned by an annotated element is considered “documentation” in MagicDraw.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">If you do not select an annotation property tagged value for any UML comment or «Annotation» in your model, any time we export it to an OWL ontology, the Concept Modeler will use the preferred annotation property that does not have a tagged value, which is <em><a href="http://purl.org/dc/terms/description">http://purl.org/dc/terms/description</a> </em>[3]. This preferred annotation property without a tagged value (unspecified) in the Concept Modeler is active until you change it through the <strong>Project </strong><strong>Options</strong> dialog. Selecting <strong>&lt;UNSPECIFIED&gt;</strong> from the <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog will remove the current preferred annotation property tagged value.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Picture1.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Preferred Annotation Property" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="text-align: center;"><strong style="text-align: center;">A comment stereotyped with «Annotation» to provide a correct meaning of an item.</strong><br /></p><p>The following diagram is an example of how the Preferred annotation property option works. In the figure, a class, <strong>autonomous</strong> <strong>agent,</strong> is plotted on the diagram along with five annotations.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Screen Shot 2018-02-08 at 2.01.01 PM.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Preferred Annotation Property" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="text-align: center;"><strong>A class with annotations in the diagram pane.</strong></p><p>The first Containment tree example shows you how they are structured before you select a preferred annotation property from the <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog. Notice that the <strong>Documentation</strong> pane shows no annotation when the class is clicked. Using the instructions above, we select the <strong>definition</strong> as the preferred annotation property for the «Annotation» with the (annotationProperty = definition).</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="400"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Annotations in the Containment tree.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Preferred Annotation Property" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="text-align: center;"><strong>Annotations in the Containment tree before the Preferred annotation property is selected.</strong></p><p>The second Containment tree example shows how the annotation ownership changed after you selected <strong>definition</strong> as the preferred annotation property. If you open the Specification dialog of the «Annotation» with the (annotationProperty = definition), you will see that the new owner is <strong>autonomous agent </strong>(previously, it was the package <strong>Agents</strong>’). The <strong>Documentation</strong> pane will also show the annotation if you click the class <strong>autonomous agent</strong>.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="400"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Picture3.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Preferred Annotation Property" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="text-align: center;"><strong>The annotation appears in the Documentation pane after the preferred annotation property is selected.</strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="31cbc448-38d2-4434-925e-d2328b7538f3"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">85604218</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478201 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00180: Property chain

- page_id: `170478201`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478201/Property+chain
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:53.939+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

1284903596

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

1284903598

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

1284903597

The Concept Modeler can import property chains. A property chain is useful for composing a property from two or more other properties that are put together in a chain. It defines the property with reference to the other properties. The property chain allows you to navigate from a starting class (the one with the stereotype «Subproperty Chain») through a chain of properties that take a path through multiple classes.

A property chain is an ordered list of composed properties, therefore, it should have two or more properties in the chain. The same property can appear more than once in a chain. For example, “has parent • has parent” is a subproperty of “has grandparent”.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

- An existential or universal quantification restriction cannot have or be a part of a subproperty chain, although the property it restricts can .
- A sub-property can have or be part of a subproperty chain for another property.

The following example describes a Person class that has two instances “Female Person” and “Male Person”, and four properties “has parent”, “has father”, “has uncle”, and “has brother”. The stereotype of the property “has uncle” will be **«****Subproperty****Chain»** of **Element** type and the tagged value is **chain = has father, has brother**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Property chain in Concept Modeler.

For more information on how to create a property chain, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a property chain' space='MCM2024xR1'].

1284903595

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']
- Create a Property Chain

A property chain is useful for composing a property from two or more other properties that are put together in a chain. It defines the property with reference to the other properties. The property chain allows you to navigate from a starting class (the one with the stereotype «Subproperty Chain») through a chain of properties that take a path through multiple classes.</p><p>A property chain is an ordered list of composed properties, therefore, it should have two or more properties in the chain. The same property can appear more than once in a chain. For example, “has parent • has parent” is a subproperty of “has grandparent”.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9a2179b9-6810-4656-8992-96be2856190b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>An existential or universal quantification restriction <em>cannot</em> have or be a part of a subproperty chain, although the property it restricts <em>can</em>.</li><li>A sub-property <em>can</em> have or be part of a subproperty chain for another property.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The following example describes a Person class that has two instances “Female Person” and “Male Person”, and four properties “has parent”, “has father”, “has uncle”, and “has brother”. The stereotype of the property “has uncle” will be <strong>«</strong><strong>Subproperty</strong><strong> Chain»</strong> of <strong>Element</strong> type and the tagged value is <strong>chain = has father, has brother</strong>.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-23 13:33:27.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Property chain" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Property chain in Concept Modeler.</h6><p>For more information on how to create a property chain, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Creating a property chain" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Create a Property Chain]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="61fc0885-9622-461b-969b-bbfb8a25f71f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1284903595</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MCM2024xR1/Creating+a+property+chain">Create a Property Chain</a></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478400 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00181: Property chains

- page_id: `170478400`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478400/Property+chains
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:58.631+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

5622411

5622413

5622412

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Properties in a property chain.

```text
Declaration(Class(:FemalePerson))
      Declaration(Class(:MalePerson))
      Declaration(Class(:Person))
      Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasBrother))
      Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasFather))
      Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasUncle))
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :FemalePerson "Female Person"@en)
      SubClassOf(:FemalePerson :Person)
      DisjointClasses(:FemalePerson :MalePerson)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :MalePerson "Male Person"@en)
      SubClassOf(:MalePerson :Person)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Person "Person"@en)
      SubClassOf(:Person ObjectIntersectionOf(ObjectMaxCardinality(1 :hasFather :MalePerson) ObjectMinCardinality(1 :hasFather :MalePerson)))
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasBrother "has brother"@en)
      ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasBrother :Person)
      ObjectPropertyRange(:hasBrother :MalePerson)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasFather "has father"@en)
      ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasFather :Person)
      ObjectPropertyRange(:hasFather :MalePerson)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasUncle "has uncle"@en)
      ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasUncle :Person)
      ObjectPropertyRange(:hasUncle :MalePerson)
      SubObjectPropertyOf(ObjectPropertyChain(:hasFather :hasBrother) :hasUncle)
)]]>
```

5622409

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax' space='MCM2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ae7f39ce-eaf9-480c-b086-fcbb450ff674"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">5622411</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b055f154-b1d3-447d-9953-026f5909ab52"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">5622413</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b647e2c4-2c77-48ed-bc6a-6e90560b3e7c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">5622412</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-24 14:49:14.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Property chains" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Properties in a property chain.</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9a2ed8d6-b2fb-4bf7-871d-5e77d943b5ad"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Ontology(<http://example.com/ontology/Unnamed>
      Declaration(Class(:FemalePerson))
      Declaration(Class(:MalePerson))
      Declaration(Class(:Person))
      Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasBrother))
      Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasFather))
      Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasUncle))
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :FemalePerson "Female Person"@en)
      SubClassOf(:FemalePerson :Person)
      DisjointClasses(:FemalePerson :MalePerson)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :MalePerson "Male Person"@en)
      SubClassOf(:MalePerson :Person)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Person "Person"@en)
      SubClassOf(:Person ObjectIntersectionOf(ObjectMaxCardinality(1 :hasFather :MalePerson) ObjectMinCardinality(1 :hasFather :MalePerson)))
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasBrother "has brother"@en)
      ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasBrother :Person)
      ObjectPropertyRange(:hasBrother :MalePerson)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasFather "has father"@en)
      ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasFather :Person)
      ObjectPropertyRange(:hasFather :MalePerson)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasUncle "has uncle"@en)
      ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasUncle :Person)
      ObjectPropertyRange(:hasUncle :MalePerson)
      SubObjectPropertyOf(ObjectPropertyChain(:hasFather :hasBrother) :hasUncle)
)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="98c50839-db66-4c90-8425-47a51121375a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">5622409</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

## PAGE 00182: Property holder with datatype property

- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax
- labels: ['datatype-property', 'property-holder']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

86502261

86502264

86502262

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A property holder with datatype property.

```text
Import()
 Declaration(
 DataProperty(:hasName)
 )
 Declaration(
 AnnotationProperty()
 )
 Declaration(
 Datatype(xsd:string)
 )
 SubClassOf(
 owl:Thing 
 ObjectIntersectionOf(
 DataMaxCardinality(3 :hasName xsd:string) 
 DataMinCardinality(2 :hasName xsd:string)
 )
 )
 AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasName "has name"@en)
 DataPropertyRange(:hasName xsd:string)
 AnnotationAssertion(http://purl.org/dc/terms/description
  "An instance of String defines a piece of text. The semantics of the string itself depends on its purpose, it can be a comment, computational language expression, OCL expression, etc. It is used for String attributes and String expressions in the metamodel."@en)
 )]]>
```

86502260

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax' space='MCM2024xR1']

````html
 Import(<http://www.omg.org/spec/PrimitiveTypes/20100901>)
 Declaration(
 DataProperty(:hasName)
 )
 Declaration(
 AnnotationProperty(<http://purl.org/dc/terms/description>)
 )
 Declaration(
 Datatype(xsd:string)
 )
 SubClassOf(
 owl:Thing 
 ObjectIntersectionOf(
 DataMaxCardinality(3 :hasName xsd:string) 
 DataMinCardinality(2 :hasName xsd:string)
 )
 )
 AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasName "has name"@en)
 DataPropertyRange(:hasName xsd:string)
 AnnotationAssertion(http://purl.org/dc/terms/description
 <http://www.omg.org/spec/PrimitiveTypes/20100901#String> "An instance of String defines a piece of text. The semantics of the string itself depends on its purpose, it can be a comment, computational language expression, OCL expression, etc. It is used for String attributes and String expressions in the metamodel."@en)
 )]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2c2b054d-21d0-40b6-be55-88a7e7f76851"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">86502260</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

## PAGE 00185: Property holder with self-referential subproperty

- page_id: `170478383`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478383/Property+holder+with+self-referential+subproperty
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:57.403+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

4156687

4156689

4156688

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A property holder with self-referential subproperty.

```text
Declaration(
             ObjectProperty(:contains)
      )
      Declaration(
             ObjectProperty(:holds)
      )
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :contains "contains"@en)
      SubObjectPropertyOf(:contains :holds)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :holds "holds"@en)
)]]>
```

## PAGE 00186: Property holder with subproperty

- page_id: `170478386`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`

- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:57.581+02:00`

- labels: ['property-holder', 'subsetted-object-property']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

4955995

4955997

4955996

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A property with subproperty.

```text
Declaration(
             Class(:Acid)
      )
      Declaration(
             Class(:Liquid)
      )
      Declaration(
             ObjectProperty(:isCorrodedBy)
      )
      Declaration(
             ObjectProperty(:isDissolvedBy)
      )
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Acid "Acid"@en)
      SubClassOf(:Acid :Liquid)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :Liquid "Liquid"@en)
      SubClassOf(
             owl:Thing 
             ObjectIntersectionOf(
                    ObjectMinCardinality(1 :isCorrodedBy :Acid)
             )
      )
      SubClassOf(
             owl:Thing 
             ObjectIntersectionOf(
                    ObjectMinCardinality(1 :isDissolvedBy :Liquid)
             )
      )
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :isCorrodedBy "is corroded by"@en)
      SubObjectPropertyOf(:isCorrodedBy :isDissolvedBy)
      ObjectPropertyRange(:isCorrodedBy :Acid)
      AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :isDissolvedBy "is dissolved by"@en)
      ObjectPropertyRange(:isDissolvedBy :Liquid)
)    ]]>
```

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478192 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00187: Property ownership

- page_id: `170478192`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478192/Property+ownership
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:49.090+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

1149615940

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

1149615942

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

1149615941

The concept modeling profile of UML interprets the owner of a property as a context in which that property must conform to certain constraints. These constraints can include multiplicity (which includes a minimum cardinality and a maximum cardinality), a type for the property, existential quantification, and universal quantification. When an instance is a member of an owning class, all of that class’ constraints must be met.

An OWL ontology may define a property that is foreign to its namespace. If you import an OWL ontology into MCM that defines a property that is in a foreign namespace, you will see the association ends with cross (x) marks. In the Concept Modeler, these non-navigable association ends mean that the properties belong to foreign domains and therefore, they are owned by the association. The following diagram shows the examples of non-navigable association ends.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Properties owned by an association.

In the diagram, the Employer, Vehicle, and Employed classes belong to three different namespaces, and the association belongs to the same namespace as that of the Employer.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

It is recommended that duplicate property names in a concept modeling diagram be avoided because they will result in conflicting definition of domains and ranges when exported to OWL.

1149615939

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Concept Modeling Semantics' space='MCM2024xR1']

These constraints can include multiplicity (which includes a minimum cardinality and a maximum cardinality), a type for the property, existential quantification, and universal quantification. When an instance is a member of an owning class, all of that class’ constraints must be met.</p><p>An OWL ontology may define a property that is foreign to its namespace. If you import an OWL ontology into MCM that defines a property that is in a foreign namespace, you will see the association ends with cross (x) marks. In the Concept Modeler, these non-navigable association ends mean that the properties belong to foreign domains and therefore, they are owned by the association. The following diagram shows the examples of non-navigable association ends.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2016-8-23 11:33:27.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Property ownership" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color: rgb(112,112,112);font-size: 12.0px;">Properties owned by an association.</span></h6><p>In the diagram, the Employer, Vehicle, and Employed classes belong to three different namespaces, and the association belongs to the same namespace as that of the Employer.  </p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="61f0f850-c254-4923-b76a-5aafec3f6c9b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>It is recommended that duplicate property names in a concept modeling diagram be avoided because they will result in conflicting definition of domains and ranges when exported to OWL.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d0d05d91-c4b4-4e51-b05b-6922086e7ffd"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1149615939</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 00188: Property restriction from a different namespace

- page_id: `170478406`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478406/Property+restriction+from+a+different+namespace
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:59.155+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Change the Owned by of the property *'has child'* to Association and then move the Association to a different concepy model, *Functional*and then export the Functional concept model**

```text
Declaration(Class(:Husband))
Declaration(Class(:Wife))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasChild))

############################
#   Object Properties
############################

# Object Property: :hasChild (has child)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasChild "has child"^^xsd:string)
AsymmetricObjectProperty(:hasChild)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasChild )
ObjectPropertyRange(:hasChild )




)


]]>
```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="800"><ri:attachment ri:filename="f3.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Property restriction from a different namespace" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="text-align: center;"><strong>Change the Owned by of the property <em>'has child'</em> to Association and then move the Association to a different concepy model, <em>Functional </em>and then export the Functional concept model</strong></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9439cba3-0c0a-4dbc-b729-c4c3ec00695e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Ontology(<http://example.com/ontology/Functional>

Declaration(Class(:Husband))
Declaration(Class(:Wife))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasChild))

############################
#   Object Properties
############################

# Object Property: :hasChild (has child)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasChild "has child"^^xsd:string)
AsymmetricObjectProperty(:hasChild)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasChild <http://example.com/ontology/Asymmetric#BiologicalMother>)
ObjectPropertyRange(:hasChild <http://example.com/ontology/Asymmetric#Child>)




)


]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478203 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00189: Property restrictions

- page_id: `170478203`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478203/Property+restrictions
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:49.486+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1056928587

1056928590

1056928588

In the Concept Modeler, a property restriction appears as one with a {subsets} or {redefines}.

**Importing Property Restrictions**

The Concept Modeler will import each property restriction as a unidirectional association between the two concepts.

The Concept Modeler will import eachconcept restriction as a unidirectional association between the concept on which the restriction is specified and the concept filler specified in the restriction definition. If the restriction is defined in an ontology different than the concept on which it is defined, then the resulting unidirectional association is owned by the «Model» corresponding to the ontology in which it is defined. In this case, the restricting property is owned by the association.

1056928586

If the restriction is defined in an ontology different than the concept on which it is defined, then the resulting unidirectional association is owned by the «Model»  corresponding to the ontology in which it is defined. In this case, the restricting property is owned by the association.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d96cec55-5f0b-412c-af25-eceddd43f9f2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1056928586</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Concept Modeling Semantics" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Cascading restrictions" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Existential quantification constraint" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Universal quantification constraint" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478389 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00190: Property with a maximum but no minimum cardinality

- page_id: `170478389`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478389/Property+with+a+maximum+but+no+minimum+cardinality
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:57.768+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Showing the cardinality 0..3, the 0 means no minimum.

Note the *SubClassOf(:A ObjectMaxCardinality(3 :hasB)).*This is important, because the cardinality is actually exported and noted in the OWL ontology. In previous versions, this was not supported.

```text
Annotation(rdfs:label "Example"^^xsd:string)

Declaration(Class(:A))
Declaration(Class(:B))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasB))

############################
#   Object Properties
############################

# Object Property: :hasB (has b)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasB "has b"^^xsd:string)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasB :A)
ObjectPropertyRange(:hasB :B)



############################
#   Classes
############################

# Class: :A (:A)

SubClassOf(:A ObjectMaxCardinality(3 :hasB))


)]]>
```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h6 class="auto-cursor-target" style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="55"><ri:attachment ri:filename="maxnomin.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Property with a maximum but no minimum cardinality" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>Showing the cardinality 0..3, the 0 means no minimum. </h6><p>Note the <em>SubClassOf(:A ObjectMaxCardinality(3 :hasB)). </em>This is important, because the cardinality is actually exported and noted in the OWL ontology. In previous versions, this was not supported. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b3297c5b-5594-4c09-a92a-03d90875eb1b"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Ontology(<http://example.com/ontology/Unnamed>
Annotation(rdfs:label "Example"^^xsd:string)

Declaration(Class(:A))
Declaration(Class(:B))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:hasB))

############################
#   Object Properties
############################

# Object Property: :hasB (has b)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :hasB "has b"^^xsd:string)
ObjectPropertyDomain(:hasB :A)
ObjectPropertyRange(:hasB :B)



############################
#   Classes
############################

# Class: :A (:A)

SubClassOf(:A ObjectMaxCardinality(3 :hasB))


)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478391 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00191: Property with multiple domains and ranges

- page_id: `170478391`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478391/Property+with+multiple+domains+and+ranges
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:39:57.936+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Showing the property named *has*has multiple domains: Person and Cat Owner**

#### INFO: ObjectUnionOf

ObjectUnionOf

A key thing to note here is:

```text
ObjectPropertyDomain(:has ObjectUnionOf(:CatOwner :Person))ObjectPropertyRange(:has ObjectUnionOf(:Cat :Pet))
```

This shows that a property which has multiple domains is exported as a union of all the domains and ranges.

```text

```

```text
Annotation(rdfs:label "Example"^^xsd:string)

Declaration(Class(:Cat))
Declaration(Class(:CatOwner))
Declaration(Class(:Person))
Declaration(Class(:Pet))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:has))

############################
#   Object Properties
############################

# Object Property: :has (:has)

ObjectPropertyDomain(:has ObjectUnionOf(:CatOwner :Person))
ObjectPropertyRange(:has ObjectUnionOf(:Cat :Pet))



############################
#   Classes
############################

# Class: :Cat (:Cat)

SubClassOf(:Cat :Pet)

# Class: :CatOwner (Cat Owner)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :CatOwner "Cat Owner"^^xsd:string)
SubClassOf(:CatOwner :Person)


)


]]>
```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="150"><ri:attachment ri:filename="MDR.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Property with multiple domains and ranges" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="text-align: center;"><strong>Showing the property named <em>has </em>has multiple domains: Person and Cat Owner</strong></p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b2dacf7c-7873-4f1d-9db8-19e9f56e36c4"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">ObjectUnionOf</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>A key thing to note here is:</p><pre>ObjectPropertyDomain(:has ObjectUnionOf(:CatOwner :Person))<br />ObjectPropertyRange(:has ObjectUnionOf(:Cat :Pet))</pre><p>This shows that a property which has multiple domains is exported as a union of all the domains and ranges.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><pre><br /></pre><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c47894b0-a30f-4b70-b474-ea565b5a91c7"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Ontology(<http://example.com/ontology/Unnamed>
Annotation(rdfs:label "Example"^^xsd:string)

Declaration(Class(:Cat))
Declaration(Class(:CatOwner))
Declaration(Class(:Person))
Declaration(Class(:Pet))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:has))

############################
#   Object Properties
############################

# Object Property: :has (:has)

ObjectPropertyDomain(:has ObjectUnionOf(:CatOwner :Person))
ObjectPropertyRange(:has ObjectUnionOf(:Cat :Pet))



############################
#   Classes
############################

# Class: :Cat (:Cat)

SubClassOf(:Cat :Pet)

# Class: :CatOwner (Cat Owner)

AnnotationAssertion(rdfs:label :CatOwner "Cat Owner"^^xsd:string)
SubClassOf(:CatOwner :Person)


)


]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

## PAGE 00192: Rebuilding a glossary table

- page_id: `170479076`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`

- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

1302159039

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

1302159041

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

1302159040

When the glossary-generating options change, you may want to rebuild existing glossary tables to contain only the specified kinds of entries.

To rebuild an existing glossary table for a concept model

1. Right-click on the existing concept modeling generated glossary.
2. Select Concept Modeling .
3. Select Rebuild Glossary Table .

#### NOTE: Note

Note

The owning package must have the correct «Model» or «Concept Model» stereotype, and a glossary table must not already exist for this menu item to appear.

**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

###### The Concept Modeler's Rebuild Glossary Table menu.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

At least one glossary generation project option must be enabled for the **Rebuild Glossary Table** menu item to be enabled, otherwise it will look like the last menu item in the following figure.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Rebuild Glossary Table menu is disabled.

1302159038

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Usage' space='MCM2024xR1']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478974 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00193: Redefined property

- page_id: `170478974`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478974/Redefined+property
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:58.432+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Working with properties
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

426601211

426601214

426601212

It is often useful to go to a redefined property to see its original definition. There are two ways in which this can be done. The first is to go to the redefined property in the Containment tree. The second is to go to the redefined property on a diagram.

To go to a redefined property in the Containment tree

1. Right-click on a redefining property, its multiplicity, or its redefinition. [ATTACHMENT filename='image2016-8-30 14:31:35.png'] A redefining property in Concept Modeler.
2. Select Go To > Redefined property in containment tree . [ATTACHMENT filename='image2016-8-30 14:32:45.png'] The Redefined property in containment tree shortcut menu.

The focus will jump to the redefined property in the containment tree, as shown in the following figure.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Concept Modeler highlights the redefined property in the Containment tree.

To focus on a redefined property on a diagram

1. Right-click on a redefining property, its multiplicity, or its redefinition. [ATTACHMENT filename='image2016-8-30 14:35:2.png'] A redefining property in the Concept Modeler.
2. Select Go To > Redefined property on diagram and choose a diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Redefined property on diagram shortcut menu.

The focus will jump to the redefined property on the selected diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Concept Modeler highlights the redefined property on the selected diagram.

426601210

**Related page**

- Usage

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478994 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00195: Removing property subsetting

- page_id: `170478994`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478994/Removing+property+subsetting
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:58.840+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Working with properties
- labels: ['subsetted-property', 'property-subset']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1278286251

1278286253

1278286252

To remove subsetting of a property

1. Double-click the subsetting property.
2. Click on the box next to "Subsetted Property" in the right pane of the Specification window for the subsetting property ("composed of" from "Futsal Team").
3. Click on the [ATTACHMENT filename='button-2.png'] button next to the subsetted property. [ATTACHMENT filename='property_specification_dialog.png']
4. Select the subsetted property in the right pane of the dialog.
5. Click the – button.
6. Click OK . [ATTACHMENT filename='selecting_subsetted_property.png']
7. The subsetting of the property is removed. [ATTACHMENT filename='property_subsetting_removed.png']

1278286250

**Related page**

- Usage
