<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478310 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00233: UML to Equivalent OWL in OWL Functional Syntax

- page_id: `170478310`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478310/UML+to+Equivalent+OWL+in+OWL+Functional+Syntax
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-31T14:23:15.897+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation
- labels: ['owl', 'owl-functional-syntax', 'owl-ontology']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

1285872795

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

1285872797

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

1285872796

There are various syntaxes available for encoding OWL ontologies. The Concept Modeler can export UML to an OWL ontology using the following syntaxes:

- RDF/XML . It is the original standard syntax for writing RDF (Resource Description Framework), which is a general-purpose language for representing information on the Web. Although it is wordy and difficult to read, it is the only syntax written in various concrete formats called RDF serialization formats, and it is mandatory to be supported by OWL 2 tools. It provides an XML representation of an RDF graph. This syntax is the default syntax used in the Concept Modeler.
- JSON-LD or JavaScript Object Notation for Linked Data is a method of encoding linked data using JSON, which is a concrete RDF syntax. JSON-LD is used to map JSON terms (keys and values) to IRIs, giving them a global context. A JSON-LD document is both an RDF document and a JSON document and correspondingly represents an instance of an RDF data model.
- OWL Functional . It is a simple text-based syntax designed to be easier for specification purposes, and to provide a foundation for the implementation of OWL 2 tools such as APIs and reasoners. It is used in most of the OWL 2 specification documents as the primary presentation syntax that translates the structural specification into other concrete syntaxes. A functional-style syntax ontology document consists of sequences of Unicode characters and is encoded in UTF-8.
- Turtle . A concrete syntax for RDF, Turtle (Terse RDF Triple Language) is a plain-text RDF representation. It is more concise, and easier to read and edit manually than RDF/XML. A Turtle document is a collection of RDF-triples, which groups three URIs to make a triple and provides ways to abbreviate information, e.g., by factoring out common portions of URIs. Each triple has the format: <subject> <predicate> <object>. Each statement ends with a period, and each element in the triple is an URI, except the <object>, which can be a bit of text or a number.
- Manchester . It provides a compact textual-based representation of OWL ontologies that is easy to read and write. It uses IRIs as term identifiers. The syntax for entering and displaying annotations and descriptions in the Manchester OWL syntax closely corresponds to the syntax in the OWL Functional syntax. A Manchester OWL document consists of sequences of Unicode characters, and is encoded in UTF-8.

This section gives examples that show the transformation of UML modeled in the Concept Modeler to an exported OWL ontology. The OWL ontologies are presented in OWL Functional Syntax.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

- A model may contain elements, (e.g. classes, properties, or datatypes) that belong to other models. When exporting the model, the Concept Modeler will include the OWL declaration of the elements that exist in the current model only, not those of the other models.
- However, if the entity that belongs to another model is an object property with an inverse property defined, you will see the OWL declaration of the inverse property in the current OWL ontology upon export.

1285872794

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Setting the concept model export syntax' space='MCM2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exporting your concept model to an OWL ontology' space='MCM2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Usage' space='MCM2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="486d2b7a-72cb-47e0-9f27-d5ccc42f30ec"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1285872795</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cec6cc90-4609-437e-b134-6de97676785f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1285872797</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="40cef8e9-e791-4f6a-8f6c-9077027eaa86"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1285872796</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>There are various syntaxes available for encoding OWL ontologies. The Concept Modeler can export UML to an OWL ontology using the following syntaxes:</p><ul><li><strong>RDF/XML</strong>. It is the original standard syntax for writing RDF (Resource Description Framework), which is a general-purpose language for representing information on the Web. Although it is wordy and difficult to read, it is the only syntax written in various concrete formats called RDF serialization formats, and it is mandatory to be supported by OWL 2 tools. It provides an XML representation of an RDF graph. This syntax is the default syntax used in the Concept Modeler.</li><li><strong>JSON-LD </strong>or JavaScript Object Notation for Linked Data is a method of encoding linked data using JSON, which is a concrete RDF syntax. JSON-LD is used to map JSON terms (keys and values) to IRIs, giving them a global context. A JSON-LD document is both an RDF document and a JSON document and correspondingly represents an instance of an RDF data model.</li><li><strong>OWL Functional</strong>.  It is a simple text-based syntax designed to be easier for specification purposes, and to provide a foundation for the implementation of OWL 2 tools such as APIs and reasoners. It is used in most of the OWL 2 specification documents as the primary presentation syntax that translates the structural specification into other concrete syntaxes. A functional-style syntax ontology document consists of sequences of Unicode characters and is encoded in UTF-8.</li><li><strong>Turtle</strong>.  A concrete syntax for RDF, Turtle (Terse RDF Triple Language) is a plain-text RDF representation. It is more concise, and easier to read and edit manually than RDF/XML. A Turtle document is a collection of RDF-triples, which groups three URIs to make a triple and provides ways to abbreviate information, e.g., by factoring out common portions of URIs. Each triple has the format: &lt;subject&gt; &lt;predicate&gt; &lt;object&gt;. Each statement ends with a period, and each element in the triple is an URI, except the &lt;object&gt;, which can be a bit of text or a number.</li><li><strong>Manchester</strong>.  It provides a compact textual-based representation of OWL ontologies that is easy to read and write. It uses IRIs as term identifiers. The syntax for entering and displaying annotations and descriptions in the Manchester OWL syntax closely corresponds to the syntax in the OWL Functional syntax. A Manchester OWL document consists of sequences of Unicode characters, and is encoded in UTF-8.</li></ul><p>This section gives examples that show the transformation of UML modeled in the Concept Modeler to an exported OWL ontology. The OWL ontologies are presented in OWL Functional Syntax.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a2eb2af2-7fe6-4b94-be14-73c320afccec"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>A model may contain elements, (e.g. classes, properties, or datatypes) that belong to other models. When exporting the model, the Concept Modeler will include the OWL declaration of the elements that exist in the current model only, not those of the other models.</li><li>However, if the entity that belongs to another model is an object property with an inverse property defined, you will see the OWL declaration of the inverse property in the current OWL ontology upon export.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d4d86a69-9a7e-4b2d-b6d2-f62bca703fbd"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1285872794</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Setting the concept model export syntax" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Exporting your concept model to an OWL ontology" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Usage" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="098695cf-f019-4b40-9c77-d5771115a0ec" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

```text

Declaration(Class(:B))
Declaration(Class(:C))
############################

############################

DisjointClasses(:C ObjectUnionOf(:A :B))


)


]]>
```

## PAGE 00242: Union equivalent to Union

- page_id: `170478434`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478434/Union+equivalent+to+Union

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b67a474b-ccc4-459d-aad9-2322ededcc80"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1565371504</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="04e0affe-771b-459a-9cbe-6b6f77ccd354"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1565371525</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="db2340e7-f9e3-4570-accc-b654b28d78aa"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1565371515</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="CCM__ClassNotationAnalysisRow082__ClassNotationAnalysisRow082.jpg"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Union subset of Union" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fc64c39f-d766-4752-99b8-ae9d1cd88a33"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Ontology(<http://www.semanticweb.org/wmilnor/ontologies/2018/3/ClassNotationAnalysisRow082>
Import(<http://nomagic.com/ConceptModeler/ConceptModelingSupportLibrary/>)

Declaration(Class(:A))
Declaration(Class(:B))
Declaration(Class(:C))
Declaration(Class(:D))

SubClassOf(ObjectUnionOf(:A :B) ObjectUnionOf(:C :D))
)


]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478209 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00253: Universal quantification constraint

- page_id: `170478209`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478209/Universal+quantification+constraint
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:54.006+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Concept Modeling Semantics > Property restrictions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Optimized Multiplicity

85419690

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Optimized Multiplicity

85419693

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Optimized Multiplicity

85419691

Sometimes, in the context of some class, it is necessary to constrain *all* the values of a property to a particular type. This constraint is known as a *universal* quantification or *for-all*constraint (∀). This kind of constraint is an assertion that only values of the specified type are valid, and that the number of values must be between some minimum and maximum cardinality. In the concept modeling interpretation of UML, introducing a new property or redefining an existing property creates a universal quantification constraint in the context of the owning class. This interpretation is based on {redefines} in UML, which allows adding more specific constraints to an existing property without defining a new property.

The diagram below shows the introduction of a new property, “consists of”, defining a universal quantification constraint on the property. The constraint states, in the context of Soccer Team and any of its subclasses, that all values of this property must be of the type “Soccer Player”, and that there must be between 5 and 11 values of this property.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**The property 'consists of' defining a universal quantification constraint.**

The following diagram shows a universal quantification constraint on the property “has” (owned by the class “Person”). It states, in the context of “Dog Owner”, that all values of the property “has” must be of type “Dog”, and that at least one value of this property must exist.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

A property that is redefined must have the same name as the redefined property. In this case, leaving the redefined property unnamed is equivalent to naming the property the same as the one being redefined (“has” from the class “Person”).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**A universal quantification constraint on the property 'has'.**

#### INFO: Optimized Multiplicity

Optimized Multiplicity

A UML multiplicity on a redefining property is now optimized for unqualified cardinality restrictions in OWL.

85419689

**Related pages**

- Concept Modeling Semantics
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Property restrictions' space='MCM2024xR1']

This kind of constraint is an assertion that only values of the specified type are valid, and that the number of values must be between some minimum and maximum cardinality. In the concept modeling interpretation of UML, introducing a new property or redefining an existing property creates a universal quantification constraint in the context of the owning class. This interpretation is based on {redefines} in UML, which allows adding more specific constraints to an existing property without defining a new property.</p><p>The diagram below shows the introduction of a new property, “consists of”, defining a universal quantification constraint on the property. ## PAGE 00255: Updating symbol styles in existing projects

- page_id: `170478688`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478688/Updating+symbol+styles+in+existing+projects
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:55.664+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Existing Project Migration
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Enabling/Disabling the options

1277660066

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Enabling/Disabling the options

1277660069

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Enabling/Disabling the options

1277660067

GreenAvailable From 19.0

#### INFO: When does this apply?

When does this apply?

- When you open an existing project with outdated symbol styles, the dialog described below will show.

#### INFO: Which versions work?

Which versions work?

- Updating symbol styles in older projects works with version 18.0 SP3 and later , but it is not compatible in version 18.0 SP2 or earlier.

#### NOTE: Versioning

Versioning

- Versioning is now available for updating a project's symbol styles programmatically.
- Updating symbol styles will overwrite the existing styles. If you have manually made changes or added new features to the existing styles, those values will not be retained.

Concept Modeling Diagrams are intended to be as non-technical as possible for subject matter experts. However, symbol styles may sometimes expose technical details that are not appropriate for that kind of audience. The modeling tool, therefore, offers to tweak the symbol styles called **Default** and **Defined Elsewhere**.

You can update the symbol styles, defer updating the styles, or make Concept Modeler ask you or stop asking you about updating them by setting the **Ask to update outdated symbol styles** options to **true** or **false**. Expand the instructions below to see how to update the styling.

The **Ask to update outdated symbol styles** options, which open the **Update 'Default' symbol style** dialog or **Update 'Defined Elsewhere' symbol style** dialog asking you to update symbol styles when loading a Concept Modeling project with an older set of symbol styles, are set to **true** by default. If you set them to **false**, those dialogs will not open.

#### EXPAND: Enabling/Disabling the options

Enabling/Disabling the options

To enable/disable the **Ask to update outdated symbol styles** options

1. On the main menu, click Options > Project.
2. In the Project Options dialog, on the left pane, click General then Styling.
3. Select the Ask to update outdated 'Default' symbol styles option and the Ask to update outdated 'Defined Elsewhere' symbol styles option to set them to true*.* [ATTACHMENT filename='Styling for Updating Symbol Styles.png']
4. Click OK.

In the **Update 'Default' symbol style** dialog and **Update 'Defined Elsewhere' symbol style** dialog, if you select **Yes**, the modeling tool will update the symbol styles. If you select **No**, you will defer updating the styles, and the dialogs will continue to appear next time when loading a Concept Modeling project with outdated symbol styles.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The message dialog when loading a Concept Modeling project with outdated symbol styles.

1277738274

**Related pages**

- Usage

If you have manually made changes or added new features to the existing styles, those values will not be retained.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Concept Modeling Diagrams are intended to be as non-technical as possible for subject matter experts. However, symbol styles may sometimes expose technical details that are not appropriate for that kind of audience. The modeling tool, therefore, offers to tweak the symbol styles called <strong>Default</strong> and <strong>Defined Elsewhere</strong>. </p><p>You can update the symbol styles, defer updating the styles, or make Concept Modeler ask you or stop asking you about updating them by setting the <strong>Ask to update outdated symbol styles</strong> options to <strong>true</strong> or <strong>false</strong>. Expand the instructions below to see how to update the styling.</p><p>The <strong>Ask to update outdated symbol styles</strong> options, which open the <strong>Update 'Default' symbol style</strong> dialog or <strong>Update 'Defined Elsewhere' symbol style</strong> dialog asking you to update symbol styles when loading a Concept Modeling project with an older set of symbol styles, are set to <strong>true</strong> by default. If you set them to <strong>false</strong>, those dialogs will not open.</p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="expand" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="12a1a717-6f1f-4378-a8a2-31be2c402620"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Enabling/Disabling the options</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>To enable/disable the <strong>Ask to update outdated symbol styles</strong> options</p><hr /><ol><li>On the main menu, click <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Project.</strong></li><li>In the <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog, on the left pane, click <strong>General</strong> then <strong>Styling.</strong></li><li>Select the <strong>Ask to update outdated 'Default' symbol styles</strong> option and the <strong>Ask to update outdated 'Defined Elsewhere' symbol styles</strong> option to set them to <strong>true<em>.</em></strong><br /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="700"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Styling for Updating Symbol Styles.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Updating symbol styles in existing projects" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Click <strong>OK.</strong></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>In the <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Update 'Default' symbol style</strong> dialog and <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Update 'Defined Elsewhere' symbol style</strong> dialog, if you select <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Yes</strong>, the modeling tool will update the symbol styles. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478764 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00256: Updating the XML catalog file

- page_id: `170478764`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478764/Updating+the+XML+catalog+file
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:40:13.379+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Importing an OWL ontology to a concept model
- labels: ['xml-catalog']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

238783642

238783644

238783643

The Concept Modeler can import a local ontology model into the concept model. Occasionally, an ontology file may contain references to external ontologies (i.e. ontologies not stored locally and not available to import directly into the concept model). An XML catalog (OASIS Standard V1.1) may be used to locate these external ontologies as a locally cached equivalent.

An XML catalog describes the mapping between external entity references and locally cached equivalents for an XML external resource. In this case, the external entity references are URIs to external ontologies, and the locally cached equivalent is a pointer to a local copy of the root folder containing the external ontologies.

For instance, the following entry from an XML catalog for the Red branch FIBO ontologies tells the Concept Modeler to look for the ontology named “[http://spec.edmcouncil.org/fibo/red/be/](http://spec.edmcouncil.org/fibo/red/be/)” in the folder “be/be.rdf.”

| <uri id="Automatically generated entry, Timestamp=1425183124824" name="http://spec.edmcouncil.org/fibo/red/be/" uri="be/be.rdf"/> |
| --- |

The local copy of this ontology is located relative to the root folder of the locally cached equivalent. This root folder must be set in the XML catalog file by modifying the “id” attribute of the element “group” in the XML catalog file:

| <group id="Folder Repository, directory=file:///C:/Users/Jplatt/Desktop/FIBO-red/, recursive=true, Auto-Update=true, version=2" prefer="public" xml:base="file:///C:/Users/Jplatt/Desktop/FIBO-red/"> |
| --- |

The identical, bolded file URIs above point to the folder “FIBO-red” on the Windows desktop of user “Jplatt.” These file URIs must be set to the location of the local folder containing the locally cached equivalent of the external ontologies. Windows users should note the use of forward slashes, as well as the triple forward slash before the Windows file location.

238783641

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Usage' space='MCM2024xR1']

Occasionally, an ontology file may contain references to external ontologies (i.e. ontologies not stored locally and not available to import directly into the concept model). An XML catalog (OASIS Standard V1.1) may be used to locate these external ontologies as a locally cached equivalent.</span></p><p>An XML catalog describes the mapping between external entity references and locally cached equivalents for an XML external resource. In this case, the external entity references are URIs to external ontologies, and the locally cached equivalent is a pointer to a local copy of the root folder containing the external ontologies.</p><p>For instance, the following entry from an XML catalog for the Red branch FIBO ontologies tells the Concept Modeler to look for the ontology named “<a href="http://spec.edmcouncil.org/fibo/red/be/" style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">http://spec.edmcouncil.org/fibo/red/be/</a>” in the folder “be/be.rdf.”</p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th><pre>&lt;uri id=&quot;Automatically generated entry, Timestamp=1425183124824&quot; name=&quot;<a href="http://spec.edmcouncil.org/fibo/red/be/">http://spec.edmcouncil.org/fibo/red/be/</a>&quot; uri=&quot;be/be.rdf&quot;/&gt;</pre></th></tr></tbody></table><p>The local copy of this ontology is located relative to the root folder of the locally cached equivalent. This root folder must be set in the XML catalog file by modifying the “id” attribute of the element “group” in the XML catalog file:</p><table class="fixed-table wrapped"><colgroup><col style="width: 1050.0px;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th><pre>&lt;group id=&quot;Folder Repository, directory=file:///C:/Users/Jplatt/Desktop/FIBO-red/, recursive=true, Auto-Update=true, version=2&quot; prefer=&quot;public&quot; xml:base=&quot;file:///C:/Users/Jplatt/Desktop/FIBO-red/&quot;&gt;</pre></th></tr></tbody></table><p class="auto-cursor-target">The identical, bolded file URIs above point to the folder “FIBO-red” on the Windows desktop of user “Jplatt.” These file URIs must be set to the location of the local folder containing the locally cached equivalent of the external ontologies. Windows users should note the use of forward slashes, as well as the triple forward slash before the Windows file location.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a064682e-ddc0-4603-9f05-34a396ede189"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">238783641</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Usage" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478818 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00258: Use Path Variables to Export a Concept Model to an OWL Ontology

- page_id: `170478818`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478818/Use+Path+Variables+to+Export+a+Concept+Model+to+an+OWL+Ontology
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:56.802+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Exporting your concept model to an OWL ontology
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

570588469

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

570588472

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

570588471

When you export a **«Concept Model»** stereotyped package, you must have the '**Always prompt for a file destination when exporting OWL**' set to *true.* Please refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Setting destination for the OWL export folder' space='MCM2024xR1'] to see how to enable this option. This new support allows you to collaborate your projects with other team members without having to keep in mind the exact destination of the file.

To use a path variable to export the concept model to OWL

1. In the containment tree, find the Concept Model package you want to export, and right-click on it.
2. Select Concept Modeling > Export Model to OWL . You will be prompted to select a folder from your directory.
3. Select the desired location and click **OK**. The **Use Path Variables** dialog opens. NoteThe **Use Path Variables** dialog appears only when the selected destination folder has the same path destination that is defined for the path variable.
4. Do one of the following:
  - Click Use Selected to show the form highlighted in purple. [ATTACHMENT filename='Use Path Variables Dialog.png']
  - Click Use Original to show the path shown in quotes directly above the highlighted portion .

The file is generated, exported, and saved inside the directory path described.

570604531

**Related pages**

- Usage
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exporting your concept model to an OWL ontology' space='MCM2024xR1']

This new support allows you to collaborate your projects with other team members without having to keep in mind the exact destination of the file.</p><p><br /></p><p>To use a path variable to export the concept model to OWL</p><hr /><ol><li>In the containment tree, find the <strong>Concept Model</strong> package you want to export, and right-click on it.</li><li>Select <strong>Concept Modeling</strong> &gt; <strong>Export Model to OWL</strong>. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478776 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00259: Using a path variable to share OWL import catalog files

- page_id: `170478776`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478776/Using+a+path+variable+to+share+OWL+import+catalog+files
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:56.082+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Importing an OWL ontology to a concept model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

240730660

240730663

240730661

To use a path variable to share OWL import catalog files

1. Click Options > Project . [ATTACHMENT filename='image2016-8-30 11:43:28.png']
2. In the left column, select General > Concept Modeling.
3. Click in the field next to OWL Import Catalog.
4. Click the ... button.
5. Select the XML catalog file. [ATTACHMENT filename='image2016-8-30 11:46:14.png']
6. Click **Open**.
7. Select the path to the OWL import catalog that includes the defined path variable. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. Click **Use Selected**.

240756571

- Usage
- Importing an OWL ontology to a concept model
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a path variable to share OWL import catalog files' space='MCM2024xR1']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478727 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00260: Using Concept Modeling Capabilities with non-MCM projects

- page_id: `170478727`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478727/Using+Concept+Modeling+Capabilities+with+non-MCM+projects
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-31T14:09:42.733+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Working with non-MCM projects
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1283810250

1283810261

1283810251

MagicDraw allows you to enable all the functionalities of Magic Concept Modeler (MCM) plugin when you are working in a non-MCM project by adding the **Concept Modeling** **Profile** to your non-MCM project using the **Project Usages** dialog.

When working with a non-MCM project, e.g. *a SysML project,*you cannot select the **Concept Modeling** option.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**The Concept Modeling option is grayed out.**

To add the **Concept Modeling** profile using **Project Usages**

1. If you have already installed the MCM plugin, click File > Use Project > Use Local Project . [ATTACHMENT filename='UseLocalProject.png']
2. Select the options as shown below. [ATTACHMENT filename='UseLocalProjectDialog.png']
3. Click Next.
4. Select the Read Only option. Click Finish.
5. The Concept Modeling Profile has been added to your non-MCM project. [ATTACHMENT filename='CCMProfile.PNG']
6. You can now choose the Concept Modeling option and create concept models. [ATTACHMENT filename='CCMPopsUP.png']

You can now choose the **Concept Modeling** option, create a Concept Model, and benefit from all the MCM functionalities.

1283810249

**Related Pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Usage' space='MCM2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

adding the <strong>Concept Modeling</strong> <strong>Profile</strong> to your non-MCM project using the <strong>Project Usages</strong> dialog.</p><p>When working with a non-MCM project, e.g. <em>a SysML project, </em>you cannot select the <strong>Concept Modeling</strong> option.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="500"><ri:attachment ri:filename="CMOptionNotThere.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Using Concept Modeling Capabilities with non-MCM projects" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="text-align: center;"><strong>The Concept Modeling option is grayed out.</strong></p><p>To add the <strong>Concept Modeling</strong> profile using <strong>Project Usages</strong></p><hr /><ol><li>If you have already installed the MCM plugin, click <strong>File</strong> &gt; <strong>Use Project</strong> &gt; <strong>Use Local Project</strong>.<br /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="500"><ri:attachment Modeling Capabilities with non-MCM projects" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Click <strong>Next.</strong></li><li>Select the <strong>Read Only</strong> option. Click <strong>Finish.</strong></li><li>The <strong>Concept Modeling Profile</strong> has been added to your non-MCM project.<br /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="400"><ri:attachment ri:filename="CCMProfile.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Using Concept Modeling Capabilities with non-MCM projects" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>You can now choose the <strong>Concept Modeling</strong> option and create concept models.<br /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="500"><ri:attachment ri:filename="CCMPopsUP.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Using Concept Modeling Capabilities with non-MCM projects" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p>You can now choose the <strong>Concept Modeling</strong> option, create a Concept Model, and benefit from all the MCM functionalities.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="626e266f-d67e-4c5e-8b60-ada840a589b5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1283810249</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Usage" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170478791 space=MCM2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00261: Using the log file to track changes during import

- page_id: `170478791`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478791/Using+the+log+file+to+track+changes+during+import
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-24T11:40:13.907+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Importing an OWL ontology to a concept model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The modeling tool has the capability of tracking your changes during importing your OWL ontology files. These changes are stored in the log file. The notification window shows the location of the log file in your local directory.

To see the summary of changes from your imports

1. Open a new, or existing, MCM project.
2. Import an OWL ontology.
3. The notification window will show you the location of the log file. [ATTACHMENT filename='c1.PNG']
4. The file says something like the following: textNew Concept Model: Asymmetric New Concept Model: rdf-schema New Concept: BiologicalMother New Concept: Thing New Concept: Thing New Concept: Child New Datatype: string New Property: has child New Property: label ________________________________________________


]]>
5. The structure is the same for all log files: a section for Summary of Changes, and section for Details of Changes.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The modeling tool has the capability of tracking your changes during importing your OWL ontology files. These changes are stored in the log file. The notification window shows the location of the log file in your local directory.</p><p>To see the summary of changes from your imports</p><hr /><ol><li>Open a new, or existing, MCM project.</li><li>Import an OWL ontology.</li><li>The notification window will show you the location of the log file.<br /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="23"><ri:attachment ri:filename="c1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Using the log file to track changes during import" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">The file says something like the following:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="144fc4a7-2c33-45bc-b00f-5630f3648243"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[________________________________________________

          Summary of Changes
________________________________________________

New Concept Model : 3
New Concept : 4
New Datatype : 1
New Property : 2
________________________________________________

          Details of Changes
________________________________________________

New Concept Model: XMLSchema <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema>
New Concept Model: Asymmetric <http://example.com/ontology/Asymmetric>
New Concept Model: rdf-schema <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema>
New Concept: BiologicalMother <http://example.com/ontology/Asymmetric#BiologicalMother>
New Concept: Thing <http://example.com/ontology/Asymmetric#Thing>
New Concept: Thing <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#Thing>
New Concept: Child <http://example.com/ontology/Asymmetric#Child>
New Datatype: string <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#string>
New Property: has child <http://example.com/ontology/Asymmetric#hasChild>
New Property: label <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#label>
________________________________________________


]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li>The structure is the same for all log files: a section for <strong>Summary of Changes,</strong> and section for <strong>Details of Changes.</strong></li></ol><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=170479179 space=MCM2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00262: Variables of the natural language glossary

- page_id: `170479179`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170479179/Variables+of+the+natural+language+glossary
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:09:00.239+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Working with the Natural Language Glossary > Customizing your Natural Language Glossary
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: If you are unfamiliar with regular expressions

1235417006

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: If you are unfamiliar with regular expressions

1235417017

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: If you are unfamiliar with regular expressions

1235417007

When [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Generating a natural language glossary' space='MCM2024xR1'] the Concept Model's **Natural Language Glossary (NLG)**, you are able to customize the glossary's header by changing the title, image, and element expressions through the NLG's **Report Wizard.** By default, the NLG header looks like so:

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The default NLG header.

To change the Title in the NLG header

1. On the main menu, click Tools > Report Wizard.
2. The Report Wizard dialog opens. Click on Concept Modeler and then Natural Language Glossary.
3. Click Next.
4. Select the Default report data.
5. Click **Variable.** [IMAGE alt='' src='']
6. The **Report Variable** dialog opens. To start editing the Title, double-click on the **Title** field. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

Remaining in the same **Report Variable** dialog, refer to the next set of instructions to see how to change the **TitlePreamble**. By default, the **TitlePreamble** is a hyperlinked image of the MCM plugin linked to the MCM website. If you would like to read more about the **Report Variable** dialog, please refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Report Data Management Pane' space='MD190'].

To change the TitlePreamble in the NLG header

1. Click on the three dots at the end of the **TitlePreamble** field. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. The **TitlePreamble** dialog opens. This dialog is also called a HTML editor dialog. To see how to use the HTML editor dialog, please refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='HTML editor' space='MD190']. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Click on the [ATTACHMENT filename='changetoAHE.PNG'] button. This opens the Advanced HTML editor for the TitlePreamble of your NLG header.
4. Please refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Advanced HTML Editor dialog' space='MD2024xR1'] to learn about how to work with the dialog, then return to these instructions.
5. You have now changed the the image and link of your NLG header.
6. You should be back in the Report Variable dialog.

You have now learned how to change the Title and TitlePreamble of your NLG report. The rest of the page shows you how to change the **Publisher,** the **ClassSuppressionRegex,** and the **PropertySuppressionRegex** fields.

The default Publisher is No Magic.

To change the Publisher of the NLG report

1. Double-click the Publisher field to edit the Publisher.
2. Start typing to change the field.

The **ClassSuppressionRegex** and **PropertySuppressionRegex** require knowledge of regular expressions, regex, or rational expressions. These regular expressions are used to suppress any Class or Property elements from the NLG report.

#### INFO: If you are unfamiliar with regular expressions

If you are unfamiliar with regular expressions

Please refer to [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Regular_expression](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Regular_expression).

To change the ClassSuppressionRegex and PropertySuppressionRegex

1. This applies to both fields, so double-click on the field you would like to change.
2. Start typing the regular expression in the field.

You will see your changes after clicking **OK.**

You are now back in the Report Wizard dialog, and can continue generating your NLG report.

1235417005

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Usage' space='MCM2024xR1']

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customizing your Natural Language Glossary' space='MCM2024xR1']

This dialog is also called a HTML editor dialog. This opens the Advanced HTML editor for the TitlePreamble of your NLG header. </li><li>Please refer to <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Advanced HTML Editor dialog" /></ac:link> to learn about how to work with the dialog, then return to these instructions.</li><li>You have now changed the the image and link of your NLG header.</li><li>You should be back in the <strong>Report Variable</strong> dialog.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>You have now learned how to change the Title and TitlePreamble of your NLG report. The rest of the page shows you how to change the <strong>Publisher,</strong> the <strong>ClassSuppressionRegex,</strong> and the <strong>PropertySuppressionRegex</strong> fields. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="80cdbead-c398-4b42-a6fa-2bea93f633b3"><ac:rich-text-body>The default Publisher is No Magic.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To change the Publisher of the NLG report</p><hr /><ol><li>Double-click the <strong>Publisher</strong> field to edit the Publisher.</li><li>Start typing to change the field. </li></ol><p><br /></p><p>The <strong>ClassSuppressionRegex</strong> and <strong>PropertySuppressionRegex</strong> require knowledge of regular expressions, regex, or rational expressions. These regular expressions are used to suppress any Class or Property elements from the NLG report.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6bb5e056-7272-49a5-b54a-be77fa772550"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">If you are unfamiliar with regular expressions</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Please refer to <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Regular_expression">https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Regular_expression</a>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To change the ClassSuppressionRegex and PropertySuppressionRegex </p><hr /><ol><li>This applies to both fields, so double-click on the field you would like to change. </li><li>Start typing the regular expression in the field.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>You will see your changes after clicking <strong>OK.</strong> </p><p>You are now back in the Report Wizard dialog, and can continue generating your NLG report. </p><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1788be0f-f8ce-406e-960d-da60c02ce4d9"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1235417005</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Usage" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Working with the Natural Language Glossary" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Working with the natural language glossary]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCM2024xR1" ri:content-title="Customizing your Natural Language Glossary" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Customizing your natural language glossary]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 00263: View the MCM watermark in an exported OWL

- page_id: `170478838`
- space_key: `MCM2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCM2024xR1/pages/170478838/View+the+MCM+watermark+in+an+exported+OWL
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T15:08:57.112+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Concept Modeler Documentation > Usage > Exporting your concept model to an OWL ontology
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1389873772

1389873783

1389873782

To view the MCM watermark in your exported OWL ontology

1. Open your exported OWL ontology file. (We recommend opening the file with Notepad++)
2. Scroll to the bottom of the file.
3. You will find a watermark saying something such as:

```text
]]>
```

The information in the parenthesis will be different according to your application version and build.

1389873771

- Usage
